theorem:
[Noferini--Williams \cite{MR4418964}]\label{conj}
For $n\ge1$ and $0\le m<n$, $H(n,m)^{\mathrm{ab}}\cong\mathbb{Z}^2$
if and only if 
$n\equiv0\pmod6$ and 
$m\equiv2\pmod n.$
sketch:
The introduction outlines a proof of
\begin{conjecture}[Noferini--Williams \cite{MR4418964}]\label{conj}
For $n\ge1$ and $0\le m<n$, $H(n,m)^{\mathrm{ab}}\cong\mathbb{Z}^2$
if and only if 
$n\equiv0\pmod6$ and 
$m\equiv2\pmod n.$
\end{conjecture}
as follows. The forward implication is obtained by exhibiting “cyclotomic roots of order six for the trinomial $1+t^m-t$ under the stated congruences.” For the converse, the paper uses an arithmetic method: the “$\mathbb{Z}$-rank~2, torsion-free condition reduces to $\operatorname{Res}(F,G)=1$,” with
\[
F=\frac{1+t^m-t}{\Phi_6(t)},\quad
G=\frac{t^n-1}{\Phi_6(t)}.
\]
The rest of the argument is “a minimality argument.” First, we use the technical results underpinning the proof of the main theorem:
\label{tech}
In this section, we present the technical results that underpin the proof of Conjecture~\ref{conj}. All notation is as in Section~\ref{std}.

\medskip
Our first result extends \cite[Corol
In this part the conjecture is proved for the families $H(n, 2 \pm n/3)$ and $H(n, 2 + n/2)$, and “strong necessary conditions on $n$ and $m$ that any minimal counterexample must satisfy” are derived. Then (Section~\ref{sec:mainproof}) it is shown that any minimal counterexample “must in fact belong to one of these families,” so “no minimal counterexample exists,” yielding the conjecture.
question: For integers \(n\ge 1\) and \(0\le m<n\), write \(H(n,m)^{\mathrm{ab}}\) for the abelianization of the group \(H(n,m)\). Which of the following statements is equivalent to \(H(n,m)^{\mathrm{ab}}\cong \mathbb{Z}^2\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: \(n\equiv 0\pmod 6\) and \(m\equiv 2\pmod n\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(n\equiv 0\pmod 6\) and \(m\equiv 2\pm \frac n3\pmod n\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: \(n\equiv 0\pmod 6\) and \(m=2\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(n\equiv 0\pmod 6\) and \(m\equiv 2+\frac n2\pmod n\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: \(n\equiv 0\pmod 6\) and \(m\equiv 2\pmod{n/3}\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: \(n\equiv 0\pmod 6\) and \(m\equiv 2\pmod n\).

sketch: To prove the main theorem, the approach is to reduce the integro-differential equation to “a pair of integral equations for the derivative of its solution,” coupled at a point $u_0\in(0,\infty)$. On $[u_0,\infty)$, one treats “a somewhat exotic terminal value problem,” seeking a solution with asymptotic behavior $u^{-\gamma}$ as $u\to\infty$; for sufficiently large $u_0$ this solution is obtained “using the Banach fixed point theorem,” and uniqueness is established. On $(0,u_0]$, the derivative satisfies “a Volterra integral equation of the second kind” with a terminal condition at $u_0$ coming from the first part. “Gluing the solutions together” gives the derivative (with a multiplicative constant $C$ to determine), then integrating yields $\Phi$ with an additive constant $C_0$, which “must be taken as zero” to enforce $\Phi(0)=0$. The constant $C$ is chosen so that “the limit at infinity equals one,” and $C$ is “calculable” via an integral involving the constructed solutions. Finally, “using It\^o's formula,” one concludes that $\Phi$ coincides with the survival probability. The method “avoid[s] reducing the IDE to a higher-order ODE” and instead uses “standard results concerning the uniqueness of solutions to IDEs.”
question:
Consider the annuity risk model in which the capital reserve process \(X^u=(X_t^u)\) with initial capital \(u\ge 0\) satisfies
\[
X_t^u=u+\int_0^t \kappa X_s^u(a\,ds+\sigma\,dW_s)+\int_0^t r(1-\kappa)X_s^u\,ds-ct+\sum_{i=1}^{N_t}\xi_i,
\]
where \(W\) is a standard Wiener process, \(N\) is a Poisson process with intensity \(\lambda>0\), independent of \(W\), and \((\xi_i)_{i\ge1}\) are i.i.d. positive random variables, independent of \(N\), with distribution function \(F\) on \([0,\infty)\). Let
\[
\tau^u:=\inf\{t\ge 0:X_t^u\le 0\},\qquad \Phi(u):={\bf P}(\tau^u=\infty),\qquad \Psi(u):=1-\Phi(u).
\]
Assume \(F(0)=0\), \({\bf E}[\xi]<\infty\), \(\sigma>0\), and
\[
\gamma:=\frac{(a-r)\kappa+r}{\kappa^2\sigma^2}>1.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic behavior of the ruin probability \(\Psi(u)\) as \(u\to\infty\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)\sim C\,u^{-\gamma+1}\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)\sim C\,u^{-\gamma}\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)=O\!\left(u^{-\gamma+1}\right)\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exists \(\varepsilon>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)=O\!\left(u^{-\gamma+1-\varepsilon}\right)\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Phi(u)=1-C\,u^{-\gamma}+o\!\left(u^{-\gamma}\right)
\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)\sim C\,u^{-\gamma+1}\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

theorem:
\label{Thm:main}
There exists a family of countable $W^\ast$-McDuff groups $\{ G_i\}_{i\in I}$ such that $|I|=2^{\aleph_0}$, $G_i\not\cong G_j$ whenever $i\ne j$, and every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid.,
sketch: The groups $G_i$ are described as "infinite direct sums of $W^\ast$-superrigid groups with property $(T)$ constructed in I. Chifan, A. Ioana, A. O. Sasyk, and D. Stefănescu, CIOS21." Beyond this, the text only states that "[t]he proof of Theorem To prove the main theorem, incorporates several new ideas on both the group-theoretic and analytic sides" without outlining any steps or structure.
question: A countable discrete group $G$ is called a $W^*$-McDuff group if its group von Neumann algebra $L(G)$ is a McDuff II$_1$ factor. Here, $G$ is called McDuff superrigid in the strongest sense if whenever $L(G)\cong L(H)$ for some group $H$, one has $H\cong G\times A$ for some ICC amenable group $A$ (ICC means every nontrivial conjugacy class is infinite). What is the strongest existence statement that can be proved about countable $W^*$-McDuff groups?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There exists a family $\{G_i\}_{i\in I}$ of countable $W^*$-McDuff groups with $|I|=2^{\aleph_0}$ such that $G_i\not\cong G_j$ whenever $i\ne j$, and every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists a family $\{G_i\}_{i\in I}$ of countable $W^*$-McDuff groups with $|I|=2^{\aleph_0}$ such that $G_i\not\cong G_j$ whenever $i\ne j$, and every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid in the strongest sense.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a countably infinite family $\{G_i\}_{i\in I}$ of pairwise non-isomorphic countable $W^*$-McDuff groups such that every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a family $\{G_i\}_{i\in I}$ of countable ICC groups with $|I|=2^{\aleph_0}$ such that $G_i\not\cong G_j$ whenever $i\ne j$, and every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every countable $W^*$-McDuff group $G$, there exists a family $\{G_i\}_{i\in I}$ of countable $W^*$-McDuff groups with $|I|=2^{\aleph_0}$ such that $G_i\not\cong G_j$ whenever $i\ne j$, every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid, and $G$ is isomorphic to one of the $G_i$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: There exists a family $\{G_i\}_{i\in I}$ of countable $W^*$-McDuff groups with $|I|=2^{\aleph_0}$ such that $G_i\not\cong G_j$ whenever $i\ne j$, and every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid.

question:
Let \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{R}^2\) be a bounded open set containing \(0\). Suppose \((v_n)\) is a sequence of solutions of
\[
-\Delta v_n=(\varepsilon_n^2+|x|^2)^{\alpha_n}V_n(x)e^{v_n}\qquad\text{in }\Omega,
\]
where \(\alpha_n\to \alpha_\infty\in(-1,1)\), and the coefficients \(V_n\) satisfy
\[
0<a\le V_n\le b<\infty,\qquad V_n\in C^0(\Omega),\qquad V_n\to V\ \text{locally uniformly in }\Omega.
\]
Fix any
\[
C_1>\max\left\{1,\frac{1+\alpha_\infty}{1-\alpha_\infty}\right\}
\]
and any compact set \(K\subset \Omega\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the uniform estimate satisfied by \((v_n)\) under these assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exists a constant \(C_2>0\), depending only on \(a\), \(b\), \(\operatorname{dist}(K,\partial\Omega)\), \(\alpha_\infty\), and the uniform modulus of continuity of \(V\) on \(K\), such that for every \(n\),
\[
\sup_{\Omega} v_n + C_1\inf_{\Omega} v_n\le C_2.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists a constant \(C_2>0\), depending only on \(a\), \(b\), \(\operatorname{dist}(K,\partial\Omega)\), \(\alpha_\infty\), the uniform modulus of continuity of \(V\) on \(K\), and \(C_1\), such that for all sufficiently large \(n\),
\[
\sup_K v_n + C_1\inf_{\Omega} v_n\le C_2.
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exists a constant \(C_2>0\), depending only on \(a\), \(b\), \(\operatorname{dist}(K,\partial\Omega)\), \(\alpha_\infty\), and the uniform modulus of continuity of \(V\) on \(K\), such that for every \(n\),
\[
\sup_K v_n + \frac{1+\alpha_\infty}{1-\alpha_\infty}\inf_{\Omega} v_n\le C_2.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists a constant \(C_2>0\), depending only on \(a\), \(b\), \(\operatorname{dist}(K,\partial\Omega)\), \(\alpha_\infty\), and the uniform modulus of continuity of \(V\) on \(K\), such that for every \(n\),
\[
\sup_K v_n + C_1\inf_{K} v_n\le C_2.
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question:
Let $h,r>0$ be fixed, and let
\[
X:=\{\rho\in AC([-h,h])\colon \rho(x)>0\text{ for all }x\in[-h,h],\ \rho(-h)=\rho(h)=r\}.
\]
For each $c\in\mathbb R$, define
\[
\mathcal F_c(\rho):=\int_{-h}^h\left(\rho(x)+\frac{c}{\rho(x)}\right)\sqrt{1+(\rho'(x))^2}\,dx,
\]
and let $\Pi_0(h,r)$ be a fixed quantity depending on $h$ and $r$. Which statement gives the existence, uniqueness, and case-by-case description of the minimizer of $\mathcal F_c$ in $X$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every $c\in\mathbb R$, the functional $\mathcal F_c$ admits a unique minimizer $\rho_c\in X$. If $c<r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{2r^2-E^2+2c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has exactly one positive solution $\smileacc{E}(c)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{\frac{\smileacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\smileacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\smileacc{E}}+\frac{\smileacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly convex, and $\rho_0<\rho_c<r$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_0$ denotes the minimizer for $c=0$. If $c=r^2$, then $\rho_c(x)=r$ for all $x\in[-h,h]$. If $c>r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{-2r^2+E^2-4c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has a unique positive solution $\frownacc{E}(c)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{-\frac{\frownacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\frownacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\frownacc{E}}+\frac{\frownacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly concave, and $r<\rho_c<\rho_\infty$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_\infty(x)=\sqrt{h^2+r^2-x^2}$. Moreover, $\rho_c\to\rho_\infty$ uniformly on $[-h,h]$ as $c\to+\infty$. Finally, whenever $c_1<c_2$, one has $\rho_{c_1}<\rho_{c_2}$ on $(-h,h)$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every $c\in\mathbb R$, the functional $\mathcal F_c$ admits a unique minimizer $\rho_c\in X$. If $c=r^2$, then $\rho_c(x)=r$ for all $x\in[-h,h]$. If $c<r^2$, then $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly convex, and $\rho_0<\rho_c<r$ on $(-h,h)$. If $c>r^2$, then $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly concave, and $r<\rho_c<\rho_\infty$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_\infty(x)=\sqrt{h^2+r^2-x^2}$. Moreover, $\rho_c\to\rho_\infty$ uniformly on $[-h,h]$ as $c\to+\infty$. Finally, whenever $c_1<c_2$, one has $\rho_{c_1}<\rho_{c_2}$ on $(-h,h)$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every $c\in\mathbb R$, the functional $\mathcal F_c$ admits a unique minimizer $\rho_c\in X$. If $c<r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{2r^2-E^2+2c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has exactly one solution $\smileacc{E}(c)>\Pi_0(h,r)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{\frac{\smileacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\smileacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\smileacc{E}}+\frac{\smileacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly convex, and $\rho_0<\rho_c<r$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_0$ denotes the minimizer for $c=0$. If $c=r^2$, then $\rho_c(x)=r$ for all $x\in[-h,h]$. If $c>r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{-2r^2+E^2-4c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has a unique positive solution $\frownacc{E}(c)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{-\frac{\frownacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\frownacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\frownacc{E}}+\frac{\frownacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly concave, and $r<\rho_c<\rho_\infty$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_\infty(x)=\sqrt{h^2+r^2-x^2}$. Moreover, $\rho_c\to\rho_\infty$ pointwise on $[-h,h]$ as $c\to+\infty$, but the convergence is not uniform on $[-h,h]$. Finally, whenever $c_1<c_2$, one has $\rho_{c_1}<\rho_{c_2}$ on $(-h,h)$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every $c\in\mathbb R$, the functional $\mathcal F_c$ admits a unique minimizer $\rho_c\in X$. If $c<r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{2r^2-E^2+2c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has exactly one solution $\smileacc{E}(c)>\Pi_0(h,r)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{\frac{\smileacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\smileacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\smileacc{E}}+\frac{\smileacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly convex, and $\rho_0<\rho_c<r$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_0$ denotes the minimizer for $c=0$. If $c=r^2$, then $\rho_c(x)=r$ for all $x\in[-h,h]$. If $c>r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{-2r^2+E^2-4c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has a unique positive solution $\frownacc{E}(c)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{-\frac{\frownacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\frownacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\frownacc{E}}+\frac{\frownacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly concave, and $r<\rho_c<\rho_\infty$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_\infty(x)=\sqrt{h^2+r^2-x^2}$. Moreover, $\rho_c\to\rho_\infty$ uniformly on $[-h,h]$ as $c\to+\infty$. Finally, whenever $c_1<c_2$, one has $\rho_{c_1}>\rho_{c_2}$ on $(-h,h)$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{conj:PTB}
Let $\pi$ be a generic representation of $G$, and $\phi=\phi_\pi$.
\begin{itemize}
\item[(1)] If $\pi$ is $E$-distinguished, then $\phi$ takes values in $\Sp_n(\mathbb{C})$.
\item[(2)] Suppose $\phi$ takes values in $\Sp_n(\mathbb{C})$ and we write it in the following form:
    \[
    \phi = \bigoplus_{i\in I_\phi} \phi_i \oplus \bigoplus_{j\in J_\phi}(\phi_j\oplus\phi_j^\vee),
    \]
where $I_\phi,  J_\phi$ are finite sets and $\phi_k\,\colon WD_F\to\GL_{n_k}(\mathbb{C})$ is a discrete $L$-parameter such that
\begin{itemize}
\item for each $i\in I_\phi$,  $n_i$ is even and $\phi_i$ takes values in $\Sp_{n_i}(\mathbb{C})$,
\item for any $i,  i'\in I_\phi$,  we have $\phi_i\not\simeq\phi_{i'}$ if $i\neq i'$. 
\end{itemize}
Then $\pi$ is $E$-distinguished if and only if we have 
    \[
    \e_E(\phi_i) = (-1)^{n_i/d},   \qquad  i\in I_\phi.
    \]
\end{itemize}
question:
Let \(F\) be a finite extension of \(\mathbb{Q}_p\), let \(D\) be a central division \(F\)-algebra of dimension \(d^2\), let \(m\ge 1\), and set \(G=\mathrm{GL}_m(D)\). Let \(n=md\), assume \(n\) is even, let \(E/F\) be a quadratic extension, and let \(H\) be the centralizer of \(E^\times\) in \(G\). A smooth representation \(\pi\) of \(G\) is called \(E\)-distinguished if \(\operatorname{Hom}_H(\pi,\mathbb{C})\neq 0\). Let \(\pi\) be a generic representation of \(G\), and let \(\phi=\phi_\pi\) be its \(L\)-parameter, viewed as a representation of the Weil-Deligne group \(WD_F\). Assume that \(\phi\) takes values in \(\mathrm{Sp}_n(\mathbb{C})\) and can be written as
\[
\phi = \bigoplus_{i\in I_\phi} \phi_i \oplus \bigoplus_{j\in J_\phi}(\phi_j\oplus\phi_j^\vee),
\]
where \(I_\phi\) and \(J_\phi\) are finite sets, each \(\phi_k\colon WD_F\to\mathrm{GL}_{n_k}(\mathbb{C})\) is a discrete \(L\)-parameter, for every \(i\in I_\phi\) the integer \(n_i\) is even and \(\phi_i\) takes values in \(\mathrm{Sp}_{n_i}(\mathbb{C})\), and \(\phi_i\not\simeq \phi_{i'}\) whenever \(i\neq i'\) in \(I_\phi\). Here \(\phi_j^\vee\) denotes the contragredient dual of \(\phi_j\). For such symplectic \(\phi_i\), write
\[
\varepsilon_E(\phi_i):=\eta_{E/F}(-1)^{n_i/2}\,\epsilon\bigl(1/2,\phi_i|_{WD_E},\psi_E\bigr),
\]
where \(\eta_{E/F}\) is the quadratic character of \(F^\times\) attached to \(E/F\), \(\psi_E=\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{E/F}\) for a nontrivial character \(\psi:F\to\mathbb{C}^\times\), and \(\epsilon\) is the Deligne-Langlands epsilon factor. Under these assumptions, which statement about the \(E\)-distinguished property of \(\pi\) is valid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished if and only if \(\varepsilon_E(\phi_i)=(-1)^m\) for every \(i\in I_\phi\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: If \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished, then \(\varepsilon_E(\phi_i)=(-1)^{n_i/d}\) for every \(i\in I_\phi\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished if and only if \(\varepsilon_E(\phi_i)=(-1)^{n_i/d}\) for every \(i\in I_\phi\) such that \(n_i/d\) is odd; no further condition is required for the remaining indices in \(I_\phi\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished if and only if \(\eta_{E/F}(-1)^{n/2}\,\epsilon\bigl(1/2,\phi|_{WD_E},\psi_E\bigr)=(-1)^m\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question: Let \(\mathbb A\in\{\mathbb H,\mathbb O\}\), viewed as a real Euclidean space with its standard norm \(\|\cdot\|\), and let \(P_0(x)\in\mathbb R[x]\) be a central polynomial over \(\mathbb A\). Suppose its zero set \(V_0=Z(P_0)\) contains a non-real manifold of dimension \(d_{\rm M}>0\). Let \(P_\epsilon(x)\in\mathbb A[x]\) be a generically non-central analytic deformation of \(P_0\), meaning that the coefficients depend analytically on \(\epsilon\in\mathbb R\), \(P_\epsilon(x)=P_0(x)\) when \(\epsilon=0\), and for \(\epsilon\neq 0\) near \(0\) the automorphism stabilizer \(G_{P_\epsilon}=\{g\in\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb A): g(a_k)=a_k\text{ for all coefficients }a_k\}\) is trivial. Let \(V_\epsilon=Z(P_\epsilon)\). Define \(\mathcal{V}_\epsilon(x)=\|P_\epsilon(x)\|^2\) and consider the gradient flow \(\dot{x}=-\nabla \mathcal{V}_\epsilon(x)\). As \(\epsilon\to 0\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the collapse under this flow?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and the collapse timescale exhibits critical slowing down with \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-2}\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and for a generic non-central analytic deformation the characteristic collapse time scales as \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-1}\), reflecting a restoring rate that is linear in \(\epsilon\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and the characteristic collapse time diverges as \(\epsilon\to 0\), without asserting the exact power law.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and analyticity of the deformation already forces the Lojasiewicz exponent to be \(1/2\), so \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-2}\) for every analytic deformation, including non-generic ones.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The gradient flow drives trajectories into the attractor set \(V_\epsilon\) with critical slowing down \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-2}\), but in general this gives only a retract of a neighborhood of \(V_0\), not a deformation retract of \(V_0\) onto \(V_\epsilon\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and the collapse timescale exhibits critical slowing down with \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-2}\).

theorem:
[Local density of $\mathcal{S}$ in short intervals]\label{thm:main-elements}
Fix $\varepsilon>0$. Let $\theta$ satisfy $2/15+\varepsilon<\theta<1-\delta_0$ for some absolute constant $\delta_0>0$, and set $h=X^{\theta}$.
Then for all but $O_{\varepsilon}\left(X\exp\left(-c_{\varepsilon}(\log X)^{1/4}\right)\right)$ integers $x\in[X,2X]$, one has
\[
\#\left(\mathcal{S}\cap(x,x+h]\right)\ \asymp\ h.
\]
The implied constants depend at most on $\lambda$, $\varepsilon$ and $\theta$.
sketch:
“To access [Theorem~\ref{thm:main-elements}] through Cauchy--Schwarz,” the paper first proves a first-moment statement for the representation function $f$, namely Theorem~\ref{thm:main-rep} that $R(x)\asymp h$ for almost all $x\in[X,2X]$. For this, the “pointwise upper bound $R(x)\ll h$ follows from Brun--Titchmarsh together with $\#\cA_{\lambda}(X)\asymp\log X$,” while the “almost-all lower bound $R(x)\gg h$ is obtained by restricting to $a\leqslant h/2$ and applying the Guth--Maynard ... asymptotic for primes in almost all short intervals (with a stretched-exponential exceptional set).”

“To pass from representations to elements” (i.e. to deduce Theorem~\ref{thm:main-elements}), they “combine Cauchy--Schwarz, which relates $R(x)$, $S(x)$ and the local second moment $Q(x)$, with a uniform bound $Q(x)\ll h$.” This uniform second-moment bound “is proved using Selberg's upper-bound sieve for prime pairs together with Chen--Ding--Xu--Zhai's estimate controlling the contribution of small prime divisors in differences $a_1-a_2$ with $a_1,a_2\in\cA_{\lambda}(X)$.”
question:
Fix \(\varepsilon>0\). Let \(\theta\) satisfy \(2/15+\varepsilon<\theta<1-\delta_0\) for some absolute constant \(\delta_0>0\), and set \(h=X^{\theta}\). Let \(\mathbb P\) denote the set of prime numbers, and define
\[
\mathcal A_{\lambda}:=\left\{2^{\lfloor k_1^{r_1}\rfloor}+\cdots+2^{\lfloor k_{s-1}^{r_{s-1}}\rfloor}+2^{\lfloor \lfloor k_s^{\lambda}\rfloor^{r_s}\rfloor}:\ k_1,\dots,k_s\in\mathbb N\right\},
\]
with \(\mathcal A_{\lambda}(X):=\mathcal A_{\lambda}\cap[1,2X]\), and let
\[
\mathcal S:=\mathbb P+\mathcal A_{\lambda}(X).
\]
Write \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\) for the number of elements of \(\mathcal S\) in the interval \((x,x+h]\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\) for integers \(x\in[X,2X]\) as \(X\to\infty\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists \(c_{\varepsilon}>0\) such that, for all but \(O_{\varepsilon}\!\left(X\exp\!\left(-c_{\varepsilon}(\log X)^{1/3}\right)\right)\) integers \(x\in[X,2X]\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h\); equivalently, the number of elements of \(\mathcal S\) in \((x,x+h]\) is bounded above and below by positive constant multiples of \(h\) outside an exceptional set of that size.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a set \(\mathcal E_X\subset [X,2X]\cap\mathbb Z\) with \(\#\mathcal E_X=o(X)\) such that for every integer \(x\in([X,2X]\cap\mathbb Z)\setminus \mathcal E_X\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists \(c_{\varepsilon}>0\) such that, for all but \(O_{\varepsilon}\!\left(X\exp\!\left(-c_{\varepsilon}(\log X)^{1/4}\right)\right)\) integers \(x\in[X,2X]\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h\), with implied constants absolute; in particular, they are uniform in \(\lambda\), \(\varepsilon\), and \(\theta\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists \(c_{\varepsilon}>0\) such that, for all but \(O_{\varepsilon}\!\left(X\exp\!\left(-c_{\varepsilon}(\log X)^{1/4}\right)\right)\) integers \(x\in[X,2X]\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h/\log X\); equivalently, the number of elements of \(\mathcal S\) in \((x,x+h]\) is bounded above and below by positive constant multiples of \(h/\log X\) outside that exceptional set.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question:
Let \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) be a locally \(C^{1,1}\) domain, let \(0<s<1\), and fix a boundary point of \(\partial\Omega\). For \(r>0\), let \(B_r\) denote the ball of radius \(r\) centered at that boundary point. Assume \(g\in L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)\), and let \(u\) be a nonnegative classical solution of
\[
\begin{cases}
(-\Delta)^s u=g & \text{in } \Omega\cap B_4,\\
u=0 & \text{in } B_4\setminus \Omega.
\end{cases}
\]
Here \(( -\Delta)^s\) denotes the fractional Laplacian
\[
(-\Delta)^s u(x)=C_{n,s}\,\mathrm{P.V.}\!\int_{\mathbb{R}^n}\frac{u(x)-u(y)}{|x-y|^{n+2s}}\,dy,
\]
and, in the classical sense, one assumes \(u\in C^{1,1}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega)\cap \mathcal L_{2s}\), where
\[
\mathcal L_{2s}=\left\{u\in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(\mathbb{R}^n):\int_{\mathbb{R}^n}\frac{|u(x)|}{1+|x|^{n+2s}}\,dx<\infty\right\}.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the quantitative boundary regularity estimate for \(u\) in \(\Omega\cap B_{1/2}\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
One has \(u\in C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})\) and
\[
\|u\|_{C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})}
\le C\bigl(\|g\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}+\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}\bigr),
\]
where \(C\) depends only on \(n\), \(s\), and the local \(C^{1,1}\) norm of \(\partial\Omega\).

choices.label: B
choices.text:
One has \(u\in C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})\) and
\[
\|u\|_{C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})}
\le C\bigl(\|g\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}+\|u\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^n)}\bigr),
\]
where \(C\) depends only on \(n\), \(s\), and the local \(C^{1,1}\) norm of \(\partial\Omega\)."

choices.label: C
choices.text:
One has \(u\in C^\alpha(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})\) for some \(\alpha\in(0,s]\), and in particular one may take \(\alpha=\min\{s,1-s\}\), with
\[
\|u\|_{C^\alpha(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})}
\le C\bigl(\|g\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}+\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}\bigr),
\]
where \(C\) depends only on \(n\), \(s\), and the local \(C^{1,1}\) norm of \(\partial\Omega\).

choices.label: D
choices.text:
One has \(u\in C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})\) and
\[
\|u\|_{C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})}
\le C\bigl(\|g\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}+\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}\bigr),
\]
where \(C\) depends only on \(n\) and the local \(C^{1,1}\) norm of \(\partial\Omega\), but is independent of \(s\).

choices.label: E
choices.text:
One has \(u\in C^{2s-\varepsilon}(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})\) for every \(0<\varepsilon<2s\), and
\[
\|u\|_{C^{2s-\varepsilon}(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})}
\le C_{\varepsilon}\bigl(\|g\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}+\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}\bigr),
\]
where \(C_{\varepsilon}\) depends only on \(n\), \(s\), \(\varepsilon\), and the local \(C^{1,1}\) norm of \(\partial\Omega\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
One has \(u\in C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})\) and
\[
\|u\|_{C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})}
\le C\bigl(\|g\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}+\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}\bigr),
\]
where \(C\) depends only on \(n\), \(s\), and the local \(C^{1,1}\) norm of \(\partial\Omega\).

theorem:
[Unconditional exceedances and maximum edge length]\label{thm:uncond-max}
Consider the continuum model in which $V$ is a homogeneous Poisson point process on $\mathbb{R}^d$ with unit intensity, and let the connection probabilities be given by
\begin{equation}\label{eq:conn-prob-intro}
p_{xy}=1-\exp\!\left\{-\lambda\,\frac{W_xW_y}{|x-y|^\alpha}\right\},
\qquad \lambda>0,\ \alpha>0,
\end{equation}
For $n\in\mathbb{N}$ set $B_n=[-n,n]^d$ and define
\[
e_n^*:=\max_{\{x,y\}\in E_{n}} |x-y|,
\qquad
E_{n}:=\bigl\{\{x,y\}\in E:\ x\in V\cap B_n\bigr\},
\]
as well as the exceedance count $F(n,r)$ from
\begin{align}
F(n,r)
&:= \sum_{x\in V\cap B_n}\sum_{y\in V\cap B_n^c}
\mathbf{1}_{\{|x-y|>r_n,\, x\leftrightarrow y\}}
+ \frac12\sum_{x\in V\cap B_n}\sum_{y\in V\cap B_n}
\mathbf{1}_{\{|x-y|>r_n,\, x\leftrightarrow y\}},
\label{eq:Fnr-intro}
\end{align}
Assume that the weight distribution satisfies $\mathbb{P}(W>w)=w^{-\beta}$ for $w\ge 1$, with tail index $\beta>0$, and that the
finite-degree condition
\begin{equation}\label{eq:finite-degree-cond-intro}
\min\{\alpha,\alpha\beta\}>d.
\end{equation}
holds.

\smallskip
For $r>0$ set $r_n=c_n r$ and define
\[
\theta=
\begin{cases}
\alpha\beta-d, & 0<\beta<1,\\
\alpha-d, & \beta\ge 1.
\end{cases}
\]
Then, in each of the regimes \textup{(a)}--\textup{(c)} below, with the corresponding choice of $c_n$, we have
\begin{equation*}\label{eq:pois-frechet-unified}
F(n,r)\ \xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{d}\ \mathrm{Poisson}\!\bigl(r^{-\theta}\bigr),
\qquad
\mathbb{P}\!\left(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\le r\right)\ \longrightarrow\ \exp\!\bigl(-r^{-\theta}\bigr),
\qquad r>0,
\end{equation*}
that is, $c_n^{-1}e_n^*$ converges in distribution to a Fr\'echet random variable with parameter $\theta$.

\smallskip
\noindent\textup{(a) {Infinite-mean regime} $(0<\beta<1)$.}
Assume $\alpha\beta\in(d,2d]$ and set
\[
c_n=C_1\,n^{\frac{d}{\alpha\beta-d}}(\log n)^{\frac{1}{\alpha\beta-d}}.
\]

\smallskip
\noindent\textup{(b) {Borderline regime} $(\beta=1)$.}
Assume $\alpha\in(d,2d]$ and set
\[
c_n=C_2\,n^{\frac{d}{\alpha-d}}(\log n)^{\frac{2}{\alpha-d}}.
\]

\smallskip
\noindent\textup{(c) {Finite-mean regime} $(\beta>1)$.}
Assume $\alpha\in(d,2d)$ and set
\[
c_n=C_3\,n^{\frac{d}{\alpha-d}}.
\]
Here $C_1, C_2, C_3\in(0,\infty)$ denote explicit constants independent of $n$ (specified in the following corollary.

\begin{corollary}\label{cor:cn-choice}
Let $\theta:=\alpha\beta-d$ if $0<\beta<1$ and $\theta:=\alpha-d$ if $\beta\ge 1$. Define $c_n$ by
\[
c_n:=
\begin{cases}
\Bigl(2^d\omega_d\,\Gamma(1-\beta)\lambda^\beta\,\beta\,\dfrac{d}{\alpha\beta-d}\cdot \dfrac{\alpha d}{\theta}\Bigr)^{1/\theta}
\,n^{d/\theta}(\log n)^{1/\theta},
& 0<\beta<1,\\[2mm]
\Bigl(2^d\omega_d\,\dfrac12\lambda\,\dfrac{d}{\alpha-d}\cdot \dfrac{\alpha^2 d^2}{\theta^2}\Bigr)^{1/\theta}
\,n^{d/\theta}(\log n)^{2/\theta},
& \beta=1,\\[2mm]
\Bigl(2^d\omega_d\,\lambda\,\dfrac{\beta^2}{(\beta-1)^2}\,\dfrac{d}{\alpha-d}\Bigr)^{1/\theta}
\,n^{d/\theta},
& \beta>1,
\end{cases}
\]
and set $r_n=c_n r$ for fixed $r>0$. Then
\[
I_1 \sim r^{-\theta},
\qquad n\to\infty.
\]
\end{corollary}
sketch:
The post-theorem introduction only gives a high-level outline of the proof strategy (without detailed steps specific to each regime). To prove the main theorem, it says:

- “The Poisson approximation for the exceedance counts is established as follows. First we develop a Palm--coupling variant of Stein's method for Poisson functionals, extending ideas from Mathew Penrose, \emph{Random Geometric Graphs} (2003).”
- “Then, an essential input is a precise asymptotic evaluation of the mean number of exceedances in the relevant scaling regimes.”

It further indicates where the full proof is located: “Next we give the full proof of the main theorem; in particular, we state the Poisson approximation theorem based on a Palm--coupling approach and derive the corresponding exceedance limits.”
question:
In the continuum scale-free percolation model, let \(V\) be a unit-intensity homogeneous Poisson point process on \(\mathbb{R}^d\). Each \(x\in V\) carries an i.i.d. weight \(W_x\) with \(\mathbb{P}(W>w)=w^{-\beta}\) for \(w\ge 1\), and, conditional on \(V\) and the weights, distinct vertices \(x,y\) are connected independently with probability \(p_{xy}=1-\exp\{-\lambda W_xW_y/|x-y|^\alpha\}\), where \(|\cdot|\) is the Euclidean norm. Write \(E\) for the random edge set and \(x\leftrightarrow y\) for the event that \(x\) and \(y\) are joined by an edge. Define \(B_n=[-n,n]^d\), \(E_n=\{\{x,y\}\in E:x\in V\cap B_n\}\), \(e_n^*=\max_{\{x,y\}\in E_n}|x-y|\), and for fixed \(r>0\) let
\[
F(n,r)=\sum_{x\in V\cap B_n}\sum_{y\in V\cap B_n^c} \mathbf{1}_{\{|x-y|>r_n,\ x\leftrightarrow y\}} + \frac12\sum_{x\in V\cap B_n}\sum_{y\in V\cap B_n} \mathbf{1}_{\{|x-y|>r_n,\ x\leftrightarrow y\}},
\]
where \(r_n=c_n r\). Assume \(\min\{\alpha,\alpha\beta\}>d\) and either (a) \(0<\beta<1\) with \(\alpha\beta\in(d,2d]\) and \(c_n=C_1 n^{d/(\alpha\beta-d)}(\log n)^{1/(\alpha\beta-d)}\), or (b) \(\beta=1\) with \(\alpha\in(d,2d]\) and \(c_n=C_2 n^{d/(\alpha-d)}(\log n)^{2/(\alpha-d)}\), or (c) \(\beta>1\) with \(\alpha\in(d,2d)\) and \(c_n=C_3 n^{d/(\alpha-d)}\), where \(C_1,C_2,C_3>0\) are constants independent of \(n\). Writing \(\theta=\alpha\beta-d\) for \(0<\beta<1\) and \(\theta=\alpha-d\) for \(\beta\ge 1\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic behavior of \(F(n,r)\) and \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\) as \(n\to\infty\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(r^{-\theta}\), and \(\mathbb{P}(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\le r)\to \exp(-r^{-\theta})\), so \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\) converges in distribution to a Fr\'echet random variable with parameter \(\theta\); however, in the borderline regime \(\beta=1\) this conclusion requires \(\alpha\in(d,2d)\), excluding the endpoint \(\alpha=2d\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(r^{-\theta}\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(\kappa r^{-\theta}\) for some finite constant \(\kappa=\kappa(d,\alpha,\beta,\lambda)\in(0,\infty)\), and \(\mathbb{P}(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\le r)\to \exp(-\kappa r^{-\theta})\); equivalently, \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\) has a Fr\'echet limit with parameter \(\theta\) but with a non-unit scale.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(r^{-\theta}\), but because edges with both endpoints in \(B_n\) are counted with the factor \(1/2\), the event \(\{e_n^*\le r_n\}\) is not exactly the same as \(\{F(n,r)=0\}\); hence the Poisson limit for \(F(n,r)\) does not by itself yield the Fr\'echet limit for \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

sketch:
To prove the above power counting formulas, the authors “introduce several techniques in the asymptotic analysis of divided differences, and prove upper and lower bounds for them.” A “pivotal concept throughout the proof is the weighted divided difference
$$f'\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}:=(-1)^n x_1\cdots x_n f'[x_1,\ldots,x_n],$$
whose asymptotic behavior is “more easily understood, because sending any of its variables to infinity yields another weighted divided difference.”

A “key result” used in the proof is that “weighted divided differences are positive on large enough subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ for the functions $f$ we are considering,” which generalizes Hunter’s positivity theorem. Moreover, for functions “bounded above or below by $x^{-p}$, with similar bounds on their derivatives,” they show that “the asymptotic behavior of the weighed divided differences away from the origin is determined by the variable with the smallest modulus,” and this is stated to be “crucial in the proof of our main theorem.”

Finally, “our main theorem is proved by combining this positivity and the mentioned upper and lower bounds with an interesting graph-theoretical argument.”,
question: In the spectral action matrix model, let $N\in\mathbb{N}$, let $\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_N$ be spectral parameters, and for any listed arguments let $f'[\mu_0,\dots,\mu_m]$ denote the divided difference of $f'$, defined inductively by $f'[x]:=f'(x)$ and $f'[x,y]:=\frac{f'(x)-f'(y)}{x-y}$ (and similarly for higher orders). For fixed external labels $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, consider the two ribbon-graph amplitudes with one vertex carrying external labels $i$ and $j$: (1) the amplitude with a single attached loop, and (2) the amplitude with two attached loops. Which statement holds for every such pair $(i,j)$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
and the two-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k,l=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_l]}.
\]
Thus each added loop contributes one second-order divided-difference factor, both referenced to the external label $j$.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude is given by
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
while the two-loop amplitude is a double sum over $k,l\in\{1,\dots,N\}$ whose numerator is
\[
f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i],
\]
and whose denominator is a product of two second-order divided differences.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
and the two-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k,l=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_l]f'[\lambda_k,\lambda_l]}.
\]
In other words, the two denominator factors are attached to the pairs $(j,l)$ and $(k,l)$.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
and the two-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k,l=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_l,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]f'[\lambda_k,\lambda_l]}.
\]
So the denominator has the same two factors as in the standard two-loop pattern, but the higher divided difference repeats the internal label $l$ rather than $k$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{thm:main_window}
Let $u\ge 1$, $q\ge 2$ be integers, and let $\mathcal F$ be a finite family of
non-empty binary words.  Let $L=\max\{|f|:f\in\mathcal F\}$ and fix $M\ge L$.
Then the set
\[
   S_u^{(M)}=\{n\ge 0:\ uq^n\in \mathcal K_{\mathcal F}^{(M)}\}
\]
is either finite or ultimately periodic.  That is, there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$
such that
\[
   n\in S_u^{(M)}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad(n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively compute such a pair $(n_0,p)$ and decide whether
$S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite.  In the finite case, the same procedure
outputs all elements of $S_u^{(M)}$.
sketch: To prove the main theorem, the argument is “entirely finite-state”. The key input is that “multiplication by a fixed integer $q$ in the Fibonacci numeration system is realizable by a finite sequential transducer”, and when “reading digits from the least significant side” this yields (for each $M$) “a deterministic update rule on the set of length-$M$ windows $\{0,1,\#\}^M$.” Composing this with “the finite automaton that checks avoidance of $\mathcal F$ inside the window” produces “a deterministic dynamical system on a finite state space.” The orbit corresponding to $(uq^n)_{n\ge 0}$ is thus “eventually periodic”, and “which yields ultimate periodicity of $S_u^{(M)}$ as an immediate corollary.” Finally, “the same finite-state construction gives an explicit algorithm to compute the eventual period” (hence to compute $(n_0,p)$ and decide finiteness, and list elements in the finite case).
question:
Let $(F_n)_{n\ge 0}$ be the Fibonacci numbers, defined by $F_0=0$, $F_1=1$, and $F_{n+2}=F_{n+1}+F_n$. Every integer $N\ge 1$ has a unique Zeckendorf representation
\[
N=\sum_{i=2}^k \varepsilon_i F_i,
\]
where $\varepsilon_i\in\{0,1\}$, $\varepsilon_k=1$, and $\varepsilon_i\varepsilon_{i+1}=0$. Write $\operatorname{rev}(Z(N))=\varepsilon_2\varepsilon_3\cdots \varepsilon_k$ for the Zeckendorf digits read from least significant to most significant, and let $\widetilde Z(N)=\operatorname{rev}(Z(N))\,\#^{\omega}$, where $\#\notin\{0,1\}$ is a padding symbol.

Let $u\ge 1$ and $q\ge 2$ be integers, let $\mathcal F$ be a finite family of non-empty binary words, let $L=\max\{|f|:f\in\mathcal F\}$, and fix $M\ge L$. Define
\[
\mathcal K_{\mathcal F}^{(M)}=\{N\ge 1:\text{ the prefix of length }M\text{ of }\widetilde Z(N)\text{ contains no factor from }\mathcal F\},
\]
and
\[
S_u^{(M)}=\{n\ge 0: u q^n\in \mathcal K_{\mathcal F}^{(M)}\}.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about $S_u^{(M)}$?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
$S_u^{(M)}$ is either finite or ultimately periodic: there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$ such that
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively compute such a pair $(n_0,p)$ and decide whether $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite; in the finite case, the same procedure outputs all elements of $S_u^{(M)}$.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
$S_u^{(M)}$ is either finite or ultimately periodic: for each fixed $q$, $\mathcal F$, and $M$, there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$, independent of $u$, such that for every $u\ge 1$ one has
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively compute such a universal pair $(n_0,p)$ and decide for each $u$ whether $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite; in the finite case, the same procedure outputs all elements of $S_u^{(M)}$.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
$S_u^{(M)}$ is ultimately periodic: there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$ such that
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively compute such a pair $(n_0,p)$.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
$S_u^{(M)}$ is either finite or ultimately periodic: there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$ such that
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can always choose these with $n_0<3^M$ and $1\le p\le 3^M$, and effectively compute such a pair together with a decision procedure for whether $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite; in the finite case, the same procedure outputs all elements of $S_u^{(M)}$.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
$S_u^{(M)}$ is either finite or ultimately periodic: there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$ such that
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively decide whether $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite and, in the infinite case, compute an eventual period $p$; however, in general there is no procedure that always computes a valid preperiod $n_0$ or lists all elements when $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
$S_u^{(M)}$ is either finite or ultimately periodic: there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$ such that
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively compute such a pair $(n_0,p)$ and decide whether $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite; in the finite case, the same procedure outputs all elements of $S_u^{(M)}$.

theorem:
\label{T1} Let $(M,\xi,\eta,\Phi,g,\omega)$ be a complete noncompact Sasakian
$(2n+1)$-manifold of nonnegative transverse bisectional curvature. Suppose
that the maximal volume growth holds, then

\begin{enumerate}
\item $M$ is CR-biholomorphic to $\Omega\times\mathbb{R},$ where $\Omega$ is a
pseuodoconvex domain in the $\mathbb{C}^{n}.$

\item In addition for $n=2$, if the transverse bisectional curvature is
positive, then $M$ must be CR-biholomorphic to the standard Heisenberg group
of dimension five
\[\begin{array}
[c]{c}\mathbb{H}_{2}=\mathbb{C}^{2}\times\mathbb{R}.
\end{array}
\]

\end{enumerate},
question:
Let \((M,\eta,\xi,\Phi,g)\) be a complete noncompact Sasakian \(5\)-manifold. Assume that \(M\) has maximal volume growth, meaning that for some point \(p\in M\),
\[
\lim_{r\to\infty}\frac{\mathrm{Vol}_{\xi}(B_p(r))}{r^{4}}=\alpha_0>0,
\]
where \(B_p(r)\) is the metric ball of radius \(r\) and \(\mathrm{Vol}_{\xi}\) denotes the transverse volume, and assume that the transverse bisectional curvature of the transverse K\"ahler metric \(g^T=g|_{\ker\eta}\) is positive. Up to CR-biholomorphism, which CR manifold must \(M\) be?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The standard Heisenberg group of dimension five, \(\mathbb{H}_2 = \mathbb{C}^2 \times \mathbb{R}\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: A CR manifold CR-biholomorphic to \(\Omega\times \mathbb{R}\), where \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{C}^2\) is a smoothly bounded strictly pseudoconvex domain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A CR manifold CR-biholomorphic to \(\Omega\times \mathbb{R}\), where \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{C}^2\) is a pseudoconvex domain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A CR manifold CR-biholomorphic to a nontrivial finite quotient \(\mathbb{H}_2/\Gamma\), where \(\Gamma\) acts freely by CR automorphisms.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The smooth product \(\mathbb{C}^2\times \mathbb{R}\) equipped with the product CR structure induced from the standard complex structure on \(\mathbb{C}^2\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: The standard Heisenberg group of dimension five, \(\mathbb{H}_2 = \mathbb{C}^2 \times \mathbb{R}\).

theorem:
\label{ergodic-th}
If Assumption B holds, then $\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in \mathbb{R}}$ is ergodic, for any $t>0$. Consequently, by the mean ergodic theorem,
\[
\frac{1}{R}F_R(t) \to 0 \quad \mbox{a.s and in $L^2(\Omega)$}, \quad \mbox{as $R \to \infty$}.
\]
question:
Let \(u\) be the solution to the one-dimensional stochastic wave equation
\[
\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}(t,x)=\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}(t,x)+u(t,x)\,\dot X(t,x),\qquad t>0,\ x\in\mathbb R,
\]
with initial conditions \(u(0,x)=1\) and \(\partial_t u(0,x)=0\), where \(X\) is the Lévy colored noise from the setup. Suppose Assumption B holds, and let \(x\mapsto u(t,x)\) denote the spatial random field at a fixed time \(t>0\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the field \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb R}\) for every \(t>0\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: For every fixed \(t>0\), the spatial process \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}}\) is ergodic.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every fixed \(t>0\), the spatial process \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}}\) is strongly mixing under spatial shifts, since the one-dimensional wave kernel has finite propagation speed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every fixed \(t>0\), the spatial process \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}}\) is stationary with respect to spatial translations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For sufficiently large times \(t\), but not necessarily for all \(t>0\), the spatial process \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}}\) becomes ergodic because the wave kernel averages the noise over larger spatial regions.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every fixed \(t>0\), the spatial process \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}}\) is ergodic, and moreover \(R^{-1}F_R(t)\to 0\) almost surely and in \(L^2(\Omega)\) uniformly for \(t\) in every compact interval of \((0,\infty)\) as \(R\to\infty\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For every fixed \(t>0\), the spatial process \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}}\) is ergodic.

sketch: To prove the main theorem, we combine two ingredients: (i) “the controllability of the associated linearized system” and (ii) “the use of Schauder's fixed-point theorem to the nonlinear system.” More specifically, the paper “present[s] the reduction of the radial problem to the one-dimensional case and show[s] the null controllability of the linearized system using an adapted Carleman inequality,” and then “prove[s] the main theorem using Schauder's fixed point theorem.”
question:
Let \(0<b<R_*<R_0<E\), let \(T>0\), and let \(y_0\in H_0^1(B_{R_0})\) be radially symmetric, where \(B_r\subset \mathbb{R}^3\) denotes the ball of radius \(r\) centered at the origin. Let \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be globally Lipschitz with \(f(0)=0\), let \(\omega:=B_b\), and let \(1_\omega\) denote the characteristic function of \(\omega\). Consider the free-boundary problem on the moving ball \(B_{R(t)}\):
\[
\begin{cases}
 y_t-\Delta y+f(y)=1_{\omega}u, & \text{in } Q_R,\\
 y(x,t)=0, & \text{on } \Sigma_R,\\
 y(\cdot,0)=y_0, & \text{in } B_{R(0)},
\end{cases}
\qquad
R'(t)=-\nabla y(x,t)\cdot \hat n \quad \text{for } x\in \partial B_{R(t)},
\]
with \(R(0)=R_0\), \(Q_R=\{(x,t):x\in B_{R(t)},\ 0<t<T\}\), \(\Sigma_R=\{(x,t):x\in \partial B_{R(t)},\ 0<t<T\}\), and \(\hat n\) the outward unit normal on \(\partial B_{R(t)}\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about controllability for this three-dimensional free-boundary system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The system is locally null controllable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The system is globally null controllable.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The system is controllable to the zero state for all sufficiently small radially symmetric initial data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every radially symmetric initial datum, there exists a control depending only on the initial radius and the Lipschitz bound of \(f\) such that the system is locally null controllable.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The system is locally null controllable without assuming that the initial datum \(y_0\) is radially symmetric.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: The system is locally null controllable.

theorem: \label{thm:main} An extreme ray $r$ of the SONC cone on a finite ground set $\mathcal{A}\subseteq\NN^{n}$ is not exposed if and only if $r=\R_+ \mathbf{x}^\alpb$ for some $\alpb\in \mathcal{A}$, and there exists a circuit $(S,\betab)$ on $\mathcal{A}$ such that $\alpb \in S$ and $\betab\neq \alpb$ is even.,
sketch: The result is proved by (i) "showing separately that the extreme rays that correspond to the conditions in the theorem are not exposing," and (ii) "constructing explicit exposing normals for all of the remaining cases." The construction of exposing normals "relies on a graded partition of the ground set $\mathcal{A}$" which "allows precise control over the relative magnitudes of the normal entries and reflects the combinatorial layering inherent in $\mathcal{A}$." The proof is "fully constructive" in that it provides "explicit normals certifying exposure of each ray." Concretely, the paper "establish[es] the negative part of the result" in Proposition~\ref{prop:unexposed}, then proves the remaining extreme rays are exposed in Propositions~\ref{prop:monomial} and~\ref{prop:nonmonomial}, and "finalize[s] the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:main}."
question: Let \(\mathcal{A}\subseteq \mathbb{N}^n\) be finite, and let \(r\) be an extreme ray of the SONC cone on \(\mathcal{A}\), i.e. the cone of sums of nonnegative circuit polynomials whose exponents lie in \(\mathcal{A}\). Here \(\mathbf{x}^\alpha=x_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots x_n^{\alpha_n}\), \(\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) denotes the ray generated by the monomial \(\mathbf{x}^\alpha\), an exponent \(\beta\) is called even if \(\beta\in (2\mathbb{N})^n\), and a circuit \((S,\beta)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\) means that \(S\subseteq \mathcal{A}\cap (2\mathbb{N})^n\) is affinely independent, \(\beta\in \mathcal{A}\), and \(\beta\) lies in the relative interior of \(\operatorname{conv}(S)\). Which statement is equivalent to saying that \(r\) is not exposed (i.e. not an exposed face of the cone)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: \(r=\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) for some \(\alpha\in \mathcal{A}\), and there exists a circuit \((S,\beta)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\) such that \(\alpha\in S\) and \(\beta\neq \alpha\) is even.

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(r=\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) for some even \(\alpha\in \mathcal{A}\), and there exists a circuit \((S,\alpha)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\), i.e. \(\alpha\) itself occurs as an inner exponent of a circuit on \(\mathcal{A}\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: \(r=\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) for some \(\alpha\in \mathcal{A}\), and there exists a circuit \((S,\beta)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\) for which \(\alpha\in S\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(r=\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) for some \(\alpha\in \mathcal{A}\), and for every circuit \((S,\beta)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\) with \(\alpha\in S\), the inner exponent \(\beta\) is even.

choices.label: E
choices.text: \(r=\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) for some even \(\alpha\in \mathcal{A}\), and there exists a circuit \((S,\beta)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\) such that \(\alpha\) lies in the relative interior of \(\operatorname{conv}(S)\) and \(\beta\) is even.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: \(r=\mathbb{R}_+\mathbf{x}^\alpha\) for some \(\alpha\in \mathcal{A}\), and there exists a circuit \((S,\beta)\) on \(\mathcal{A}\) such that \(\alpha\in S\) and \(\beta\neq \alpha\) is even.

question:
Let \(T>0\), \(\mathbb R^+=(0,\infty)\), and \(s>\tfrac34\). Suppose \(u_0\in H^s(\mathbb R^+)\) and \(f\in H^{\frac{s+1}{3}}_{loc}(\mathbb R^+)\) satisfy the required compatibility conditions at \((x,t)=(0,0)\), where \(H^{\alpha}_{loc}(\mathbb R^+)\) denotes the local Sobolev space on \(\mathbb R^+\). For the initial-boundary value problem
\[
\begin{cases}
\partial_t u+\partial_x(\partial_x^2u+u^2)=0, & (x,t)\in \mathbb R^+\times(0,T),\\
u(x,0)=u_0(x), & x\in \mathbb R^+,\\
u(0,t)=f(t), & t\in(0,T),
\end{cases}
\]
what is the strongest statement that can be proved about existence and uniqueness of solutions to this IBVP?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
There exists exactly one solution \(u\) to this IBVP such that
\[
u\in C\bigl((0,T);H^s(\mathbb R^+)\bigr)\cap L^2\bigl(0,T;H^{s+1}_{loc}(\mathbb R^+)\bigr).
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exists exactly one solution \(u\) to this IBVP such that
\[
u\in C\bigl((0,T);H^s(\mathbb R^+)\bigr)\cap L^2\bigl(0,T;H^{s+1}(\mathbb R^+)\bigr).
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists a solution \(u\) to this IBVP such that
\[
u\in C\bigl((0,T);H^s(\mathbb R^+)\bigr)\cap L^2\bigl(0,T;H^{s+1}_{loc}(\mathbb R^+)\bigr).
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every solution \(u\) to this IBVP, one has
\[
u\in C\bigl((0,T);H^s(\mathbb R^+)\bigr)\cap L^2\bigl(0,T;H^{s+1}_{loc}(\mathbb R^+)\bigr).
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists exactly one solution \(u\) to this IBVP such that
\[
u\in C\bigl([0,T];H^s(\mathbb R^+)\bigr)\cap L^2\bigl(0,T;H^{s+1}_{loc}(\mathbb R^+)\bigr).
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
There exists exactly one solution \(u\) to this IBVP such that
\[
u\in C\bigl((0,T);H^s(\mathbb R^+)\bigr)\cap L^2\bigl(0,T;H^{s+1}_{loc}(\mathbb R^+)\bigr).
\]

sketch: The post-theorem introduction states that the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:mainisoperim} “relies on a crucial induction argument of Kahn and Park \cite{KP20}, which reduced proving lower bounds for $\mathbf{E} h_A^\beta$ to finding a certain Bellman function and verifying a two-point inequality.” It further says: “Our Bellman function is a further refinement of the function used in \cite{DIR24}, which in turn builds on important work by Beltran--Ivanisvili--Madrid \cite{BIM23}.” Finally, it notes that “[i]n the proof of the two-point inequality we draw from estimates and tools introduced in \cite{DIR24}.”
question: Let \(\{0,1\}^n\) be equipped with the uniform probability measure, and for a set \(A\subseteq \{0,1\}^n\) write \(|A|\) for its measure under this distribution. For each \(x\in \{0,1\}^n\), define \(h_A(x)\) to be the number of Hamming neighbors of \(x\) that lie outside \(A\) if \(x\in A\), and \(h_A(x)=0\) if \(x\notin A\). Assume \(|A|\le \tfrac12\), and let \(\mathbf E\) denote expectation over uniform \(x\in\{0,1\}^n\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(\mathbf E\sqrt{h_A}\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: \[\mathbf E\sqrt{h_A}\ge |A|\sqrt{\log_2\!\bigl(1/|A|\bigr)}\qquad\text{for all }A\subseteq\{0,1\}^n.\]

choices.label: C
choices.text: \[\mathbf E\sqrt{h_A}\ge |A|\sqrt{\ln\!\bigl(1/|A|\bigr)}\qquad\text{for all }A\subseteq\{0,1\}^n\text{ with }|A|\le \tfrac12.\]

choices.label: D
choices.text: \[\mathbf E\sqrt{h_A}\ge |A|\sqrt{\log_2\!\bigl(1/|A|\bigr)+1}\qquad\text{for all }A\subseteq\{0,1\}^n\text{ with }|A|\le \tfrac12.\]

choices.label: E
choices.text: \[\mathbf E\sqrt{h_A}\ge |A|\sqrt{\log_2\!\Bigl(\frac{1}{|A|(1-|A|)}\Bigr)}\qquad\text{for all }A\subseteq\{0,1\}^n\text{ with }|A|\le \tfrac12.\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{thm:main}
There exists a constant $c>0$ such that the following holds. Let $M$ be a closed hyperbolic 3-manifold and let $\gamma\subset M$ be a simple closed geodesic. Suppose that $\gamma$ has length $\ell$ and has an embedded tubular neighborhood of radius at least $R\in(0,1)$. Then the unique hyperbolic metric on $M-\gamma$ has volume
\[
{\rm vol}(M-\gamma)\le{\rm vol}(M)+c\frac{\ell}{R}.
\]
sketch:
To prove Theorem~\ref{thm:main}, the authors “follow the approach of \cite[Theorem 10.1]{AST} with a difference.” The steps described are:
\begin{itemize}
\item “We first change the metric of $M$ inside a tubular neighborhood $T$ of $\gamma$ interpolating between the metric on the tube and the metric on a suitable hyperbolic cusp.”
\item This is done “by keeping under control the scalar curvature (see Lemma~\ref{lem:functiondrill}).”
\item “Then we compare the volume of the modified metric with that of the hyperbolic structure on $M-\gamma$.”
\item “Instead of relying on Perelman's monotonicity formula,” they “exploit some other consequences of Perelman's work \cite{Pe1,Pe2}, namely, the exact computation of the sigma invariant of closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds as achieved by Anderson \cite{And} and Kleiner and Lott \cite{KL}.”
\item “This allows us to keep track of the fact that we \emph{did not change} the metric outside the tubular neighborhood $T$ and obtain an additive error rather than a multiplicative one in the volume increase.”
\end{itemize}
question: Let $M$ be a closed hyperbolic $3$-manifold, and let $\gamma\subset M$ be a simple closed geodesic of length $\ell$. Assume that $\gamma$ has an embedded tubular neighborhood, with respect to the hyperbolic metric on $M$, of radius at least $R$, where $R\in(0,1)$. The complement $M-\gamma$ carries a unique hyperbolic metric. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the quantitative estimate for $\operatorname{vol}(M-\gamma)$, with a constant independent of $M$, $\gamma$, $\ell$, and $R$?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
There exists a constant $c>0$ such that for every such $M$, $\gamma$, $\ell$, and $R\in(0,1)$, one has
\[
\operatorname{vol}(M-\gamma)\le \operatorname{vol}(M)+c\frac{\ell}{R}.
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exists a constant $c>0$ such that for every such $M$, $\gamma$, $\ell$, and $R\in(0,1)$, one has
\[
\operatorname{vol}(M-\gamma)\le \operatorname{vol}(M)+c\,\ell\,\log\!\left(\frac1R\right).
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
For every fixed $\rho\in(0,1)$ there exists a constant $c_\rho>0$ such that for every such $M$, $\gamma$, and $\ell$, if $\gamma$ has an embedded tubular neighborhood of radius at least $R\ge \rho$, then
\[
\operatorname{vol}(M-\gamma)\le \operatorname{vol}(M)+c_\rho\ell.
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exists a constant $c>0$ such that for every such $M$, $\gamma$, $\ell$, and $R\in(0,1)$, one has
\[
\operatorname{vol}(M-\gamma)\le \operatorname{vol}(M)+c\ell.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists a constant $c>0$ such that for every such $M$, $\gamma$, $\ell$, and $R\in(0,1)$, one has
\[
\operatorname{vol}(M-\gamma)\le \operatorname{vol}(M)+c\,\ell R.
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
There exists a constant $c>0$ such that for every such $M$, $\gamma$, $\ell$, and $R\in(0,1)$, one has
\[
\operatorname{vol}(M-\gamma)\le \operatorname{vol}(M)+c\frac{\ell}{R}.
\]

question:
Fix \(R>0\) sufficiently large so that \(\phi(R)>\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\), where \(\phi\) is the kink profile for the nonlinear Schr\"odinger equation
\[
i\psi_t=\psi_{xx}-\psi+4|\psi|^2\psi-3|\psi|^4\psi,
\]
and \(\phi(x)\to 0\) as \(x\to-\infty\), \(\phi(x)\to 1\) as \(x\to+\infty\). Let the energy space be
\[
\mathcal E=\left\{\psi\in H^1_{\rm loc}(\mathbb R):\ \psi_x\in L^2(\mathbb R),\ \psi\sqrt{1-|\psi|^2}\in L^2(\mathbb R),\ \psi\sqrt{1-|\psi|^4}\in L^2(\mathbb R)\right\}.
\]
Define the Hilbert space \(\mathcal H_R\) by
\[
\langle f,g\rangle_{\mathcal H_R}:=\int_{-\infty}^R f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx+\int_R^{\infty}\frac{1-\phi^2(x)}{1-\phi^2(R)}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx,
\]
with norm \(\|f\|_{\mathcal H_R}=\langle f,f\rangle_{\mathcal H_R}^{1/2}\). For \(\psi,\phi\in \mathcal E\cap\mathcal H_R\), define
\[
\rho_R(\psi,\phi)=\|\psi_x-\phi_x\|_{L^2(\mathbb R)}^2+\|\psi-\phi\|_{\mathcal H_R}^2+\||\psi|^2-|\phi|^2\|_{L^2(R,\infty)}^2.
\]
Here \(\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb R\) represent phase and spatial shifts via \(e^{i\alpha}\psi(t,\cdot+\beta)\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about every sufficiently small perturbation \(\psi_0\in\mathcal E\) of \(\phi\) measured by \(\rho_R\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every small \(\epsilon>0\) there exists \(\delta>0\) such that, if \(\psi_0\in\mathcal E\) satisfies \(\rho_R(\psi_0,\phi)\le \delta^2\), then the unique solution \(\psi\in C^0(\mathbb R,\mathcal E)\) of
\[
i\psi_t=\psi_{xx}-\psi+4|\psi|^2\psi-3|\psi|^4\psi
\]
with initial condition \(\psi|_{t=0}=\psi_0\) admits constants \(\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb R\), depending on \(\psi_0\) but not on \(t\), such that
\[
\rho_R\bigl(e^{i\alpha}\psi(t,\cdot+\beta),\phi\bigr)\le \epsilon^2
\quad \text{for all } t\in\mathbb R.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
For every small \(\epsilon>0\) there exists \(\delta>0\) such that, if \(\psi_0\in\mathcal E\) satisfies \(\rho_R(\psi_0,\phi)\le \delta^2\), then the unique solution \(\psi\in C^0(\mathbb R,\mathcal E)\) of
\[
i\psi_t=\psi_{xx}-\psi+4|\psi|^2\psi-3|\psi|^4\psi
\]
with initial condition \(\psi|_{t=0}=\psi_0\) satisfies
\[
\inf_{\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb R}\rho_R\bigl(e^{i\alpha}\psi(t,\cdot+\beta),\phi\bigr)\le \epsilon^2
\quad \text{for all } t\ge 0.
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every \(R>0\) and every small \(\epsilon>0\) there exists \(\delta>0\) such that, if \(\psi_0\in\mathcal E\) satisfies \(\rho_R(\psi_0,\phi)\le \delta^2\), then the unique solution \(\psi\in C^0(\mathbb R,\mathcal E)\) of
\[
i\psi_t=\psi_{xx}-\psi+4|\psi|^2\psi-3|\psi|^4\psi
\]
with initial condition \(\psi|_{t=0}=\psi_0\) satisfies
\[
\inf_{\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb R}\rho_R\bigl(e^{i\alpha}\psi(t,\cdot+\beta),\phi\bigr)\le \epsilon^2
\quad \text{for all } t\in\mathbb R.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every small \(\epsilon>0\) there exists \(\delta>0\) such that, if \(\psi_0\in\mathcal E\) satisfies \(\rho_R(\psi_0,\phi)\le \delta^2\), then the unique solution \(\psi\in C^0(\mathbb R,\mathcal E)\) of
\[
i\psi_t=\psi_{xx}-\psi+4|\psi|^2\psi-3|\psi|^4\psi
\]
with initial condition \(\psi|_{t=0}=\psi_0\) satisfies
\[
\inf_{\beta\in\mathbb R}\rho_R\bigl(\psi(t,\cdot+\beta),\phi\bigr)\le \epsilon^2
\quad \text{for all } t\in\mathbb R.
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question:
Let \(X\) be a compact arithmetic congruence complex hyperbolic surface, and let \(\psi\) be an \(L^2\)-normalized Hecke--Maass form on \(X\) with spectral parameter \(\lambda>0\). Let \(\Pi\) denote the space of unit-length geodesic segments in \(X\), and for \(\gamma\in\Pi\) let \(\mathcal T_{\lambda^{-1/2}}(\gamma)\) be the \(\lambda^{-1/2}\)-neighborhood of \(\gamma)\). If the Kakeya--Nikodym norm is defined by
\[
\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN}:=\sup_{\gamma\in\Pi}\|\psi|_{\mathcal T_{\lambda^{-1/2}}(\gamma)}\|_2,
\]
what is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN}\) for every \(\epsilon>0\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: For every \(\epsilon>0\), one has \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \ll \lambda^{-1/28+\epsilon}\), where the implied constant depends only on \(X\) and \(\epsilon\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists an absolute constant \(A>0\) such that \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \ll \lambda^{-1/28}(\log \lambda)^A\), where the implied constant depends only on \(X\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists some \(\delta>0\) such that \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \ll \lambda^{-\delta}\), where the implied constant depends only on \(X\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every \(\epsilon>0\), one has \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \ll \lambda^{-1/24+\epsilon}\), where the implied constant depends only on \(X\) and \(\epsilon\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists a constant \(C_X>0\) such that for every \(\epsilon>0\) and every \(\lambda>0\), one has \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \le C_X\lambda^{-1/28+\epsilon}\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For every \(\epsilon>0\), one has \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \ll \lambda^{-1/28+\epsilon}\), where the implied constant depends only on \(X\) and \(\epsilon\).

theorem: \label{t:mainthm1} Fix $(M^{2n}, J)$ an OT manifold and $g_0$ a pluriclosed metric on $M$. The solution to pluriclosed flow with initial data $g_0$ exists on $[0, \infty)$.,
sketch: The proof of To prove the main theorem, is described only at a high level: it “exploits [a] natural class of background metrics arising from the homogeneous structure on OT-manifolds.” In the case of Inoue surfaces, “these are known as Tricerri metrics.”
question: Let \((M^{2n},J)\) be an OT manifold (that is, an Oeljeklaus--Toma complex manifold, with complex structure \(J\)), and let \(g_0\) be a pluriclosed metric on \(M\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the pluriclosed flow with initial metric \(g_0\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The all-time existence conclusion is guaranteed only when the initial metric \(g_0\) belongs to the OT-homogeneous background class (for instance, a Tricerri-type metric), and not for an arbitrary pluriclosed metric.

choices.label: C
choices.text: In particular, the flow can be continued through every finite time: for each \(T>0\), the solution with initial data \(g_0\) exists on \([0,T]\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: What is guaranteed is a uniform positive lifespan depending only on the OT manifold \((M,J)\), so the solution exists on some interval \([0,T]\) independent of \(g_0\), but no general conclusion that it exists for all \(t\ge 0\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: The theorem guarantees more than existence: after passing to the normalized pluriclosed flow, every solution starting from a pluriclosed \(g_0\) converges in the Gromov--Hausdorff sense to the canonical torus associated to the OT data.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
[Main theorem, informal]
Let $X$ be a local spectral expander whose links are $\beta$-coboundary expanders. For every group $\Gamma$ and every locally minimal cochain $f : X(k) \to \Gamma$, if $\left\|f\right\| \lesssim \beta^k/(k+2)!$ then $\left\|\delta f\right\| \ge c\left\|f\right\|$, where $c = \beta^k/(k!(k+1)^4)$.
sketch:
We found a bridge between these techniques that allows us to use the local-to-global averaging operators for any cochain which is not necessarily a cocycle. **Key Idea:** any general cochain either has a ``global'' structure (``it looks much like a cocycle'') or it has a ``local'' structure (``it is concentrated on links of some smaller dimension''). This enables applying the local-to-global averaging operators to general cochains.

Moreover, the method ``decouples between the coboundary and spectral expansion of the links'': in the ``global'' case expansion is deduced ``merely by spectral arguments'', while in the ``local'' case expansion is deduced ``merely by coboundary expansion of the links''.

Concretely, the proof proceeds by a case analysis of general cochains (Propositions 3.3 and 3.4): if the cochain is ``local'' one gets expansion from Proposition 3.3; if the cochain is ``global'' one gets expansion from Proposition 3.4. ``We prove our main theorem with these two propositions and then we turn to prove each proposition.''
question:
Let $X$ be a simplicial complex that is a local spectral expander, and suppose every link of $X$ is a $\beta$-coboundary expander. Fix a group $\Gamma$ and a locally minimal $k$-cochain $f\colon X(k)\to \Gamma$ (that is, a function on the set $X(k)$ of $k$-faces that is minimal on every link), where $\delta f$ denotes the coboundary of $f$ and $\|\cdot\|$ is the cochain norm. Assume that
$$\|f\|\lesssim \frac{\beta^k}{(k+2)!}.$$ 
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the coboundary norm $\|\delta f\|$ under these hypotheses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
It satisfies the linear lower bound
$$\|\delta f\|\ge c\,\|f\|,$$
with
$$c=\frac{\beta^k}{k!(k+2)^4},$$
that is,
$$\|\delta f\|\ge \frac{\beta^k}{k!(k+2)^4}\,\|f\|.$$

choices.label: C
choices.text:
It is nonzero whenever $f\neq 0$, i.e.
$$\|\delta f\|>0.$$

choices.label: D
choices.text:
It satisfies the linear lower bound
$$\|\delta f\|\ge c\,\|f\|,$$
with
$$c=\frac{\beta^{k+1}}{k!(k+1)^4},$$
that is,
$$\|\delta f\|\ge \frac{\beta^{k+1}}{k!(k+1)^4}\,\|f\|.$$

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists a constant
$$c=\frac{\beta^k}{k!(k+1)^4}$$
such that for every locally minimal $k$-cochain $f\colon X(k)\to \Gamma$,
$$\|\delta f\|\ge c\,\|f\|,$$
without needing the assumption
$$\|f\|\lesssim \frac{\beta^k}{(k+2)!}.$$

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{thm0}

		Let $M$ be a closed orientable $n$-manifold with an open book decomposition $\textrm{OB}(P^{n-1},\rho)$. Let $B^{n-2}$ be a connceted component of the binding $\partial P$ and let $\textrm{OB}(V_B,\phi_B)$ be an open book decomposition of $B$. Then, $\textrm{OB}(V_B, \phi_B^k)$ spun embeds in $\textrm{OB}(P^{n-1},\rho)$ for all $k \in \mathbb{Z}$.
sketch: The only proof-related description given is that \emph{``Theorem \ref{thm0} is proved using a simple generalization of the \emph{Hopf annulus trick} (see section \ref{genhopftrick}), which we call the \emph{page trick}.''} No further steps or outline of the argument are provided in the post-theorem introduction.
question: Let \(M\) be a closed orientable \(n\)-manifold with an open book decomposition \(\mathrm{OB}(P^{n-1},\rho)\), where \(P\) is the page, \(\rho\) is the monodromy, and the binding is \(\partial P\). Let \(B^{n-2}\) be a connected component of this binding, and suppose \(B\) itself has an open book decomposition \(\mathrm{OB}(V_B,\phi_B)\). For each integer \(k\), write \(\mathrm{OB}(V_B,\phi_B^k)\) for the open book on \(B\) with page \(V_B\) and monodromy the \(k\)-th iterate of \(\phi_B\). Here, saying that one open book spun embeds in another means there is an embedding taking page to page and compatible with the monodromies up to isotopy. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the spun embeddability of \(\mathrm{OB}(V_B,\phi_B^k)\) into \(\mathrm{OB}(P^{n-1},\rho)\) for every such \(M\), \(B\), and integer \(k\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every integer \(k \ge 0\), the open book \(\mathrm{OB}(V_B,\phi_B^k)\) spun embeds in \(\mathrm{OB}(P^{n-1},\rho)\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The open book \(\mathrm{OB}(V_B,\phi_B)\) spun embeds in \(\mathrm{OB}(P^{n-1},\rho)\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every integer \(k \in \mathbb{Z}\), there exists an open book decomposition \(\mathrm{OB}(V_B,\psi_k)\) of \(B\), with \(\psi_k\) isotopic to \(\phi_B^k\), that spun embeds in \(\mathrm{OB}(P^{n-1},\rho)\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every integer \(k \in \mathbb{Z}\), the open book \(\mathrm{OB}(V_B,\phi_B^k)\) smoothly embeds in \(\mathrm{OB}(P^{n-1},\rho)\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
BHS, \cite[Theorem 1.1, Remark 1.2]{BHS}
\label{thm intro main}
If $G$ is an algebraic group over a perfect field $k$, then $\pi_0^{\#A^1}(G)$ is a strongly $\#A^1$-invariant sheaf. Consequently, for any Nisnevich locally
trivial $G$-torsor $\@P \to \@X$ in $\@H(k)$,
\[
P \to X \to BG
\]
is an $\#A^1$-fiber sequence.
sketch: One of the key steps in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm intro main} is \cite[Lemma 5.1]{BHS}. The note explains that the proof of \cite[Lemma 5.1]{BHS} in \cite{BHS} is incomplete for two reasons: (1) it relies on a result in \cite{Choudhury-Hogadi} whose proof contains a gap, and (2) “the existence of the key exact sequence at the bottom of \cite[page 646]{BHS} (in particular, the application of \cite[Theorem 6.50]{Morel-book}) is not properly justified.” The strategy described is to “give a complete and self-contained proof of \cite[Lemma 5.1]{BHS}, thereby completing the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm intro main}.” Concretely: Section~\ref{section preliminaries} develops preliminaries on long exact sequences of simplicial homotopy groups for homotopy principal fibrations of simplicial sheaves; Section~\ref{section key LES} establishes the “key exact sequence” needed for (2); and Section~\ref{section main result} uses the results of Section~\ref{section key LES} together with “an argument circumventing the use of results from \cite{Choudhury-Hogadi}” to prove \cite[Lemma 5.1]{BHS} (see Lemma~\ref{lemma isogeny}), which then completes the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm intro main}.
question: Let $k$ be a perfect field, let $G$ be an algebraic group over $k$, and let $\mathcal H(k)$ denote the pointed unstable $\#A^1$-homotopy category of simplicial Nisnevich sheaves on smooth $k$-schemes. Write $\pi_0^{\#A^1}(G)$ for the Nisnevich sheaf of $\#A^1$-connected components of $G$. If $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X$ is any Nisnevich locally trivial $G$-torsor in $\mathcal H(k)$, and $BG$ is the classifying space of $G$, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about every such $G$ and every such torsor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $\pi_0^{\#A^1}(G)$ is a strongly $\#A^1$-invariant sheaf, and for every Nisnevich locally trivial $G$-torsor $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X$ in $\mathcal H(k)$, the sequence $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X \to BG$ is a $\#A^1$-fiber sequence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: $\pi_0^{\#A^1}(G)$ is a strongly $\#A^1$-invariant sheaf, and for every \u00e9tale locally trivial $G$-torsor $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X$ in $\mathcal H(k)$, the sequence $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X \to BG$ is a $\#A^1$-fiber sequence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every Nisnevich locally trivial $G$-torsor $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X$ in $\mathcal H(k)$, the sequence $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X \to BG$ is a $\#A^1$-fiber sequence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: $\pi_0^{\#A^1}(G)$ is a strongly $\#A^1$-invariant sheaf, and for every Nisnevich locally trivial $G$-torsor $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X$ in $\mathcal H(k)$, the sequence $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X \to B\pi_0^{\#A^1}(G)$ is a $\#A^1$-fiber sequence.

choices.label: E
choices.text: $\pi_0^{\#A^1}(G)$ is a strongly $\#A^1$-invariant sheaf, and for every Nisnevich locally trivial $G$-torsor $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X$ in $\mathcal H(k)$, the sequence $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X \to BG$ is a simplicial fiber sequence in the unstable Nisnevich homotopy category.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: $\pi_0^{\#A^1}(G)$ is a strongly $\#A^1$-invariant sheaf, and for every Nisnevich locally trivial $G$-torsor $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X$ in $\mathcal H(k)$, the sequence $\mathcal P \to \mathcal X \to BG$ is a $\#A^1$-fiber sequence.

question: Let $k$ be a positive integer and let $\Gamma$ be a finite graph. Say that $\Gamma$ is $k$-set-homogeneous if, whenever two induced subgraphs of $\Gamma$ on $k$ vertices are isomorphic, some automorphism of $\Gamma$ maps the vertex set of one onto the vertex set of the other. Which statement is equivalent to $\Gamma$ being $k$-set-homogeneous?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $\Gamma$ is $k$-homogeneous; that is, every isomorphism between induced subgraphs of $\Gamma$ on $k$ vertices extends to an automorphism of $\Gamma$.

choices.label: B
choices.text: $\Gamma$ is $(k-1)$-homogeneous, and whenever two induced subgraphs of $\Gamma$ on $k$ vertices are isomorphic, at least one isomorphism between them extends to an automorphism of $\Gamma$; that is, every isomorphism between induced subgraphs on at most $k-1$ vertices extends, and for subgraphs on $k$ vertices existence of one extending isomorphism suffices.

choices.label: C
choices.text: $\Gamma$ is $(k-1)$-set-homogeneous; that is, whenever two induced subgraphs of $\Gamma$ on at most $k-1$ vertices are isomorphic, some automorphism of $\Gamma$ maps the vertex set of one onto the vertex set of the other.

choices.label: D
choices.text: $\Gamma$ has the extension property only for automorphisms of a single $k$-vertex induced subgraph; that is, for every induced subgraph $\Delta$ of $\Gamma$ on $k$ vertices, every automorphism of $\Delta$ extends to an automorphism of $\Gamma$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: $\Gamma$ is $k$-homogeneous with “induced” omitted; that is, every isomorphism between any pair of isomorphic subgraphs of $\Gamma$ on $k$ vertices, whether or not they are induced, extends to an automorphism of $\Gamma$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: $\Gamma$ is $k$-homogeneous; that is, every isomorphism between induced subgraphs of $\Gamma$ on $k$ vertices extends to an automorphism of $\Gamma$.

theorem:
\label{main}

Every countable (recursively presented) group embeds into a (finitely presented) group $\Gamma$ with property $\mathrm{(T)}$, that is Frobenius stable but does not have property $\mathrm{(T_2)}$. In fact, $\operatorname{H}^2(\Gamma; V) \neq 0$ for \emph{every} unitary $\Gamma$-representation $V$.
sketch:
To prove \cref{main}, one uses that “every asymptotic homomorphism is close to one that factors through an approximable quotient … Therefore groups with no non-trivial approximable quotients are (vacuously) stable. \cref{main} exploits this observation.”
