correct_choice.text: The set of \((\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)\)-approximable points on \(\mathcal M\) has full measure (with respect to the induced Lebesgue measure on \(\mathcal M\)) if \(\sum_{q=1}^\infty \psi_1(q)\cdots \psi_n(q)\) diverges, and has measure zero if that series converges.

theorem:
\label{Hao_Kodaira_codim_1}\\cite[][Thm. 1.3]{Hao_Nowhere_Vanishing_Holomorphic_One-Forms_on_Varieties_of_Kodaira_Codimension_One}
    Let $X$ be a smooth projective variety of Kodaira dimension $\kappa(X)=\dim X-1$. If $X$ has a nowhere vanishing holomorphic 1-form, then for any minimal model $X^{min}$ of $X$ there exists a finite quasi-\'etale covering $X'\rightarrow X^{min}$ such that any $\mathbb{Q}$-factorialization $X''$ of $X'$ is a product $B\times E$, where $B$ is a minimal model of general type and $E$ is an elliptic curve.,
sketch:
In the projective case, Hao’s argument is summarized as follows: take a good minimal model $X^{\min}$ and its Iitaka fibration $f\colon X^{\min}\to Y$. By Popa–Schnell, “Kodaira dimension and zeros of holomorphic 1-forms” (Thm. 2.1), the nowhere-vanishing $1$-form $\omega$ “cannot come from $Y$”. Hence “the general fiber of $f$ is not contracted by the Albanese morphism”, so it maps to “a translate of a fixed elliptic curve $E\subset \operatorname{Alb}(X)$”. Since $\operatorname{Alb}(X)$ is projective, one “dualize[s] the inclusion $E\subset \operatorname{Alb}(X)$ to get a morphism” $\varphi\colon X^{\min}\to \operatorname{Alb}(X)\to E$, and then “$B$ … as well as the splitting $X''\cong B\times E$ … are then constructed from a general fiber of $\varphi\colon X^{\min}\to E$.

For the K\"ahler generalization (Theorem~\ref{main_result}), two obstacles are noted: one cannot apply Popa–Schnell, and $\operatorname{Alb}(X)$ is not projective so “the morphism $\varphi$ does not exists in general.” To overcome this, the authors “invoke Lin’s construction of an algebraic approximation via tautological models and families” to produce a deformation $\pi\colon \mathcal{X}\to V$ of $X$ and a $G$-equivariant tautological family of elliptic fibrations $\Pi\colon \mathcal{T}\to Y\times V\to V$, with $\Pi^{-1}(0)/G\to Y/G$ bimeromorphic to the Iitaka fibration of $X$, and with $\mathcal{X}\to V$ having a dense set of projective fibers; moreover $\mathcal{X}$ and $\mathcal{T}/G$ are “bimeromorphic over $V$” and the elliptic fibrations $p_v\colon \Pi^{-1}(v)\to Y$ are “locally isomorphic over $Y$” (independent of $v$ on an open cover).

They then “show that a small deformation of $X$ also admits a holomorphic 1-form without zeros”, so there exists $v_0\in V$ with $\mathcal{X}_{v_0}$ projective and carrying such a form. Since the Iitaka fibration of $\mathcal{X}_{v_0}$ is birational to $p_{v_0}/G$ and the $p_v$ are locally isomorphic, they “conclude that the general fiber of the Iitaka fibration of any $\mathcal{X}_v$ is isomorphic to a fixed elliptic curve,” addressing the first obstacle. “To finish the argument,” they “show that the elliptic fibration $p_0\colon \Pi^{-1}(0)\to Y$ is locally trivial” (locally a product), then “carefully describe the $G$-action,” and “taking a carefully chosen subquotient, yields the desired elliptic fiber bundle $Z\to B$” with trivial monodromy.
question: Let $X$ be a smooth projective variety over $\mathbb{C}$ with Kodaira dimension $\kappa(X)=\dim X-1$, and suppose that $X$ admits a nowhere vanishing holomorphic $1$-form. For a minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$, a finite quasi-\'etale cover means a finite morphism $X'\to X^{\min}$ that is \'etale in codimension one, and a $\mathbb{Q}$-factorialization of $X'$ means a small birational model $X''\to X'$ with $X''$ $\mathbb{Q}$-factorial. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the structure of a minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$ under these hypotheses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For any minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$ there exists a finite quasi-\'etale covering $X'\to X^{\min}$ such that $X'$ itself is isomorphic to a product $B\times E$, where $B$ is a minimal model of general type and $E$ is an elliptic curve.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For any minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$ there exists a finite quasi-\'etale covering $X'\to X^{\min}$ and a $\mathbb{Q}$-factorialization $X''$ of $X'$ together with an isomorphism $X''\cong B\times E$, where $B$ is a projective variety of general type and $E$ is an elliptic curve.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For any minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$ and any finite quasi-\'etale covering $X'\to X^{\min}$, every $\mathbb{Q}$-factorialization $X''$ of $X'$ is isomorphic to a product $B\times E$, where $B$ is a minimal model of general type and $E$ is an elliptic curve.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For any minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$ there exists a finite \emph{\'etale} covering $X'\to X^{\min}$ such that any $\mathbb{Q}$-factorialization $X''$ of $X'$ is isomorphic to a product $B\times E$, where $B$ is a minimal model of general type and $E$ is an elliptic curve.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{thm:intro}
Suppose that $p$ is a prime number, and that $X^{\alpha}$ and $X^{\beta}$ are translation invariant $\mathbf{Z}/\ell$-covers of the Paley graph $X(\bF_p)$ for a prime $\ell \neq p$.  If the adjacency spectra of $X^\alpha$ and $X^\beta$ are equal as multisets, then there is an isomorphism of graphs $X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}$.
sketch:
To prove Theorem~\ref{thm:intro}, the introduction says they (1) “first record basic facts about the Paley graphs in \S\ref{sec:paley_setup},” and then (2) “use character theory to show that the eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix of a covering graph $X^{\alpha}$ are given by the exponential sums
\[
\theta^{\alpha}_{a, k} = \sum_{s \in \bFsquares} \zeta_p^{\tr(as)}\zeta_{\ell}^{k\alpha(s)} \qquad \text{for $a \in \bFq, k \in \mathbf{Z}/\ell$,}
\]
where $\zeta_p=e^{2\pi i/p}$, $\zeta_{\ell}=e^{2\pi i/\ell}$, and $\tr\colon \bF_q^{+}\to\bF_p^{+}$ is the trace homomorphism.” It further notes that “When $k=0$, the eigenvalues $\theta^{\alpha}_{a,0}$ are the adjacency eigenvalues of the Paley graph $X(\bF_q)$, and can be evaluated explicitly using the quadratic Gauss sum.” The introduction concludes that “The eigenvalue calculations and the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:intro} take up \S\ref{sec:covers}.”
question: Fix primes \(p\) and \(\ell\) with \(p \equiv 1 \pmod 4\) and \(\ell \neq p\). Let \(X(\mathbf F_p)\) be the Paley graph on \(\mathbf F_p\) (with vertices \(\mathbf F_p\), and \(x\) adjacent to \(y\) when \(x-y\) is a nonzero square in \(\mathbf F_p\)). Consider connected graph covers of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\) with deck group \(\mathbf Z/\ell\) whose defining arc-labeling is invariant under the translation action of the additive group of \(\mathbf F_p\); these are called translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers. Which statement correctly describes the role of the adjacency spectrum in classifying such covers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: For any two translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), if their adjacency spectra are equal as multisets, then \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) as graphs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For any two translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), if their adjacency spectra are equal as multisets, then there is an isomorphism \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) that commutes with the projections to \(X(\mathbf F_p)\) and with the \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-deck actions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For any two translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), if \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) as graphs, then their adjacency spectra are equal as multisets.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For any two connected \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), even without assuming translation invariance of the defining arc-labeling, equality of adjacency spectra as multisets implies \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) as graphs.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The same spectral rigidity statement remains valid with \(\mathbf F_p\) replaced by \(\mathbf F_q\) for every finite field \(\mathbf F_q\) with \(q \equiv 1 \pmod 4\): for any prime \(\ell \neq \operatorname{char}(\mathbf F_q)\), equal adjacency spectra of translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers of \(X(\mathbf F_q)\) imply graph isomorphism.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For any two translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), if their adjacency spectra are equal as multisets, then \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) as graphs.

sketch: Our proof of the main theorem is described as follows: it is “based on our recent paper with Fu and Sun~\cite{cfsx}, from which we can define a layer structure for the cut points of $B[0,\tau_\mathbbm{D}]$ and then solve it explicitly; see Section~\ref{section-backbone-1}.” The approach “heavily relies on the connection between planar Brownian motion and $\SLE_{8/3}$, and especially their coupling with Liouville quantum gravity (LQG).” In terms of organization: Next we review an “explicit relation between the laws of conformal radii and moduli for $\SLE_{8/3}$-type loops”; we then define the layer structure and “solve[s] it explicitly, based on the results of~\cite{cfsx} and the preceding review”; finally “we finish the proof of the main theorem later.”
question: Let \((B_t)_{t\ge 0}\) be a planar Brownian motion started at \(0\), let \(\mathbb D=\{z\in\mathbb C:|z|<1\}\) and \(\mathbb S^1=\{z\in\mathbb C:|z|=1\}\), and let \(\tau_{\mathbb D}\) be the first hitting time of \(\mathbb S^1\). For \(\varepsilon\in(0,1/10)\), let \(\mathcal B_\varepsilon\) be the event that there exist two disjoint subpaths of the trajectory \(B[0,\tau_{\mathbb D}]\) joining \(\varepsilon\mathbb S^1\) and \(\tfrac12\mathbb S^1\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the probabilities \(\mathbb P[\mathcal B_\varepsilon]\) uniformly for all \(\varepsilon\in(0,1/10)\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
There exist constants \(C_1,C_2>0\) such that for every \(\varepsilon\in(0,1/10)\),
\[
\frac{C_1}{\log|\log\varepsilon|}\le \mathbb P[\mathcal B_\varepsilon]\le \frac{C_2}{\log|\log\varepsilon|}.
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exist constants \(C_1,C_2>0\) such that for every \(\varepsilon\in(0,1/10)\),
\[
\frac{C_1}{(\log|\log\varepsilon|)^{1/2}}\le \mathbb P[\mathcal B_\varepsilon]\le \frac{C_2}{\log|\log\varepsilon|}.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists a constant \(C_2>0\) such that for every \(\varepsilon\in(0,1/10)\),
\[
\mathbb P[\mathcal B_\varepsilon]\le \frac{C_2}{\log|\log\varepsilon|}.
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exist constants \(C_1,C_2>0\) such that for every \(\varepsilon\in(0,1/10)\),
\[
\frac{C_1}{\log|\log\varepsilon|}\le \mathbb P[\mathcal B_\varepsilon]\le \frac{C_2}{(\log|\log\varepsilon|)^{3/2}}.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exist constants \(C_1,C_2>0\) such that for every \(\varepsilon\in(0,1/10)\),
\[
\frac{C_1}{|\log\varepsilon|}\le \mathbb P[\mathcal B_\varepsilon]\le \frac{C_2}{|\log\varepsilon|}.
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
There exist constants \(C_1,C_2>0\) such that for every \(\varepsilon\in(0,1/10)\),
\[
\frac{C_1}{\log|\log\varepsilon|}\le \mathbb P[\mathcal B_\varepsilon]\le \frac{C_2}{\log|\log\varepsilon|}.
\]

question:
Let $X$ be a smooth projective complex variety, let $H$ be an ample divisor on $X$, and let $E$ be a torsion-free $H$-slope-semistable sheaf on $X$. Define the discriminant of $E$ by
\[
\Delta(E)\coloneqq \ch_1(E)^2-2\ch_0(E)\ch_2(E).
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about $\Delta(E)\cdot H^{\dim(X)-2}$ for every such sheaf $E$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(\Delta(E)\cdot H^{\dim(X)-2}>0\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: \(\Delta(E)\cdot H^{\dim(X)-2}\ge -\,\ch_0(E)^2\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: If \(E\) is \(H\)-slope-stable, then \(\Delta(E)\cdot H^{\dim(X)-2}=0\) can occur only when \(\ch_0(E)=1\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: If \(\ch_0(E)=1\), then \(\Delta(E)\cdot H^{\dim(X)-2}=0\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question:
Let \(n\ge 3\) and \(\Omega=B_R(0)\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) for some \(R>0\), where \(B_r(0)\) denotes the ball of radius \(r\) centered at the origin. Let \(p,q\in\mathbb{R}\) satisfy
\[
p<\frac{n-2}{n-1},\qquad q<\frac{n-2}{n-1},
\]
and define
\[
f(s)=(1+s)^{-p/2},\qquad g(s)=(1+s)^{-q/2}.
\]
Assume the initial data \(u_0,w_0\in W^{1,\infty}(\overline\Omega)\) are positive and radially symmetric. Consider the Neumann problem
\[
\begin{cases}
u_t = \Delta u-\nabla\!\cdot\!\big(u f(|\nabla v|^2)\nabla v\big), & x\in\Omega,\ t>0,\\
0=\Delta v-\mu_w+w,\quad \mu_w(t)=|\Omega|^{-1}\int_\Omega w(x,t)\,dx, & x\in\Omega,\ t>0,\\
w_t = \Delta w-\nabla\!\cdot\!\big(w g(|\nabla z|^2)\nabla z\big), & x\in\Omega,\ t>0,\\
0=\Delta z-\mu_u+u,\quad \mu_u(t)=|\Omega|^{-1}\int_\Omega u(x,t)\,dx, & x\in\Omega,\ t>0,\\
\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial \nu}=\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial \nu}=\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial \nu}=\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial \nu}=0, & x\in\partial\Omega,\ t>0,\\
u(x,0)=u_0(x),\quad w(x,0)=w_0(x), & x\in\Omega,
\end{cases}
\]
where \(\nu\) on \(\partial\Omega\) denotes the outward unit normal. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence behavior of solutions to this system under these assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
There exist functions \(M_1,M_2\in C^0([0,R])\) such that, if the positive radially symmetric initial data additionally satisfy
\[
\int_{B_r(0)}u_0\,dx\ge M_1(r),\qquad \int_{B_r(0)}w_0\,dx\ge M_2(r)
\quad\text{for every } r\in(0,R),
\]
then the corresponding solution of the above system blows up in finite time.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exist constants \(m_1,m_2>0\) such that, if the positive radially symmetric initial data additionally satisfy
\[
\int_{\Omega}u_0\,dx\ge m_1,
\qquad
\int_{\Omega}w_0\,dx\ge m_2,
\]
then the corresponding solution of the above system blows up in finite time.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exist functions \(M_1,M_2\in C^0([0,R])\) such that, if the positive radially symmetric initial data additionally satisfy
\[
\int_{B_r(0)}u_0\,dx\ge M_1(r),\qquad \int_{B_r(0)}w_0\,dx\ge M_2(r)
\quad\text{for every } r\in(0,R),
\]
then the corresponding solution of the above system cannot remain globally bounded for all times.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exist functions \(M_1,M_2\in C^0([0,R])\) such that, if the positive radially symmetric initial data additionally satisfy that for every \(r\in(0,R)\), at least one of the two inequalities
\[
\int_{B_r(0)}u_0\,dx\ge M_1(r),
\qquad
\int_{B_r(0)}w_0\,dx\ge M_2(r)
\]
holds, then the corresponding solution of the above system blows up in finite time.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exist functions \(M_1,M_2\in C^0([0,R])\) such that, if the positive radially symmetric initial data additionally satisfy
\[
\int_{B_r(0)}u_0\,dx\ge M_1(r),\qquad \int_{B_r(0)}w_0\,dx\ge M_2(r)
\quad\text{for every } r\in(0,R),
\]
then the corresponding solution of the above system exists globally for all \(t>0\), but
\[
\sup_{t>0}\Big(\|u(\cdot,t)\|_{L^\infty(\Omega)}+\|w(\cdot,t)\|_{L^\infty(\Omega)}\Big)=\infty.
\]
