\quad\text{for every } r\in(0,R),
\]
then the corresponding solution of the above system blows up in finite time.

theorem:
For almost all irrational $\theta\in [0,1]$, if we denote by $q_k/p_k$ the sequence of convergents of the continued fraction expansion of $\theta$, then one has
$$\lim_{k\to \infty}\frac{\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)}{p_k^2}=\frac{16}{\pi^3}\sum_{n\ge 1 \textrm{ odd}}\frac{1}{n^3\sin(n\pi\theta)}.$$
question:
Let $\theta\in[0,1]$ be an irrational number, and let $q_k/p_k$ denote the $k$-th convergent in the continued fraction expansion of $\theta$. Here, for a rational number $q/p\in(0,1)$, define
$$\sigma_2\!\left(\frac{q}{p}\right)=\sum_{(j,k,\ell)\in \Delta_p} (-1)^{\lfloor jq/p\rfloor+\lfloor kq/p\rfloor+\lfloor \ell q/p\rfloor},$$
where
$$\Delta_p=\left\{(j,k,\ell)\in\mathbb Z^3:\; 0<j,k,\ell<p,\; j<k+\ell,\; k<j+\ell,\; \ell<j+k,\; j+k+\ell<2p,\; j+k+\ell\text{ odd}\right\}.$$ What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the limit of the normalized quantity $\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)/p_k^2$ as $k\to\infty$ for Lebesgue-almost every such irrational $\theta$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: $\displaystyle \lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)}{p_k^2}=\frac{16}{\pi^3}\sum_{n\ge 1}\frac{1}{n^3\sin(n\pi\theta)}.$

choices.label: C
choices.text: $\displaystyle \frac{\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)}{p_k^2}$ converges to a finite real limit for Lebesgue-almost every irrational $\theta\in[0,1]$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: $\displaystyle \lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)}{p_k^2}=\frac{16}{\pi^3}\sum_{n\ge 1\ \mathrm{odd}}\frac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n^3\sin(n\pi\theta)}.$

choices.label: E
choices.text: $\displaystyle \lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{\sigma_2(q_k/p_k)}{p_k^2}=\frac{16}{\pi^3}\sum_{n\ge 1\ \mathrm{odd}}\frac{1}{n^3\sin(n\pi\theta)}\quad\text{for every irrational }\theta\in[0,1].$

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

sketch:
The post-theorem discussion indicates that the proof of the main theorem is obtained by transferring pure homological dimension across functors that preserve the pure structure. Concretely:
\begin{itemize}
\item One interprets purity in a compactly generated triangulated category $\mathsf{T}$ via the restricted Yoneda embedding $\msf{y}$: it sends triangles to long exact sequences, and a triangle goes to a short exact sequence precisely when the connecting morphism is a phantom map; these are the \emph{pure triangles}. Thus “purity can be thought of as the study of phantom maps.”
\item The embedding $\msf{y}$ “establishes an equivalence between the pure structure on $\mathsf{T}$ and the pure structure on $\Flat{\mathsf{T}^{\mrm{c}}}$,” where there are enough pure injectives/projectives. One therefore defines pure projective/injective dimensions and the pure global dimension on $\Flat{\mathsf{T}^{\mrm{c}}}$, and “the pure global dimension of $\mathsf{T}$ is defined to be the pure global dimension of $\Flat{\mathsf{T}^{\mrm{c}}}$."
\item The “main tool used to prove the main theorem” is to analyze how pure homological dimension transfers along \emph{definable} (interpretation) functors, i.e. functors preserving coproducts, products, and pure triangles. Any such $F\colon\mathsf{T}\to\mathsf{U}$ induces a functor $\widehat{F}\colon\Flat{\mathsf{T}^{\mrm{c}}}\to\Flat{\mathsf{U}^{\mrm{c}}}$ compatible with $\msf{y}$, so it suffices to work in this accessible setting.
\item The transfer is implemented via the following theorem.

\begin{thmx}\label{intro:thmb}(\ref{prop:pextisos})
Let $F\colon\A\to\B$ be a definable functor between finitely accessible categories with products, and suppose that $F$ admits a left adjoint $\Lambda\colon\B\to\A$. Then there are isomorphisms
\[
\mrm{PExt}_{\msf{B}}^{n}(B,F(A))\simeq \mrm{PExt}_{\A}^{n}(\Lambda(B),A)
\]
for all $A\in\A, B\in\B$ and $n\geq 0$.
\end{thmx}

In particular, for a definable $F\colon\mathsf{A}\to\mathsf{B}$ with left adjoint $\Lambda$, there are isomorphisms
\[\mathrm{PExt}_{\mathsf{B}}^{n}(B,F(A))\simeq \mathrm{PExt}_{\mathsf{A}}^{n}(\Lambda(B),A)\]
for all $n\ge 0$, where $\mathrm{PExt}$ is computed using pure projective/pure injective resolutions and “measure[s] the pure homological dimension of objects.”
\item “In relation to the main theorem, it is through the above isomorphisms applied to particular choices of definable functors between $\msf{Mod}(R)$, $\mathsf{D}(R)$, and $\mathsf{D}(S)$ that one obtains the result.”
\end{itemize}
This is the only proof-outline content given for the main theorem in the provided post-theorem text.
question: Let $R$ be a ring, let $\mathsf D(R)$ denote the derived category of $R$-modules, and write $\mathsf{pgdim}(-)$ for projective generating dimension. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the inequality between $\mathsf{pgdim}(\mathsf D(R))$ and $\mathsf{pgdim}(R)$ for every ring $R$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: For every ring $R$, one has $\mathsf{pgdim}(\mathsf D(R)) \geq \mathsf{pgdim}(R)$.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every ring $R$, one has $\mathsf{pgdim}(\mathsf D(R)) \leq \mathsf{pgdim}(R)$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every ring $R$ with $\mathsf{pgdim}(R)\geq 2$, one has $\mathsf{pgdim}(\mathsf D(R))\geq 2$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every ring $R$ with $\mathsf{pgdim}(R)\geq 2$, one has $\mathsf{pgdim}(\mathsf D(R)) \leq \mathsf{pgdim}(R)-1$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every ring $R$ with $\mathsf{pgdim}(R)\geq 2$, one has $1\leq \mathsf{pgdim}(\mathsf D(R)) \leq \mathsf{pgdim}(R)-1$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For every ring $R$, one has $\mathsf{pgdim}(\mathsf D(R)) \geq \mathsf{pgdim}(R)$.

theorem:
For every pair of fixed integers $r,\ell\geqslant 3$, consider the random greedy high girth $r$-clique
removal process on vertex set $[n]$. Let $M$  be the number of edges in the
generated linear $r$-graph with girth larger than $\ell$ when the process terminates. 
With high probability, there exists some positive constant $\lambda=\lambda(\ell)$  such that
\begin{align*}
M\geqslant n^{1+ \frac{1}{\ell-1}-\lambda \frac{\log\log n}{\log n}}.
\end{align*}
sketch: We define the random greedy high girth $r$-clique removal process (Section 3) and introduce “some key random variables of the process that we wish to track,” then estimate “the corresponding expected trajectory” and “choos[e] error function for each tracked variable.” Next, we “formally prove the concentration of all these variables” (Section 4) by analyzing “the required one-step changes, trend hypotheses and boundedness hypotheses for these tracked variables … in higher uniformities.” Finally, “two important claims to bound the overcounting and boundedness parameters are discussed in detail in the final section.” The proof also notes that the exponent’s term $O({\log\log n}/{\log n})$ is “necessary,” and that “it is impossible to apply this approach to obtain a better lower bound than $\Omega(n^{1+ \frac{1}{\ell-1}})$.”
question: Fix integers $r,\ell\ge 3$. Run the random greedy high-girth $r$-clique removal process on the vertex set $[n]=\{1,\dots,n\}$: successively choose $r$-cliques at random so that the chosen cliques, viewed as the edges of an $r$-uniform hypergraph on $[n]$, form a linear $r$-graph (that is, any two distinct chosen $r$-cliques share at most one vertex) of girth greater than $\ell$, and stop when no further choice is possible. Let $M$ denote the number of chosen $r$-cliques, equivalently the number of edges in the resulting linear $r$-graph, when the process terminates. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic lower bound for $M$ as $n\to\infty$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every fixed positive constant $\lambda$, with high probability,
\[
M\ge n^{\,1+\frac{1}{\ell-1}-\lambda\frac{\log\log n}{\log n}}.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
With high probability,
\[
M\ge n^{\,1+\frac{1}{\ell-1}-o(1)}.
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exists a positive constant $\lambda=\lambda(\ell)$ such that, with high probability,
\[
M\ge n^{\,1+\frac{1}{\ell-1}-\lambda\frac{1}{\log n}}.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists a positive constant $\lambda=\lambda(\ell)$ such that, with high probability,
\[
M\ge n^{\,1+\frac{1}{\ell-1}-\lambda\frac{\log\log\log n}{\log n}}.
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

sketch: Key in both arguments is the Ahlswede-Daykin four function theorem (adapted to Euclidean setting in the context of the main conjecture). The authors "sketch the easy fact that the classical Ahlswede-Daykin four function theorem, stated for a finite lattice, implies the Euclidean version ... that we utilize in the proof of the main conjecture," and they also investigate "a Transport based proof for the result in $\mathbb{R}^d$" ("drawing on \cite{GRST21}"). More broadly, they confirm the main conjecture by proving a more general statement: convolution with $g$ preserves log-supermodularity when $g$ is "a log-concave product density," and they "prove that a discrete analog holds for the integer lattice as well."
question:
Let
\[
g(x)=(2\pi)^{-d/2}e^{-|x|^2/2},\qquad x\in\mathbb{R}^d,
\]
be the standard Gaussian density on \(\mathbb{R}^d\). Let \(f\in L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)\) be log-supermodular, meaning that for all \(x,y\in\mathbb{R}^d\),
\[
f(x\vee y)f(x\wedge y)\ge f(x)f(y),
\]
where \((x\vee y)_i=\max\{x_i,y_i\}\) and \((x\wedge y)_i=\min\{x_i,y_i\}\). Define the convolution
\[
(f*g)(x)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^d} f(x-t)g(t)\,dt.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the log-supermodularity of \(f*g\) under these hypotheses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The convolution \(f*g\) is log-supermodular for smooth positive log-supermodular \(f\), but this need not persist for arbitrary \(L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)\) log-supermodular \(f\); the \(L_1\) hypothesis alone is too weak.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The convolution \(f*g\) is log-supermodular whenever \(f\in L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)\) is log-supermodular.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The Gaussian kernel is genuinely exceptional: there exists a log-concave product density \(h(x)=\prod_{i=1}^d h_i(x_i)\) and a log-supermodular \(f\in L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)\) for which \(f*h\) is not log-supermodular.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Among log-concave product densities, the property that \(f*h\) is log-supermodular for every log-supermodular \(f\in L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)\) characterizes the Gaussian kernel, up to translation and diagonal rescaling.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{thm : thm1 intro}
      For each positive (or negative simple) root $\beta$, there exists a unique (up to isomorphism) strongly $\mathcal{H}_Q^{(1)}$-exact complex $C_{\bullet}[\beta]$ in $\mathcal{K}_Q^{(1)}$ such that $C_n[\beta]=0$ if $n<0$ and $C_0[\beta]$ is indecomposable and isomorphic to $Y[\beta]$.
question: Let \(Q\) be a quiver, let \(\mathcal H_Q^{(1)}\) be the associated additive monoidal category, and let \(\mathcal K_Q^{(1)}\) denote its bounded homotopy category. Fix a root \(\beta\) that is either positive or a negative simple root, and let \(Y[\beta]\) be the associated indecomposable object of \(\mathcal H_Q^{(1)}\). In \(\mathcal K_Q^{(1)}\), which object is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by \(\beta\) among the strongly \(\mathcal H_Q^{(1)}\)-exact complexes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A unique (up to isomorphism) strongly \(\mathcal{H}_Q^{(1)}\)-exact complex \(C_{\bullet}[\beta]\in\mathcal{K}_Q^{(1)}\) such that \(C_n[\beta]=0\) for all \(n<1\) and \(C_1[\beta]\) is indecomposable with \(C_1[\beta]\cong Y[\beta]\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: A strongly \(\mathcal{H}_Q^{(1)}\)-exact complex \(C_{\bullet}[\beta]\in\mathcal{K}_Q^{(1)}\) such that \(C_n[\beta]=0\) for all \(n<0\) and \(C_0[\beta]\) is indecomposable with \(C_0[\beta]\cong Y[\beta]\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: A unique (up to isomorphism) \(\mathcal{H}_Q^{(1)}\)-exact complex \(C_{\bullet}[\beta]\in\mathcal{K}_Q^{(1)}\) such that \(C_n[\beta]=0\) for all \(n<0\) and \(C_0[\beta]\) is indecomposable with \(C_0[\beta]\cong Y[\beta]\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: A unique (up to isomorphism) strongly \(\mathcal{H}_Q^{(1)}\)-exact complex \(C_{\bullet}[\beta]\in\mathcal{K}_Q^{(1)}\) such that \(C_n[\beta]=0\) for all \(n<0\), \(C_0[\beta]\) is indecomposable with \(C_0[\beta]\cong Y[\beta]\), and each differential \(d_n\) admits a weak cokernel in \(\mathcal H_Q^{(1)}\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

sketch:
To prove the main theorem, the paper “builds upon the approach of Gao et al.” by finding “a collection of $k$ admissible paths between any two given vertices $x,y$ in a suitable 2-connected graph $G$.” The “key step is to identify a specified subgraph $H$ (called the {\it core} subgraph)” so that for some $t>0$: (a) “$H$ contains $t$ admissible paths between many pairs of vertices” (a “robust spreading property”); and (b) after “contracting or deleting $V(H)$,” the remaining graph “satisfies suitable minimum degree conditions and yields $k+1-t$ admissible paths between prescribed pairs of vertices.” “By concatenating these paths appropriately, one obtains the desired $k$ admissible paths in $G$.”

A main new tool is that, unlike earlier works where core subgraphs were “typically dense structures” (complete or complete bipartite, or close to them), this work introduces “two new families of core graphs that can be very sparse … while still satisfying properties (a) and (b),” called “\textit{trigonal graphs} and \textit{tetragonal graphs},” which “handle the non-bipartite and bipartite cases, respectively.” Because of their sparsity, “these core subgraphs can be found in graphs with lower connectivity, providing the crucial ingredient” for removing connectivity assumptions.

Organizationally, the proof is reduced later by introducing “\textit{$k$-weak} graphs” and reducing the proof of the main theorem to this class; specifically, we use the following theorem.

\begin{thm}\label{thm:weak graph}
Let $k\geq 6$ be an integer. If $G$ is a $k$-weak graph not isomorphic to any graph in $\{K_{k+1}, K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal{H}_{k}$, then $G$ contains a cycle of length $r\pmod k$ for every even integer $r$.
\end{thm}

The proof is then “split between” the subsequent parts, according to whether the host graph is non-bipartite or bipartite.
question: Let \(k\ge 6\) be an integer and let \(G\) be a graph with minimum degree at least \(k\). Here, a cycle of length \(r \pmod k\) means a cycle whose length is congruent to \(r\) modulo \(k\); \(K_{k+1}\) is the complete graph on \(k+1\) vertices; \(K_{k,k}\) is the complete bipartite graph with both parts of size \(k\); an end-block of \(G\) is a block containing at most one cut-vertex of \(G\), and a non-end-block is any block of \(G\) that is not an end-block; and \(\mathcal H_k\) denotes the exceptional graph family \(\mathcal H_k\). Which description exactly classifies the graphs \(G\) that do not contain a cycle of length \(r \pmod k\) for every even integer \(r\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Precisely those graphs for which either \(k\) is odd and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to a graph in \(\{K_{k+1},K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal H_k\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(2 \pmod k\); or \(k\) is even and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to \(K_{k+1}\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(2 \pmod k\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Precisely those graphs for which either \(k\) is odd and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to a graph in \(\{K_{k+1},K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal H_k\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(0 \pmod k\); or \(k\) is even and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to \(K_{k+1}\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(0 \pmod k\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any such graph must satisfy one of the following: either \(k\) is odd and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to a graph in \(\{K_{k+1},K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal H_k\); or \(k\) is even and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to \(K_{k+1}\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Precisely those graphs for which either \(k\) is odd and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to a graph in \(\{K_{k+1},K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal H_k\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle whose length is congruent to any even residue modulo \(k\); or \(k\) is even and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to \(K_{k+1}\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle whose length is congruent to any even residue modulo \(k\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: Precisely those graphs for which, whether \(k\) is odd or even, every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to a graph in \(\{K_{k+1},K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal H_k\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(2 \pmod k\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: Precisely those graphs for which either \(k\) is odd and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to a graph in \(\{K_{k+1},K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal H_k\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(2 \pmod k\); or \(k\) is even and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to \(K_{k+1}\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(2 \pmod k\).

theorem:
\label{thm:three}
A wide Young diagram with three distinct row lengths is Latin.,
question: Let \(\lambda\) be a Young diagram, meaning a left-justified array of boxes with weakly decreasing row lengths, and suppose the set of its row lengths has exactly three distinct values. Call \(\lambda\) wide if for every subdiagram \(\mu\) obtained by deleting some rows, the partition of row lengths of \(\mu\) dominates its conjugate partition \(\mu^t\) (the partition whose parts are the column lengths of \(\mu\)); equivalently, for each \(k\), the total number of boxes in the first \(k\) rows of \(\mu\) is at least the total number of boxes in the first \(k\) columns of \(\mu\). Call \(\lambda\) Latin if its boxes can be filled with positive integers so that each row of length \(r\) contains each of \(1,2,\dots,r\) exactly once, and no column contains a repeated entry. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about every such \(\lambda\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every wide Young diagram with at most three distinct row lengths admits a unique Latin filling.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every wide Young diagram with exactly three distinct row lengths admits a filling of its boxes with positive integers such that no column contains a repeated entry.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every Young diagram with exactly three distinct row lengths that dominates its conjugate partition is Latin.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Every wide Young diagram with exactly three distinct row lengths admits an allocation, and hence is Latin.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

sketch:
Our proof strategy can be summarized as follows. We first describe a sequence $T_n$ of simplicial $2$-complexes whose fundamental groups have property (T), and so that as $n$ tends to infinity, the girth of links of vertices in $T_n$ also tends to infinity. For large $n$ we will embed these complexes $\pi_1$-injectively into negatively curved $3$-pseudomanifolds. To embed such a complex, we first delete a regular neighborhood of the vertices, and thicken the resulting hexagon complex to a $3$-dimensional handlebody. Mirroring the boundary of this handlebody appropriately, we obtain a $3$-orbifold with boundary $H_n$.

The boundary of $H_n$ contains a $\pi_1$-injective graph corresponding to the union of links of vertices of $T_n$. The $3$-orbifold $H_n$ is (orbifold) covered by a hyperbolic $3$-manifold $M$ with totally geodesic boundary, which we cone off to obtain our $3$-pseudomanifold. The handlebody $H_n$ lifts to $M$, giving rise to a subset of the cone-off homotopy equivalent to $T_n$.

They then use “quantitative control on various geometric quantities” in suitable finite covers together with “the results from \cite{KM} to construct negatively curved metrics on the cone-offs of such covers.”
question: For a compact hyperbolic 3-manifold with totally geodesic boundary, let its boundary cone-off \(\operatorname{coneoff}(M)\) denote the space obtained by collapsing each boundary component of \(\partial M\) to a cone point. (Here a closed negatively curved 3-pseudomanifold means a compact 3-dimensional pseudomanifold admitting a complete locally \(\mathrm{CAT}(-1)\) metric.) What is the strongest existence statement that can be proved about sequences of such manifolds?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There exists a sequence of compact hyperbolic 3-manifolds with totally geodesic boundary, say \(M_n\), such that for each \(n\), the boundary cone-off \(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n\) is a closed negatively curved 3-pseudomanifold, the group \(\pi_1(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n)\) contains an infinite quasiconvex subgroup with property \((T)\), and both the genera of the components of \(\partial M_n\) and the Euler characteristics of \(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n\) tend to infinity as \(n\to\infty\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists a sequence of compact hyperbolic 3-manifolds with totally geodesic boundary, say \(M_n\), such that for each \(n\), the boundary cone-off \(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n\) is a closed negatively curved 3-manifold, the group \(\pi_1(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n)\) contains an infinite quasiconvex subgroup with property \((T)\), and both the genera of the components of \(\partial M_n\) and the Euler characteristics of \(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n\) tend to infinity as \(n\to\infty\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a sequence of compact hyperbolic 3-manifolds with totally geodesic boundary, say \(M_n\), such that for each \(n\), the boundary cone-off \(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n\) is a closed negatively curved 3-pseudomanifold, the group \(\pi_1(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n)\) contains an infinite quasiconvex subgroup with property \((T)\), and the genera of the components of \(\partial M_n\) tend to infinity as \(n\to\infty\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a sequence of compact hyperbolic 3-manifolds with totally geodesic boundary, say \(M_n\), such that for each \(n\), the boundary cone-off \(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n\) is a closed negatively curved 3-pseudomanifold carrying a complete locally \(\mathrm{CAT}(-1)\) metric whose restriction makes the entire inclusion \(M_n\hookrightarrow \operatorname{coneoff} M_n\) a local isometry, the group \(\pi_1(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n)\) contains an infinite quasiconvex subgroup with property \((T)\), and both the genera of the components of \(\partial M_n\) and the Euler characteristics of \(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n\) tend to infinity as \(n\to\infty\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists a sequence of compact hyperbolic 3-manifolds with totally geodesic boundary, say \(M_n\), such that for each \(n\), the boundary cone-off \(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n\) is a closed negatively curved 3-pseudomanifold, the group \(\pi_1(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n)\) contains an infinite quasiconvex subgroup with property \((T)\), the genera of the components of \(\partial M_n\) tend to infinity as \(n\to\infty\), and \(\chi(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n)=0\) for every \(n\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: There exists a sequence of compact hyperbolic 3-manifolds with totally geodesic boundary, say \(M_n\), such that for each \(n\), the boundary cone-off \(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n\) is a closed negatively curved 3-pseudomanifold, the group \(\pi_1(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n)\) contains an infinite quasiconvex subgroup with property \((T)\), and both the genera of the components of \(\partial M_n\) and the Euler characteristics of \(\operatorname{coneoff} M_n\) tend to infinity as \(n\to\infty\).

question:
Let \(\alpha=\frac{c}{d}\), where \(c,d,s\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) satisfy \(d\ge 2\), \(1\le c,s\le d\), and \(\gcd(cs,d)=1\). Let \(p\ge 5\) be a prime with \(p\equiv s\pmod d\). Since \(d\) is prime to \(p\), we view \(\alpha\) as a \(p\)-adic integer. For a \(p\)-adic integer \(x\), let \(\langle x\rangle_p\) denote the least nonnegative residue of \(x\) modulo \(p\), and define Dwork’s dash operation by
\[
x^*=\frac{x+\langle -x\rangle_p}{p}.
\]
Recursively, set \(x^{\ast_0}=x\) and \(x^{\ast_n}=(x^{\ast_{n-1}})^*\) for \(n\ge 1\). Assume
\[
\bigl(\tfrac12+\alpha\bigr)^{\ast_r}\bigl(\tfrac12+\alpha^{\ast_r}\bigr)\not\equiv 0\pmod p.
\]
Here \((a)_k\) denotes the Pochhammer symbol and \(H_n^{(2)}=\sum_{j=1}^n j^{-2}\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{p^r-1}(2k+\alpha)\frac{(\alpha)_k^3\left(\tfrac12\right)_k}{(1)_k^3\left(\tfrac12+\alpha\right)_k}
\]
modulo \(p^{r+3}\) under these hypotheses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
C
D
E

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{th:main}
\[
N^{1/3} \log N \ll f_0(I(H_N)) \ll N^{1/3} \log N.
\],
sketch: The post-theorem introduction indicates how the proof of Theorem~\ref{th:main} will proceed only at a high level: the authors note that for the disk case the upper bound “follows from Andrews theorem (Theorem~\ref{th:Andr} below)”, while “the lower bound is more difficult and the method of its proof will be used here with suitable modifications.” They then explain an initial structural step for the hyperbola case: “we observe that $I(H_N)$ has few, about $2N^{1/3}$ vertices outside a square $[1,N_2]^2$, where $N_2$ is about $N^{2/3}$, because we can actually list these vertices,” namely $(x,y)=(k,\lceil N/k\rceil)$ and by symmetry $(\lceil N/k\rceil,k)$ for $1\le k\le N^{1/3}$. They justify these are vertices via an inductive condition and an inequality that “follows if $k\le N^{1/3}$,” and record that with $N_1=\lfloor N^{1/3}\rfloor$ and $N_2=\lceil N/N_1\rceil$ the points $(N_1,N_2)$ and $(N_2,N_1)$ are vertices. Finally, they reformulate Theorem~\ref{th:main} as the equivalent statement for $Q_N=H_N\cap[1,N_2]^2$ (Theorem~\ref{th:main2}), indicating the argument reduces to bounding $f_0(I(Q_N))$. They caution that “the case of hyperbola is more involved because the curvature changes from $N^{-1/2}$ to $N^{-2}$ while it is constant for the disk.”
question:
Let \(N\) be a positive integer, and define
\[
H_N=\{(x,y)\in \mathbb{R}^2: x,y>0,\ xy\ge N\}.
\]
Let
\[
I(H_N)=\operatorname{conv}(\mathbb{Z}^2\cap H_N)
\]
be the integer hull of \(H_N\), and let \(f_0(I(H_N))\) denote the number of vertices of \(I(H_N)\). Interpreting Vinogradov notation \(A \ll B\) to mean that \(A\) is bounded above by a constant multiple of \(B\) for all sufficiently large \(N\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic behavior of \(f_0(I(H_N))\) as \(N \to \infty\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: \(f_0(I(H_N)) \asymp N^{1/3}\log N\); equivalently, there exist constants \(c, C > 0\), independent of \(N\), such that for all sufficiently large \(N\), \(c\,N^{1/3}\log N \le f_0(I(H_N)) \le C\,N^{1/3}\log N\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(f_0(I(H_N)) \asymp N^{1/3}\); equivalently, there exist constants \(c, C > 0\), independent of \(N\), such that for all sufficiently large \(N\), \(c\,N^{1/3} \le f_0(I(H_N)) \le C\,N^{1/3}\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: \(f_0(I(H_N)) \ll N^{1/3}\log N\); equivalently, there exists a constant \(C>0\), independent of \(N\), such that for all sufficiently large \(N\), \(f_0(I(H_N))\le C\,N^{1/3}\log N\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(f_0(I(H_N)) \asymp N^{1/3}\log N\log\log N\); equivalently, there exist constants \(c, C > 0\), independent of \(N\), such that for all sufficiently large \(N\), \(c\,N^{1/3}\log N\log\log N \le f_0(I(H_N)) \le C\,N^{1/3}\log N\log\log N\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: \(f_0(I(H_N))\) satisfies \(N^{1/3} \ll f_0(I(H_N)) \ll N^{1/3}\log N\), and these bounds are best possible up to multiplicative constants.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: \(f_0(I(H_N)) \asymp N^{1/3}\log N\); equivalently, there exist constants \(c, C > 0\), independent of \(N\), such that for all sufficiently large \(N\), \(c\,N^{1/3}\log N \le f_0(I(H_N)) \le C\,N^{1/3}\log N\).

question: Let \(\mathbf V\) be a pseudovariety of semigroups (that is, a class of finite semigroups closed under finite direct products, subsemigroups, and homomorphic images), and assume that every group in \(\mathbf V\) is solvable. Here \(N\) denotes the size of the ambient finite semigroup, and a straight-line program is a sequence that builds elements from the given generators using semigroup multiplication, with length equal to the number of multiplication steps. Which statement is equivalent to the assertion that \(\mathbf V\) admits straight-line programs of length \(\mathcal O((\log N)^2)\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: \(\mathbf V\) admits straight-line programs of length \(\mathcal O(\log N)\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(\mathbf V\) admits straight-line programs of width \(\mathcal O(1)\) and length \(\mathcal O(\log N)\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: \(\mathbf V\) affords efficient compression, i.e. admits straight-line programs of length \(\operatorname{polylog} N\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(\mathbf V\) admits straight-line programs of width \(2\) and length \(\mathcal O(\operatorname{polylog} N)\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: \(\mathbf V\) contains neither \(\mathbf{LRB}\) nor \(\mathbf{RRB}\), and contains no nonsolvable group.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: \(\mathbf V\) admits straight-line programs of length \(\mathcal O(\log N)\).

sketch:
In establishing the main theorem, the proof is based on “the construction of a specific set, denoted by $\Lambda(\Omega)$, associated with the geometry of $\Omega$ and the positive flow generated by
\begin{equation}\label{con}
\begin{cases}\nu_t - \Delta u = 0, & (x,t)\in \Omega\times\mathbb{R}^+, \\
\partial_{\nu} u = \lambda g(u), & (x,t)\in \partial\Omega\times\mathbb{R}^+, \\
u(x,0)=u_0(x), & x\in \Omega,
\end{cases}
\end{equation}
.” The strategy is that “whenever $\Lambda(\Omega)$ is nonempty, it contains the desired pattern,” so “the core of the argument consists in proving that $\Lambda(\Omega)\neq\emptyset$.”

We first prove the following theorem.
\begin{theorem}\label{tmi}
Let $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a bounded convex domain and again let
$
D := D(\Omega).
$
Then there exists a constant $c>0$ depending only on the dimension such that
\[
\mu_1(\Omega) \ge \frac{c}{D}.
\]
\end{theorem}
This estimate plays a key role in the proof of the main theorem.
question:
Let \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{R}^2\) be a smooth bounded convex domain with inradius \(\operatorname{inr}(\Omega)=r\) (the radius of the largest ball contained in \(\Omega\)) and diameter \(D(\Omega)=D\). Consider the parabolic boundary-value problem with bistable boundary nonlinearity \(g(u)=u-u^3\):
\[
\begin{cases}
u_t-\Delta u=0, & (x,t)\in \Omega\times \mathbb{R}^+,\\
\partial_{\nu}u=\lambda g(u), & (x,t)\in \partial\Omega\times \mathbb{R}^+,\\
u(x,0)=u_0(x), & x\in \Omega,
\end{cases}
\]
where \(\partial_{\nu}u\) denotes the outward normal derivative on \(\partial\Omega\), and a "pattern" means a non-constant stable stationary solution of this problem. Assuming the diameter \(D\) is sufficiently large, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of patterns for this problem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There exists a parameter \(\lambda>0\) such that the problem admits a pattern.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists a parameter \(\lambda>0\), depending only on \(r\), such that every smooth bounded convex domain with inradius \(r\) and sufficiently large diameter admits a pattern for that same \(\lambda\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a parameter \(\lambda>0\) such that the problem admits a non-constant stationary solution.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a parameter \(\lambda>0\), depending only on \(r\) and \(D\), such that every smooth bounded convex domain with \(\operatorname{inr}(\Omega)=r\) and \(D(\Omega)=D\) admits a pattern for that \(\lambda\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: One may choose \(\lambda\) so that \(0<\lambda\le \mu_1(\Omega)\), where \(\mu_1(\Omega)\) is the first positive Steklov eigenvalue of \(\Omega\), and then the problem admits a pattern.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: There exists a parameter \(\lambda>0\) such that the problem admits a pattern.

theorem:
\label{thm: main thm 2 and 3}
For $k=2,3$, 
a shifted 
$k$-uniform 
hypergraph $H\subseteq\binom{[n]}{k}$ is matroidal if and only if the (hyper)edges of $H$ form an 
initial lex-segment.
sketch:
For the ``only if'' part of \begin{theorem} \label{thm: main thm 2 and 3}
For $k=2,3$, 
a shifted 
$k$-uniform 
hypergraph $H\subseteq\binom{[n]}{k}$ is matroidal if and only if the (hyper)edges of $H$ form an 
initial lex-segment.
\end{theorem}
the approach is: (i) ``first make a reduction of the general problem to a certain restricted subset of hypergraphs''; (ii) for each hypergraph in this restricted subset, ``find a suitable permutation $\pi:[n]\rightarrow [n]$ such that the pair $(H, \pi(H))$, where $\pi(H)=\{\pi(T):\ T\in H\}$, violates the exchange axiom among the elements of $M(H)$.''

For the ``only if'' part of the main theorem (the $k\ge 4$ theorem under the condition $s_H\notin H$), the method is similarly to ``find a suitable such permutation $\pi=\pi(s_M)$ for each $H$.''
question:
Let k\in\{2,3\} and let H\subseteq \binom{[n]}{k} be a shifted k-uniform hypergraph on [n]=\{1,\dots,n\}, meaning that whenever \{s_1<\cdots<s_k\}\in H and r_i\le s_i for all i, then \{r_1<\cdots<r_k\}\in H. Write
\[
M(H)=\{G\subseteq \tbinom{[n]}{k}:\ \Delta^e(G)=H\},
\]
where \Delta^e denotes exterior algebraic shifting, and call H matroidal if M(H) is the set of bases of a matroid. Which statement is equivalent to H being matroidal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The hyperedges of H form an initial colex-segment; equivalently, there exists m such that H consists of exactly the first m elements of \binom{[n]}{k} in colexicographic order.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For each i\in[n], the subfamily \(\{e\setminus\{i\}: \min(e)=i,\ e\in H\}\subseteq \binom{\{i+1,\dots,n\}}{k-1}\) is an initial lex-segment; equivalently, within each block of hyperedges having the same smallest vertex, H is lex-initial.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a permutation \(\pi\in S_n\) such that \(\pi(H)=\{\pi(T):T\in H\}\) is an initial lex-segment of \(\binom{[n]}{k}\); equivalently, it is enough for H to be relabel-isomorphic to an initial lex-segment.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every \(i\in[n]\), the link \(\operatorname{lk}_H(i)=\{e\setminus\{i\}: i\in e\in H\}\subseteq \binom{[n]\setminus\{i\}}{k-1}\) is an initial lex-segment; equivalently, a vertexwise lex-initial condition already characterizes matroidality.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{thm_A}
Let $A$ be a Noetherian domain containing a field of characteristic zero. Let $X\subset\mathbb{P}^n_A$ be a closed subscheme that is smooth over $\operatorname{Spec} A$, and $X_t$ its $t$-th thickening, as defined above. Fix an integer~$j$, and set $\mathcal{F}\colonequals\calO_{\mathbb{P}^n_A}(j)$. Then there exists a nonzero element $a\in A$ such that 
\[
\operatorname{H}^i\big(X_t,\, \calO_{X_t}\otimes_{\calO_{\mathbb{P}^n_A}}\mathcal{F}\big)\otimes_A A_a
\]
is a flat $A_a$-module for each integer $i\ge 0$, for all thickenings $t\ge 1$.
sketch:
The introduction explains that the proof of the main theorem relies on understanding the asymptotic behavior of the cohomology of powers of the ideal sheaf. The approach proceeds as follows:

1. Develop a relative theory of ampleness: the authors use the notion of relatively ample vector bundles and develop the necessary theory of $f$-ample vector bundles and establish a fiberwise criterion for ampleness for a projective morphism $f\colon X\subset\PP_S^n\to S$. The primary example is the normal bundle $\scrN_X$ for a smooth closed immersion $X\hookrightarrow\PP_S^n$.

2. Prove a uniform vanishing theorem: a key technical tool is the following uniform vanishing result for the cohomology of the symmetric powers of the conormal bundle, extending classical results of Hartshorne.

\begin{theorem}
\label{thm_uniform_vanish}
Let $S$ be a Noetherian scheme over the field of rational numbers, and $f\colon X\subset\PP_S^n\to S$ be a smooth morphism of relative dimension $d\ge 0$. Let $\calF$ be a coherent sheaf on $X$ that is flat over $S$, and set $\calF_s\colonequals \calF\otimes_{\calO_S}\kappa(s)$ for $s\in S$. Then there exists an integer $t_0>0$ such that 
\[
\HH^i\left(X_s,\, \Sym^t\big(\scrI_{X_s}/\scrI_{X_s}^2\big)\otimes_{\calO_{X_s}}\calF_s\right)\ =\ 0
\]
for all $s\in S$, $t\ge t_0$, and $i\neq d$.
\end{theorem}

3. Combine vanishing with generic freeness and local duality:

(a) Use the generic freeness of local cohomology from \cite[Theorem~B]{CRS}: the local cohomology modules $\HH_I^i(R)$ are generically free over the base ring $A$, where $I$ is an ideal of $R\colonequals A[x_0,\dots,x_n]$.

(b) Using a graded local duality argument, obtain a stabilization result for the graded components of the modules $\Ext_R^i(R/I^t,\, R)$, corresponding to thickenings.

(c) Deduce the desired flatness for cohomology of thickenings by using the identification
\[
\dlim_t\, \Ext_R^i\big(R/I^t,\, R\big)\ =\ \HH_I^i(R),
\]
and the aforementioned stabilization result for Ext modules. Concretely, one uses the following stabilization-by-injectivity statement.

\begin{proposition}
\label{prop_inject}
Let $I \subset R$ be a homogeneous ideal and $X=V(I)\subset\PP_A^n$ be the closed subscheme defined by $I$. Fix an integer $j\in\ZZ$. Assume that: 

\begin{enumerate}[\quad\rm(a)]
\item The natural projection $X\subset\PP_A^n\to\Spec A$ is a smooth morphism of relative dimension $d\ge 0$,
\item $I^t/I^{t+1}$ is $A$-flat for each $t\ge 0$,
\item $\HH_I^i(R)$ is $A$-flat for each $i\ge 0$.
\end{enumerate}
Then there is an integer $t_0>0$, such that the natural map 
\[
\CD
\psi_{B,t}^i\colon {\Ext_{R_B}^i\left(R_B/I^tR_B,\, R_B\right)}_{-j-n-1} @>>> {\Ext_{R_B}^i\left(R_B/I^{t+1}R_B,\, R_B\right)}_{-j-n-1}
\endCD
\]
is injective for each $t\ge t_0$ and $i\ge 0$, where $B$ is an arbitrary $A$-algebra, and $R_B\colonequals R\otimes_A B$.
\end{proposition}

In addition, one applies the following valuative criterion for flatness of the relevant cohomology groups.

\begin{lemma}[Valuative criterion for the flatness of cohomology]
\label{prop_valuative}
Let $R= A[x_0,\dots,x_n]$ be a standard graded polynomial ring over a Noetherian domain $A$, and $I\subset R$ a homogeneous ideal. Fix $j \in \ZZ$ and $t\ge 1$. Assume that:
\begin{enumerate}[\quad\rm(a)]
\item For each $A$-algebra $V$ that is a discrete valuation ring, and for each $i\ge 0$, the natural map 
\[
\CD
{\Ext_{R_V}^i\left(R_V/I^tR_V,\, R_V\right)}_{-j-n-1} @>>> {\HH_I^i(R_V)}_{-j-n-1}
\endCD
\]
is injective, where $R_V\colonequals R\otimes_A V$,
\item $R/I^t$ is $A$-flat,
\item $\HH_I^i(R)$ is $A$-flat for each $i\ge 0$.
\end{enumerate}
Then ${\HH_\frakM^i(R/I^t)}_j$ is $A$-flat for each $i \ge 0$.
\end{lemma}
question: Let $A$ be a Noetherian domain containing a field of characteristic $0$, and let $X\subset \mathbb{P}^n_A$ be a closed subscheme that is smooth over $\operatorname{Spec} A$. If $\mathcal I\subset \mathcal O_{\mathbb P^n_A}$ is the ideal sheaf of $X$, write $X_t$ for the $t$-th thickening of $X$, i.e. the closed subscheme defined by $\mathcal I^t$ for $t\ge 1$. Fix an integer $j$ and set $\mathcal F=\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^n_A}(j)$. For a nonzero element $a\in A$, let $A_a$ denote the localization of $A$ at the powers of $a$. Which of the following is the strongest consequence of the assumptions above?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
There exists a nonzero element $a\in A$ such that for every integer $i\ge 0$ and every thickening $t\ge 1$, the localized cohomology module
\[
\operatorname{H}^i\big(X_t,\, \mathcal O_{X_t}\otimes_{\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^n_A}} \mathcal F\big)\otimes_A A_a
\]
is flat as an $A_a$-module.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exists a nonzero element $a\in A$ such that for every integer $i\ge 0$, there is an integer $t_0=t_0(i)\ge 1$ with the property that for all $t\ge t_0$, the natural restriction map
\[
\operatorname{H}^i\big(X_{t+1},\, \mathcal O_{X_{t+1}}\otimes_{\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^n_A}} \mathcal F\big)\otimes_A A_a\longrightarrow \operatorname{H}^i\big(X_t,\, \mathcal O_{X_t}\otimes_{\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^n_A}} \mathcal F\big)\otimes_A A_a
\]
is an isomorphism.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists a nonzero element $a\in A$ such that for every thickening $t\ge 1$, the localized cohomology module
\[
\operatorname{H}^0\big(X_t,\, \mathcal O_{X_t}\otimes_{\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^n_A}} \mathcal F\big)\otimes_A A_a
\]
is flat as an $A_a$-module.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exists a nonzero element $a\in A$ such that for every integer $i\ge 0$ and every thickening $t\ge 1$, the localized cohomology module
\[
\operatorname{H}^i\big(X_t,\, \mathcal O_{X_t}\otimes_{\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^n_A}} \mathcal F\big)\otimes_A A_a
\]
is flat as an $A_a$-module, and for each fixed $i$ its rank over $A_a$ is independent of $t$.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists a nonzero element $a\in A$ such that for every integer $i\ge 0$ and every thickening $t\ge 1$, the localized cohomology module
\[
\operatorname{H}^i\big(X_t,\, \mathcal O_{X_t}\otimes_{\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^n_A}} \mathcal F\big)\otimes_A A_a
\]
is a free $A_a$-module.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
There exists a nonzero element $a\in A$ such that for every integer $i\ge 0$ and every thickening $t\ge 1$, the localized cohomology module
\[
\operatorname{H}^i\big(X_t,\, \mathcal O_{X_t}\otimes_{\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^n_A}} \mathcal F\big)\otimes_A A_a
\]
is flat as an $A_a$-module.

theorem:
\label{MainTheorem}
 Let ${\mathbf K}$ be ${\mathbf N}$-$2$-AD regular. Suppose that the coefficients of the operator ${\mathcal L}$ belong to $C^3$. Then   function 
 $f$ defined on ${\mathbf K}$ 
 belongs to the class $\Hc_{{\mathcal L}}^{{\mathbf r}+\omega}({\mathbf K})$ if 
and only if
for any $\delta< \frac14\operatorname{diam\,}({\mathbf K})$, there exists an approximating 
function
 $v_\delta(x), \, x\in\Kb_\delta,$ such that, with some constant
$\mathbbm{c}>0$,
\begin{gather}\label{appr.function}
  \Lc_y v_\delta(y)=0, \, y\in \Kb_{\delta};\\\nonumber
  |v_\delta(x)-f(x)|\le \mathbbm{c} \delta^{\mathbf r}\omega(\delta), \, x\in 
  {\mathbf K};\\\nonumber
  |v_\delta(y)-v_{\delta/2}(y)|\le \mathbbm{c}\delta^{\mathbf r}\omega(\delta), \, 
  y\in\Kb_{\frac{\delta}{2}}.
\end{gather}
sketch:
The introduction explains the structure of the proof of the main theorem as follows.

1. Start with $f\in \Hc_{{\mathcal L}}^{{\mathbf r}+\omega}({\mathbf K})$ and construct a “special extension” $f_0$ to a fixed neighborhood $\Omega$ of ${\mathbf K}$.

2. For $f_0$, use the Green function $G^{\circ}(x,y)$ of ${\mathcal L}$ in $\Omega$ to establish the integral representation
\begin{equation}\label{intro.repres}
  f_0(x)=\int_{\Om}\Lc f_0(y)G^{\circ}(x,y)dy, \, x\in \Om.
\end{equation}
Because $f_0$ may have derivatives that “behave badly when approaching ${\mathbf K}$,” the representation requires “detailed control” of ${\mathcal L}f_0(x)$ and derivatives of $f_0(x)$ near ${\mathbf K}$.

3. Obtain this control via “complicated estimates” for the Green function $G_{x,r}(x,y)$ in balls $B_r(x)$ and its derivatives “up to the third order” in $x,y$, and also in the parameter variable $\varsigma$. Some estimates are derived “using Schauder-type approach,” and others are taken from Ju.~Krasovskii \cite{Kras.Sing} and M.~Gr\"uter--K.-O.~Widman \cite{Widman}.

4. Define the approximants by
\[
  v_{\delta}(x)=\int_{\Omega\setminus \Kb_{\delta}}{\mathcal L} f_0(y)G^{\circ}(x,y)\,dy,
\]
“with addition of a collection of several compensatory ${\mathcal L}$-harmonic terms of a simpler nature”, namely (as indicated by the reference)
\begin{equation}\label{35}
  v_{\de}(x)=c_\Nb\int\limits_{\Om\setminus \Kb'_\de}G(x,y)\Lc 
  f_0(y)dy+\sum_{\n=1}^{N} F_\n(x).
\end{equation}
Then “the fact that $v_{\delta}$ is ${\mathcal L}$-harmonic in $\Kb_{\delta}$ is obvious” from the definition of $G^{\circ}(x,y)$.

5. The estimates giving the approximation quality (the bounds in
\begin{gather}\label{appr.function}
  \Lc_y v_\de(y)=0, \, y\in \Kb_{\de};\\\nonumber
  |v_\de(x)-f(x)|\le \mathbbm{c} \de^\rb\om(\de), \, x\in 
  \Kb;\\\nonumber
  |v_\de(y)-v_{\de/2}(y)|\le \mathbbm{c}\de^\rb\om(\de), \, 
  y\in\Kb_{\frac{\de}{2}}.
\end{gather}
) “follow from our estimates for the function $f_0(x)$ and its derivatives.”

The paper outline reiterates that next it constructs $f_0$, proves the integral representation, and thereby “present[s] the required approximation… thus proving the main theorem.”
question: Let \(\mathbf K\subset \mathbb R^{\mathbf N}\) be a compact \((\mathbf N-2)\)-Ahlfors-David regular set, let \(\omega\) be a continuity modulus, and let \(\mathcal L=-\nabla\!\cdot(\mathbf a(x)\nabla)\) be a formally self-adjoint second-order elliptic operator with \(C^3\) coefficients defined on a neighborhood of \(\mathbf K\). Write \(\mathbf K_\rho\) for the \(\rho\)-neighborhood of \(\mathbf K\). Which statement is equivalent to the condition that a function \(f\) on \(\mathbf K\) belongs to the class \(\mathcal H_{\mathcal L}^{\mathbf r+\omega}(\mathbf K)\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists a constant \(\mathbbm c>0\), independent of \(\delta\), such that for every \(\delta<\tfrac14\operatorname{diam}(\mathbf K)\) there is a function \(v_\delta\) on \(\mathbf K_\delta\) satisfying \(\mathcal L_y v_\delta(y)=0\) for all \(y\in \mathbf K_\delta\), \(|v_\delta(x)-f(x)|\le \mathbbm c\,\delta^{\mathbf r}\omega(\delta)\) for all \(x\in \mathbf K\), and \(|v_\delta(y)-v_{\delta/2}(y)|\le \mathbbm c\,\delta^{\mathbf r}\omega(\delta)\) for all \(y\in \mathbf K_{\delta/4}\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a constant \(\mathbbm c>0\), independent of \(\delta\), such that for every \(\delta<\tfrac14\operatorname{diam}(\mathbf K)\) there is a function \(v_\delta\) on \(\mathbf K_\delta\) satisfying \(\mathcal L_y v_\delta(y)=0\) for all \(y\in \mathbf K_\delta\) and \(|v_\delta(x)-f(x)|\le \mathbbm c\,\delta^{\mathbf r}\omega(\delta)\) for all \(x\in \mathbf K\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a constant \(\mathbbm c>0\), independent of \(\delta\), such that for every \(\delta<\tfrac14\operatorname{diam}(\mathbf K)\) and every \(x\in \mathbf K\) there is a function \(v_{x,\delta}\) on \(\mathbf K_\delta\) satisfying \(\mathcal L_y v_{x,\delta}(y)=0\) for all \(y\in \mathbf K_\delta\), \(|v_{x,\delta}(z)-f(z)|\le \mathbbm c\,\delta^{\mathbf r}\omega(\delta)\) for all \(z\in \mathbf K\cap B(x,\delta)\), and \(|v_{x,\delta}(y)-v_{x,\delta/2}(y)|\le \mathbbm c\,\delta^{\mathbf r}\omega(\delta)\) for all \(y\in \mathbf K_{\delta/2}\cap B(x,\delta/2)\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists a constant \(\mathbbm c>0\), independent of \(\delta\), such that for every \(\delta<\tfrac14\operatorname{diam}(\mathbf K)\) there is a function \(v_\delta\) on \(\mathbf K_\delta\) satisfying \(\mathcal L_y v_\delta(y)=0\) for all \(y\in \mathbf K_{\delta/2}\), \(|v_\delta(x)-f(x)|\le \mathbbm c\,\delta^{\mathbf r}\omega(\delta)\) for all \(x\in \mathbf K\), and \(|v_\delta(y)-v_{\delta/2}(y)|\le \mathbbm c\,\delta^{\mathbf r}\omega(\delta)\) for all \(y\in \mathbf K_{\delta/2}\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
[Fundamental inequality]\label{thm:main_intro}
Let $F\subset S^3$ be a closed surface. Then every knot $K\subset S^3$ satisfies:
\[
c(K;F) \ge 2\bigl(t(K)-\delta(F)\bigr)+1,
\]
where $t(K)$ is the tunnel number of $K$, and $\delta(F)=g(M_1)+g(M_2)-g(F)$ is the Heegaard deficiency of $F$.
sketch:
To prove the main theorem, the argument “relies on a chain of inequalities relating the surface crossing number $c(K;F)$, the surface ascending number $a(K;F)$, and the surface bridge number $b(K;F)$”:
\[
\frac{c(K;F)-1}{2} \ge a(K;F) \ge b(K;F)-1 \ge t(K)-\delta(F).
\]
This sequence yields the stated lower bound for $c(K;F)$ in terms of “a combination of the knot exterior's complexity, $t(K)$, and the ambient surface's Heegaard deficiency, $\delta(F)$.”

For optimality, the introduction says they “prove that the linear lower bound in the main theorem is asymptotically optimal” by taking iterated connected sums $K_m$ of a prime knot and obtaining
\[
c(K_m;F) \ge 2m - 2\delta(F) + 1.
\]
“Pairing this with a general upper bound derived from the subadditivity of the planar crossing number yields $c(K_m;F)=\Theta(t(K_m))$,” so “no general lower bound of higher order exists,” and $c(K;F)$ grows linearly with tunnel number.
question:
Let \(F\subset S^3\) be a closed surface, and let \(M_1\) and \(M_2\) be the two components of \(S^3\setminus F\). Define the Heegaard deficiency of \(F\) by
\[
\delta(F)=g(M_1)+g(M_2)-g(F),
\]
where \(g(M_i)\) denotes the Heegaard genus of \(M_i\) and \(g(F)\) denotes the genus of \(F\). For a knot \(K\subset S^3\), let \(c(K;F)\) denote the surface crossing number of \(K\) with respect to \(F\), i.e. the minimal number of crossings among all regular diagrams of \(K\) obtained by isotoping \(K\) into a regular neighborhood of \(F\). If \(t(K)\) is the tunnel number of \(K\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the quantitative lower bound for \(c(K;F)\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
For every knot \(K\subset S^3\),
\[
c(K;F)\ge 2\bigl(t(K)-\delta(F)\bigr)+1.
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every knot \(K\subset S^3\),
\[
c(K;F)\ge 2\bigl(t(K)-\delta(F)+1\bigr)+1.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
For every knot \(K\subset S^3\),
\[
c(K;F)\ge 2\bigl(t(K)-\delta(F)\bigr).
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every knot \(K\subset S^3\),
\[
c(K;F)\ge 2t(K)-\delta(F)+1.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every knot \(K\subset S^3\),
\[
c(K;F)\ge 2\bigl(t(K)-\delta(F)\bigr)+2.
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
For every knot \(K\subset S^3\),
\[
c(K;F)\ge 2\bigl(t(K)-\delta(F)\bigr)+1.
\]

question: Let $R$ be a $k$-algebra. For each positive integer $m$, write $\operatorname{Der}_k^m(R)$ for the set of $m$-integrable $k$-derivations of $R$, where a $k$-derivation $\delta$ is called $m$-integrable if it lies in the image of the truncation map $\operatorname{HS}_k^m(R)\to \operatorname{HS}_k^1(R)=\operatorname{Der}_k(R)$. Write $\operatorname{Ider}_k(R)$ for the set of integrable (i.e. infinitely integrable) $k$-derivations. Which statement correctly describes the eventual behavior of the family $\operatorname{Der}_k^m(R)$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists a positive integer \(M\), depending only on the base field \(k\) and not on \(R\), such that for every \(k\)-algebra \(R\) and every \(m\ge M\), \(\operatorname{Der}_k^m(R)=\operatorname{Ider}_k(R)\); equivalently, the family \(\operatorname{Der}_k^m(R)\) stabilizes to \(\operatorname{Ider}_k(R)\) with a uniform bound valid for all \(R\)."

choices.label: C
choices.text: If a \(k\)-derivation \(\delta\) of \(R\) is \(m\)-integrable for every positive integer \(m\), then \(\delta\in \operatorname{Ider}_k(R)\); equivalently, \(\bigcap_{m\ge 1}\operatorname{Der}_k^m(R)=\operatorname{Ider}_k(R)\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a positive integer \(M\), depending on \(R\), such that the single equality \(\operatorname{Der}_k^M(R)=\operatorname{Der}_k^{M+1}(R)\) already forces \(\operatorname{Der}_k^m(R)=\operatorname{Ider}_k(R)\) for every \(m\ge M\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists a positive integer \(M\), depending on \(R\), such that whenever a \(k\)-derivation \(\delta\) is \(M\)-integrable, it is \(m\)-integrable for every integer \(m\ge M\), although \(\delta\) need not be infinitely integrable.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
[$2\pi^2$-Theorem, Marques-Neves~\cite{MN14}]\label{2pi2-theorem}
Let $\Sigma\subset\varmathbb{S}^3$ be a smooth embedded surface of genus at least $1$.  
Then its canonical five-parameter family 
\[
\{\Sigma_{(v,t)}\}_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]}
\]
satisfies
\[
\max_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]}
\mathrm{Area}\bigl(\Sigma_{(v,t)}\bigr)\;\geqslant\; 2\pi^2.
\]
Moreover, equality holds if and only if $\Sigma$ is the Clifford torus.
sketch:
Using the Marques--Neves five-parameter family $\{\Sigma_{(v,t)}\}$, the key point is to show that if a genus--one surface $\Sigma$ satisfies $\mathcal{W}(\Sigma)\leqslant 2\pi^2+\delta^2$, then after a \emph{conformal} transformation one gets a surface $\Sigma'$ with $\mathrm{Area}(\Sigma')\geqslant 2\pi^2-C\delta^2$.

To prove the main theorem, we use that the full $(v,t)$-family contains a slice of area at least $2\pi^2$, and since $v$ encodes conformal transformations and $t$ is signed distance, the aim is to realize such a high-area slice \emph{within the conformal} four-parameter subfamily; for this one must control $t$ in terms of the energy gap $\delta$, so the maximizing slice lies close to $t=0$.

A closer examination of the Heintze--Karcher inequality used by Marques--Neves shows that, once the family is built from the quantitatively normalized surface, the problem reduces to proving the maximizing parameter $(v_0,t_0)$ stays a definite distance away from the boundary of the conformal group, i.e. there exists $\eta_1>0$ such that $|v_0|\leqslant 1-\eta_1$ (reduced later; technical proof deferred to an appendix).

Geometric intuition for this uniform bound: as $|v|\to1$, $\Sigma_{(v,t)}$ becomes quantitatively close to a geodesic sphere, forcing area strictly below $2\pi^2$, so such $v$ cannot maximize. More precisely, for $t=0$, $\Sigma_v=\Sigma_{(v,0)}$ is Hausdorff close to a geodesic sphere $S$, so $\mathrm{Area}(\Sigma_v)$ is close to $\mathrm{Area}(S)\le 4\pi$, and for small $|t|$ one uses
\[
\bigl|\mathrm{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})-\mathrm{Area}(\Sigma_v)\bigr|\lesssim |t|\,\mathcal{W}(\Sigma_v)
\]
so the area stays $<5\pi$. For larger $|t|$, either (i) if $\Sigma_v$ stays away from the poles, an Euclidean-model argument shows $\Sigma_{(v,t)}$ is a small Lipschitz graph over a geodesic sphere hence has area $<5\pi$, or (ii) if $\Sigma_v$ lies close to a pole, comparison geometry gives $\mathrm{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})<5\pi$. Thus for $|v|$ sufficiently close to $1$, $\mathrm{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})<5\pi<2\pi^2$, proving $|v_0|\le 1-\eta_1$.

With this uniform control of $v$, the remaining task is to derive a quantitative stability estimate for minimal surfaces; the paper then uses the earlier area lower bound to reduce Willmore stability to minimal-surface stability, and later performs linearization/stability analysis to obtain the quantitative bounds (conformal factor, conformal structure, and $W^{2,2}$-distance to the Clifford torus), thereby proving Theorem~\ref{Thm: main}.
question:
Let \(\Sigma\subset \mathbb{S}^3\subset \mathbb{R}^4\) be a smooth embedded surface in the round 3-sphere, with genus at least \(1\). For \(v\in \mathring{B}^4=\{v\in\mathbb{R}^4:|v|<1\}\), let
\[
F_v(x)=(1-|v|^2)\frac{x-v}{|x-v|^2}-v
\]
be the associated conformal transformation of \(\mathbb{S}^3\), and set \(\Sigma_v:=F_v(\Sigma)\). If \(\mathbb{S}^3\setminus \Sigma=A\cup A^*\) is the decomposition into connected components, let \(A_v:=F_v(A)\), let \(d_v\) be the signed distance to \(\Sigma_v\) (positive on \(A_v\), negative on the other component), and define
\[
\Sigma_{(v,t)}=\partial\{x\mid d_v(x)<t\}, \qquad (v,t)\in\mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi].
\]
Thus \(\{\Sigma_{(v,t)}\}_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]}\) is the canonical five-parameter family of \(\Sigma\). Which quantitative area estimate holds for this family, and in which case does equality occur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: \[\max_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]} \operatorname{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})\ge 2\pi^2,\] and equality holds if and only if \(\Sigma\) is the Clifford torus.

choices.label: B
choices.text: \[\max_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]} \operatorname{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})\le 2\pi^2,\] and equality holds if and only if \(\Sigma\) is the Clifford torus.

choices.label: C
choices.text: \[\max_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]} \operatorname{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})\ge 2\pi^2.\]

choices.label: D
choices.text: \[\max_{v\in \mathring{B}^4} \operatorname{Area}(\Sigma_v)\ge 2\pi^2,\] and equality holds if and only if \(\Sigma\) is the Clifford torus.

choices.label: E
choices.text: \[\max_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]} \operatorname{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})\ge 2\pi^2,\] and equality holds whenever \(\Sigma\) is conformally equivalent to the Clifford torus.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: \[\max_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]} \operatorname{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})\ge 2\pi^2,\] and equality holds if and only if \(\Sigma\) is the Clifford torus.

theorem:
\label{vbint-}
Let $0<r\leq\infty$. 
Let $M$ be a domain in ${\bf C}^n$ with $\partial M\in C^2$. Assume that   the Levi form of $\partial M$ on $T^{(1,0)}\partial M$ has either $n-1$ positive or at least three negative eigenvalues at  $\zeta_0\in\partial M$.  
Let $E$ be a complex vector bundle of class $Lip$ $($resp. $\Lambda^{r+1})$ over $\overline M$. Assume that a connection  $D\colon Lip(M,E)\to L_{(1)}^\infty(M,E)$  $($resp. $\Lambda^{r+1}(M,E)\to \Lambda^{r}_{(1)}(M,E))$ satisfies $\Omega^{(0,2)}=0$ on $ M$ in the sense of distributions. Then $E|_U$ has a    holomorphic vector bundle structure, where $U$ is some open subset of $\overline M$ and $\zeta_0\in U$. Furthermore, the holomorphic structure is of class $C^{1/2}$ $($resp. $\Lambda^{r+1/2})$ with respect to the original $Lip$ $($resp. $\Lambda^{r+1})$ structure of $E|_U$.
sketch:
To prove the main theorem, we reduce it to the following theorem.

\begin{thm}\label{vbint} 
Let $M$ be a domain in $\mathbb C^n$ with $\pd M\in C^2$. Assume that    the Levi-form of $M$ has
either $(n-1)$ positive or at least $3$ negative Levi eigenvalues at $\zeta_0\in\pd M$. Let $U$ be an open set in $\ov M$ and $\zeta_0\in U$.    Assume that $\om$ is a $k_0\times k_0$ matrix of $(0,1)$ forms on $U$ such that $\db\om=\om\wedge\om$ holds on $U\setminus\pd M$ in the sense of distributions. Assume that $\om$ is in $L^\infty(U)$ $($resp. $\Lambda^r(U)$ with $r>0)$. Then there is an invertible matrix $A$   of class $\Lambda^{1/2}$ $($resp. $\Lambda^{r+1/2})$ on some neighborhood $U'$ of $\zeta_0$ in $\ov M$ such that 
$
\db A+A\om=0
$
in the sense of distributions on $U'\setminus\pd M$.
\end{thm}

The reduction requires a careful discussion of $L^\infty$ connections on Lipschitz bundles and of \textit{admissible} frame changes for which the transformation law (\ref{hatomom}) remains valid; for this we use the following proposition.

\begin{prop}\label{d2=0}
Let $D$ be a connection for a complex vector bundle $E$. Let $e=(e_1,\dots, e_{k_0})^t$ be a local frame field of $E$ over $U$. Assume that $De=\om \otimes e$ and $\om\in L^2_{loc}(U)$ and $d\om\in L^1_{loc}(U)$. If $A$ is admissible, then $\hat\om=(dA)A^{-1}+A\om A^{-1}$ is still in $L^2_{loc}(U)$ and  the curvature  forms    satisfy $A\Om A^{-1}=\hat\Om$. 
\end{prop}

“We now outline our approach. Equation (\ref{dbAA}) may be viewed as a nonlinear $\dbar$-equation, and the integrability condition (\ref{Om02=0}) is also nonlinear. We apply homotopy formulas to the study of (\ref{dbAA}).”

The proof is then completed as follows: Sections 3 and 4 “reformulate the local homotopy formulas … for shrinking domains,” Section 5 recalls and derives “estimates … on shrinking domains,” and finally we complete the proof of the main theorem by establishing the theorem above.
question: Let $0<r\leq \infty$. Let $M\subset \mathbf{C}^n$ be a domain with $\partial M\in C^2$, and let $\zeta_0\in \partial M$ be a boundary point such that the Levi form of $\partial M$ on $T^{(1,0)}\partial M$ has either $n-1$ positive eigenvalues or at least three negative eigenvalues at $\zeta_0$. Let $E\to \overline M$ be a complex vector bundle of class $Lip$ (respectively of class $\Lambda^{r+1}$). Suppose there is a connection $D\colon Lip(M,E)\to L_{(1)}^{\infty}(M,E)$ (respectively $D\colon \Lambda^{r+1}(M,E)\to \Lambda^r_{(1)}(M,E)$), where the curvature is $\Omega=D\circ D$ and $\Omega^{(0,2)}$ denotes its $(0,2)$-component, such that $\Omega^{(0,2)}=0$ on $M$ in the sense of distributions. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of a holomorphic vector bundle structure on $E$ near $\zeta_0$ under these hypotheses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists an open neighborhood $U$ of $\zeta_0$ in $\overline M$ such that $E|_U$ admits a holomorphic vector bundle structure, and this holomorphic structure is of class $Lip$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1}$) with respect to the original $Lip$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1}$) structure of $E|_U$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists an open neighborhood $U$ of $\zeta_0$ in $\overline M$ and a number $\alpha>0$ such that $E|_U$ admits a holomorphic vector bundle structure of class $C^{\alpha}$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+\alpha}$) with respect to the original $Lip$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1}$) structure of $E|_U$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists an open neighborhood $U$ of $\zeta_0$ in $\overline M$ such that $E|_U$ admits a holomorphic vector bundle structure of class $C^{1/2}$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1/2}$) with respect to the original bundle structure, and in fact the same conclusion would still follow if the Levi form at $\zeta_0$ had merely at least two negative eigenvalues or at least one positive eigenvalue.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every open neighborhood $U$ of $\zeta_0$ in $\overline M$, the restriction $E|_U$ admits a holomorphic vector bundle structure of class $C^{1/2}$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1/2}$) with respect to the original $Lip$ (respectively $\Lambda^{r+1}$) structure of $E|_U$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question:
Let \(\mathcal H\in(\tfrac13,1)\). Work on the torus \(\mathbb T^d\), and let \(u^\epsilon,w^\epsilon,r^\epsilon:\mathbb T^d\times[0,T]\to\mathbb R^d\) be divergence-free, zero-mean vector fields. Let \(W_t^{\mathcal H}\) be a divergence-free \(\mathcal H\)-fractional Wiener process on \([L^2(\mathbb T^d)]^d\) with covariance operator \(Q\). Write \(A\) for the Laplacian/Stokes operator on divergence-free fields and \(b(a,c)=a\cdot\nabla c\). For each \(\epsilon\in(0,1)\), let \(u_0^\epsilon,v_0^\epsilon\) be divergence-free, zero-mean initial data satisfying
\[
\sup_{\epsilon\in(0,1)}\bigl(\|u_0^\epsilon\|_{L_x^2}+\|\sqrt\epsilon\,v_0^\epsilon\|_{L_x^2}\bigr)<\infty,
\]
and assume that the operators \(Q\) and \(C\) satisfy the required hypotheses. Consider the \(\epsilon\)-dependent system
\[
\partial_t u^{\epsilon}=\nu A u^{\epsilon}-b\bigl(u^{\epsilon}+\epsilon^{-\alpha+\mathcal H}w^{\epsilon}+r^{\epsilon},u^{\epsilon}\bigr),
\]
\[
dw^{\epsilon}=\epsilon^{-1}Cw^{\epsilon}\,dt+\epsilon^{-\mathcal H}dW_t^{\mathcal H},
\]
\[
\partial_t r^{\epsilon}=\epsilon^{-1}Cr^{\epsilon}+\nu A\bigl(\epsilon^{-\alpha+\mathcal H}w^{\epsilon}+r^{\epsilon}\bigr)-b\bigl(u^{\epsilon}+\epsilon^{-\alpha+\mathcal H}w^{\epsilon}+r^{\epsilon},\epsilon^{-\alpha+\mathcal H}w^{\epsilon}+r^{\epsilon}\bigr),
\]
with initial conditions \(u^{\epsilon}(0)=u_0^\epsilon\), \(w^{\epsilon}(0)=0\), and \(r^{\epsilon}(0)=v_0^\epsilon\).
