theorem:
[Boundedness of augmented irregularity]\label{thm:1-3}  Let $(X, D)$ be a $\mathcal C$-pair that satisfies one of the following conditions.
  \begin{enumerate}
  \item\label{il:1-3-1} The analytic variety $X$ is compact Kähler and $(X,D)$
    is locally uniformizable.

  \item\label{il:1-3-2} The analytic variety $X$ is projective and $(X,D)$ is
    divisorially log terminal (=dlt).
  \end{enumerate}
  If $(X,D)$ is special, then $q⁺(X, D) ≤ \dim X$.
sketch:
For Campana’s classic result, it “suffices to show that the Albanese map of a special manifold is necessarily surjective”; arguing by contraposition, Campana studies the case when the Albanese map is “not surjective” and, using positivity results for sheaves of differentials on varieties of maximal Albanese dimension, “constructs a sheaf of differentials where equality in the Bogomolov-Sommese inequality is attained,” which “show[s] that the underlying space cannot be special.”

In the present setting, “adapted differentials take the role that ordinary differentials play,” but there is “in general no ‘adapted Albanese map’,” and even when an Albanese exists, “the classical equality between the irregularity and the dimension of the Albanese variety is not true in general.” The authors “overcome this problem by considering the classic Albanese of suitable covers, where adapted differentials define a foliation.” Although the foliation typically has “Zariski dense leaves,” they “leverage ideas from Catanese’s work on generalized Castelnuovo–De Franchis theorems and ‘strict wedge subspaces of differentials’” to obtain “positivity results for the foliated variety that can be used in lieu of the classic arguments.”

A technical input is that “the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:1-3} relies in part on” an extension theorem: “adapted reflexive 1-forms on $(X,D)$ extend to log resolutions of singularities” (Theorem~\ref{thm:1-6}). For 1-forms this approach “work[s] particularly well,” yielding Corollary~\ref{cor:1-7}, which bounds the \(\mathcal C\)-Kodaira–Iitaka dimension of rank-one subsheaves of adapted one-forms on covers of special pairs.
question: Let $(X,D)$ be a $\mathcal C$-pair. Assume that either (i) $X$ is a compact K\"ahler analytic variety and $(X,D)$ is locally uniformizable, or (ii) $X$ is projective and $(X,D)$ is divisorially log terminal (dlt). Assume in addition that $(X,D)$ is special. Here $q^+(X,D)$ denotes the augmented irregularity of the pair, i.e. the supremum of the irregularities over covers of $X$ compatible with the pair structure. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about $q^+(X,D)$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: One has $q^+(X,D)\leq \dim X-1$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: One has $q^+(X,D)<\infty$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a compatible cover $\gamma:\widehat X\to X$ for which the supremum is attained, i.e. $q(X,D,\gamma)=q^+(X,D)$, and hence $q(X,D,\gamma)\leq \dim X$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: If $X$ is projective and $(X,D)$ is dlt, then $q^+(X,D)\leq \dim X$; in the compact K\"ahler locally uniformizable case, one can only conclude that $q^+(X,D)<\infty$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question:
Let \(\Omega\subset \mathbb R^n\) be a bounded domain, \(T>0\), and \(\Omega_T:=\Omega\times(0,T)\). Write \(z=(x,t)\in\Omega_T\), let \(a:\Omega_T\to[0,\infty)\) be given, and define
\[
H(z,t):=t^p+a(z)t^q \qquad (t\ge 0).
\]
Assume \(\mathcal A:\Omega_T\times \mathbb R^{Nn}\to \mathbb R^{Nn}\) is a Carathéodory vector field such that for some constants \(0<\nu\le L<\infty\),
\[
\mathcal A(z,\xi)\cdot \xi\ge \nu\big(|\xi|^p+a(z)|\xi|^q\big),
\qquad
|\mathcal A(z,\xi)|\le L\big(|\xi|^{p-1}+a(z)|\xi|^{q-1}\big)
\]
for a.e. \(z\in\Omega_T\) and all \(\xi\in\mathbb R^{Nn}\). Let \(Q_\rho(z_0)\) denote a parabolic cylinder of radius \(\rho\) centered at \(z_0\). Suppose
\[
 u\in C(0,T;L^2(\Omega,\mathbb R^N))\cap L^q(0,T;W^{1,q}(\Omega,\mathbb R^N))\cap L^\infty(\Omega_T)
\]
is a weak solution in \(\Omega_T\) of
\[
 u_t-\operatorname{div}\mathcal A(z,\nabla u)=-\operatorname{div}\big(|F|^{p-2}F+a(z)|F|^{q-2}F\big).
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the uniform higher integrability of \(\nabla u\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
There exist constants \(\varepsilon_0=\varepsilon_0(\text{structural data})\) and \(c=c(\text{structural data},\|a\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)})\) such that for every parabolic cylinder \(Q_{4r}(z_0)\subset \Omega_T\) with \(r\in(0,1)\) and every \(\varepsilon\in(0,\varepsilon_0)\),
\[
\iint_{Q_r(z_0)} H(z,|\nabla u|)^{1+\varepsilon}\,dz
\le c\left(\left(\frac{\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}}{r}\right)^p+\|a\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}\left(\frac{\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}}{r}\right)^q+1\right)^{1+\frac{q\varepsilon}{p}}
\left(\iint_{Q_{4r}(z_0)} H(z,|\nabla u|)\,dz+\left(\iint_{Q_{4r}(z_0)} H(z,|F|)^{1+\varepsilon}\,dz\right)^{\!\frac{1}{1+\varepsilon}}\right)^{1+\frac{q\varepsilon}{p}}.
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exist constants \(\varepsilon_0=\varepsilon_0(\text{structural data})\) and \(c=c(\text{structural data},\|a\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)})\) such that for every parabolic cylinder \(Q_{4r}(z_0)\subset \Omega_T\) with \(r\in(0,1)\) and every \(\varepsilon\in(0,\varepsilon_0)\),
\[
\iint_{Q_r(z_0)} H(z,|\nabla u|)^{1+\varepsilon}\,dz
\le c\left(\left(\frac{\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}}{r}\right)^p+\|a\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}\left(\frac{\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}}{r}\right)^q+1\right)^{1+\frac{q\varepsilon}{p}}
\left(\iint_{Q_{4r}(z_0)} H(z,|\nabla u|)\,dz+\iint_{Q_{4r}(z_0)} H(z,|F|)\,dz\right)^{1+\frac{q\varepsilon}{p}}.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists a constant \(\varepsilon_0=\varepsilon_0(\text{structural data})>0\) such that for every parabolic cylinder \(Q_{4r}(z_0)\subset \Omega_T\) with \(r\in(0,1)\) and every \(\varepsilon\in(0,\varepsilon_0)\), one has
\[
\iint_{Q_r(z_0)} H(z,|\nabla u|)^{1+\varepsilon}\,dz<\infty.
\]
In particular, \(H(z,|\nabla u|)\in L^{1+\varepsilon}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega_T)\) for all such \(\varepsilon\).

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exist constants \(\varepsilon_0=\varepsilon_0(\text{structural data})\) and \(c=c(\text{structural data},\|a\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)})\) such that for every parabolic cylinder \(Q_{4r}(z_0)\subset \Omega_T\) with \(r\in(0,1)\) and every \(\varepsilon\in(0,\varepsilon_0)\),
\[
\iint_{Q_r(z_0)} H(z,|\nabla u|)^{1+\varepsilon}\,dz
\le c\left(\left(\frac{\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}}{r}\right)^p+\|a\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}\left(\frac{\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}}{r}\right)^q+1\right)^{1+\varepsilon}
\left(\iint_{Q_{4r}(z_0)} H(z,|\nabla u|)\,dz+\left(\iint_{Q_{4r}(z_0)} H(z,|F|)^{1+\varepsilon}\,dz\right)^{\!\frac{1}{1+\varepsilon}}\right)^{1+\varepsilon}.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exist constants \(\varepsilon_0=\varepsilon_0(\text{structural data})\) and \(c=c(\text{structural data},\|a\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)})\) such that for every parabolic cylinder \(Q_{4r}(z_0)\subset \Omega_T\) with \(r\in(0,1)\), one has the endpoint estimate
\[
\iint_{Q_r(z_0)} H(z,|\nabla u|)^{1+\varepsilon_0}\,dz
\le c\left(\left(\frac{\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}}{r}\right)^p+\|a\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}\left(\frac{\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}}{r}\right)^q+1\right)^{1+\frac{q\varepsilon_0}{p}}
\left(\iint_{Q_{4r}(z_0)} H(z,|\nabla u|)\,dz+\left(\iint_{Q_{4r}(z_0)} H(z,|F|)^{1+\varepsilon_0}\,dz\right)^{\!\frac{1}{1+\varepsilon_0}}\right)^{1+\frac{q\varepsilon_0}{p}}.
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
There exist constants \(\varepsilon_0=\varepsilon_0(\text{structural data})\) and \(c=c(\text{structural data},\|a\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)})\) such that for every parabolic cylinder \(Q_{4r}(z_0)\subset \Omega_T\) with \(r\in(0,1)\) and every \(\varepsilon\in(0,\varepsilon_0)\),
\[
\iint_{Q_r(z_0)} H(z,|\nabla u|)^{1+\varepsilon}\,dz
\le c\left(\left(\frac{\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}}{r}\right)^p+\|a\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}\left(\frac{\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega_T)}}{r}\right)^q+1\right)^{1+\frac{q\varepsilon}{p}}
\left(\iint_{Q_{4r}(z_0)} H(z,|\nabla u|)\,dz+\left(\iint_{Q_{4r}(z_0)} H(z,|F|)^{1+\varepsilon}\,dz\right)^{\!\frac{1}{1+\varepsilon}}\right)^{1+\frac{q\varepsilon}{p}}.
\]

theorem:
\label{mainCyc5ecBou}
    Every flow-admissible, cyclically 5-edge-connected, cubic signed graph has a nowhere-zero 6-flow.,
sketch: To prove the main theorem, start with a flow-admissible, cyclically 5-edge-connected cubic signed graph $G$, which "has a special nowhere-zero $\Z_6$-flow by a theorem of DeVos et al." Because $G$ is cubic, "without loss we may assume every vertex has at most one edge carrying flow into it, and boundary in $\{0,6\}." Preserving these properties, "we contract all positive edges." Then "a perfect matching in the contracted graph converts the $\Z_6$-flow to a 6-flow, which yields a 6-flow in $G$." The cyclically 5-edge-connected assumption is "only used to find the perfect matching."
question: Let $(G,\sigma)$ be a signed graph, meaning that each edge of the cubic graph $G$ is assigned a sign $\pm 1$. Assume that $G$ is 3-regular, that $(G,\sigma)$ is flow-admissible (that is, it admits a nowhere-zero integer flow in the usual signed-graph sense), and that it is cyclically 5-edge-connected, i.e. every edge cut whose removal separates the graph into two subgraphs each containing a cycle has size at least $5$. Here a nowhere-zero $6$-flow means a nowhere-zero integer flow whose edge values lie in $\{\pm1,\pm2,\pm3,\pm4,\pm5\}$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about $(G,\sigma)$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every flow-admissible, cyclically 4-edge-connected, cubic signed graph has a nowhere-zero 6-flow.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every flow-admissible, cyclically 5-edge-connected, cubic signed graph has a nowhere-zero $\mathbb{Z}_6$-flow.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every flow-admissible, cyclically 5-edge-connected, cubic signed graph has a nowhere-zero 5-flow.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Every cyclically 5-edge-connected, cubic signed graph has a nowhere-zero $\mathbb{Z}_6$-flow.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question: Let \(F\subseteq \mathbb{R}^p\) be a connected self-similar set with finitely many directions, meaning that in a defining iterated function system for \(F\), the orthogonal parts of the similarities generate a finite subgroup of the orthogonal group. Assume that \(\dim_H F=s>1\), and fix \(\alpha\in(0,1]\). Consider a generic function \(f:F\to\mathbb{R}\) in the Baire-category sense among those satisfying the \(\alpha\)-Hölder bound \(|f(x)-f(y)|\le |x-y|^\alpha\) for all \(x,y\in F\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the upper box dimension of the level set \(f^{-1}(r)\) for Lebesgue almost every \(r\in f(F)\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: For a generic such \(f\) and for Lebesgue almost every \(r\in f(F)\), one has \(\overline{\dim}_B f^{-1}(r)=s-\alpha\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: For a generic such \(f\) and for Lebesgue almost every \(r\in f(F)\), one has \(\overline{\dim}_B f^{-1}(r)< s-\alpha\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: For a generic such \(f\) and for Lebesgue almost every \(r\in f(F)\), one has \(\overline{\dim}_B f^{-1}(r)\ge s-\alpha\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: For a generic such \(f\) and for every \(r\in f(F)\), one has \(\overline{\dim}_B f^{-1}(r)=s-\alpha\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every such \(f\) and for Lebesgue almost every \(r\in f(F)\), one has \(\overline{\dim}_B f^{-1}(r)=s-\alpha\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For a generic such \(f\) and for Lebesgue almost every \(r\in f(F)\), one has \(\overline{\dim}_B f^{-1}(r)=s-\alpha\).

theorem:
\label{thm-main}
	Let $X$ be a Euclidean  building.  Let $C\subset X$ be a convex subset of $X$
	which is isometric to a convex subset of some Euclidean space.  Then $C$ is contained in an apartment $A$  of $X$.  

	Moreover, if for some $x\in C$ we choose an apartment  $A^x$  in the infinitesimal Euclidean building  $T_xX$ with $T_xC\subset A^x$ then $A$ can be chosen so that $T _xA=A^x$.
sketch: Next, the authors prove the main theorem by enlarging the convex set step by step. To make this enlargement procedure work, they deal with the case of convex polyhedra $C$ first, and then deduce the result for general convex subsets by an appropriate approximation. They also note (contrasting with the spherical case) that in the Euclidean case one cannot rely on the finiteness of the simplicial decomposition of spherical apartments, which in the spherical setting allows for a choice of a potential enlargement $A$ of the convex subset $C$.
question: Let $X$ be a Euclidean building, where an apartment is a subspace arising from the building structure and isometric to a Euclidean space. Suppose $C\subset X$ is a convex subset that is isometric to a convex subset of some Euclidean space. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of a closed convex subset of $X$ containing $C$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every point $x\in C$ and every apartment $A^x\subset T_xX$ with $T_xC\subset A^x$, there exists a unique apartment $A\subset X$ such that $C\subset A$ and $T_xA=A^x$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a closed convex subset $Y\subset X$ that is isometric to some Euclidean space and satisfies $C\subset Y$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a family of apartments in $X$ such that every compact convex polyhedral subset of $C$ is contained in one of them, but no single apartment need contain all of $C$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Such an apartment is guaranteed only when $C$ is a convex polyhedron; for a general convex subset isometric to a Euclidean convex set, one can only obtain apartment containment for polyhedral approximations of $C$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{main}
Let $H$ be a split classical group over $F$, 
and let $\phi \in \Phi_\mathrm{temp}(H(F))$. 
Suppose that one of the following holds: 
\begin{description}
\item[(B)]
$H = \SO_{2n+1}$, $p > 6n+3$ and $\phi$ is arbitrary; 
\item[(C)]
$H = \Sp_{2n}$, $p > 6n$ and $\phi$ satisfies $(\star)$; 
\item[(D)]
$H = \SO_{2n}$, $p > 6n$ and $\phi$ satisfies $(\star)$. 
\end{description}
Set $\psi = \widehat\phi$. 

\begin{enumerate}
\item
There exists $\pi \in \Pi_\psi$ such that 
\[
d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi) \in \bar\mathcal{N}(\pi)^{\max}.
\]
\item
For $\pi \in \Pi_\psi$ and for $\oo_H^\mathrm{st} \in \bar\mathcal{N}(\pi)$, 
if $\oo_H^\mathrm{st} \not= d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$, then 
\[
\dim(\oo_H^\mathrm{st}) < \dim(d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)).
\]
Here, $\dim(\oo_H^\mathrm{st})$ is the Zariski dimension of the orbit as an algebraic variety.
\item
If we further assume the following hypothesis, then the corresponding upper-bound conjecture holds for $\phi$, i.e., 
\[
\bar\mathcal{N}(\pi)^{\max} \leq d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)
\]
for any $\pi \in \Pi_\psi$.

\begin{hyp}\label{hypo}
Let $\oo_H^\st \in \NN(\h(\overline{F}))$ be a special nilpotent orbit.
Define $\oo_G^\st \in \NN(\g^\theta(\overline{F}))$ by 
\[
\oo_G^\st = d_{G^\vee}(\Ad(G^\vee)d_{H}(\oo_H^\st)). 
\]
Then for any constants $\{c_{\oo_H} \;|\; \oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st\}$ such that 
$\sum_{\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st} c_{\oo_H} \hat\mu_{\oo_H}$ is stable, 
there exist constants $\{a_{\oo_G} \;|\; \oo_G \subset \oo_G^\st\}$ such that 
\[
\sum_{\oo_G \subset \oo_G^\st} a_{\oo_G} \hat\mu_{\oo_G}
\quad\text{is equal to the transfer of}\quad
\sum_{\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st} c_{\oo_H} \hat\mu_{\oo_H} 
\]
on a small enough neighborhood of $\theta \in G(F) \rtimes \theta$.
\end{hyp}

\begin{conj}\label{upper}
Let $\phi \in \Phi(H(F))$. 
Then for any $\pi \in \Pi_\phi$, we have
\[
\bar\NN(\hat\pi)^{\max} \leq d_{H^\vee}(\Ad(H^\vee)N_\phi).
\]
Moreover, if $\phi$ is tempered, 
then the equality holds for some $\pi \in \Pi_\phi$. 
\end{conj}
\end{enumerate}
sketch:
The proof of Theorem~\ref{main} follows Konno~\cite{K} and Varma~\cite{V}: the "main idea is to compare local character expansions via \emph{endoscopic character identities}." Fix $\phi\in\Phi_\mathrm{temp}(H(F))$, set $\psi=\widehat\phi$, and embed into $G=\GL_m$ via the standard map $H^\vee\to G^\vee$. This yields a self-dual $\pi_\psi$ of $G(F)$, extended to $\tilde\pi_\psi$ on $G\rtimes\langle\theta\rangle$, whose twisted character $\Theta_{\tilde\pi_\psi}$ matches Arthur’s stable distribution
\[
S\Theta_{\psi}=\sum_{\pi\in\Pi_\psi}\langle s_\psi,\pi\rangle_\psi\,\Theta_\pi
\]
by endoscopic character identities (Arthur’s (T1)), and similarly for $S\Theta_{\psi,s}$ (Arthur’s (T2)).

(1) "The first step" is an analogue in the twisted $\GL_m$ case: using Clozel’s twisted local character expansion
\[
\Theta_{\tilde\pi_\psi}=\sum_{\oo_G\in\mathcal N(\mathfrak g^\theta(F))} c_{\oo_G}(\tilde\pi_\psi)\,\hat\mu_{\oo_G},
\]
one defines $\bar\mathcal N_\theta(\tilde\pi_\psi)^{\max}$ and proves the following theorem.
\begin{thm}\label{twist}
Let $\phi \colon W_F \times \SL_2(\C) \rightarrow G^\vee$ 
be a self-dual tempered $L$-parameter for $G(F) = \GL_m(F)$, 
and let $\pi_\psi$ be the Zelevinsky dual of the irreducible tempered representation 
$\pi_\phi$ corresponding to $\phi$. 
Then 
\[
\bar\NN_\theta(\tl\pi_\psi)^{\max} = \{d_{G^\vee}(\Ad(G^\vee) N_\phi)\}.
\]
Moreover, $c_{\oo_G}(\tl\pi_\psi) = c_{\oo_G'}(\tl\pi_\psi)$ 
for any rational orbits $\oo_G$ and $\oo_G'$ contained in $d_{G^\vee}(\Ad(G^\vee) N_\phi)$.
\end{thm}
This uses results of Konno, Varma, and M\oe glin--Waldspurger.

(2) Setting $\oo_H^{\mathrm{st}}=d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$ and $\oo_G^{\mathrm{st}}=d_{G^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(G^\vee)N_\phi)$, "the second step" is the following transfer statement.
\begin{thm}\label{transfer}
Let $\oo_H^\st \in \NN(\h(\overline{F}))$ be a special nilpotent orbit.
Define $\oo_G^\st \in \NN(\g^\theta(\overline{F}))$ by 
\[
\oo_G^\st = d_{G^\vee}(\Ad(G^\vee)d_{H}(\oo_H^\st)). 
\]
Then for each rational orbit $\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st$, 
there exists a constant $\gamma_{\oo_H} \in \C$ such that 
\[
\sum_{\oo_G \subset \oo_G^\st} \hat\mu_{\oo_G}
\quad\text{is equal to the transfer of }\quad
\sum_{\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st} \gamma_{\oo_H} \hat\mu_{\oo_H}
\]
on a small enough neighborhood of $\theta \in G(F) \rtimes \theta$.
\end{thm}
The following hypothesis is described as an analogue of this transfer result.
\begin{hyp}\label{hypo}
Let $\oo_H^\st \in \NN(\h(\overline{F}))$ be a special nilpotent orbit.
Define $\oo_G^\st \in \NN(\g^\theta(\overline{F}))$ by 
\[
\oo_G^\st = d_{G^\vee}(\Ad(G^\vee)d_{H}(\oo_H^\st)). 
\]
Then for any constants $\{c_{\oo_H} \;|\; \oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st\}$ such that 
$\sum_{\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st} c_{\oo_H} \hat\mu_{\oo_H}$ is stable, 
there exist constants $\{a_{\oo_G} \;|\; \oo_G \subset \oo_G^\st\}$ such that 
\[
\sum_{\oo_G \subset \oo_G^\st} a_{\oo_G} \hat\mu_{\oo_G}
\quad\text{is equal to the transfer of}\quad
\sum_{\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st} c_{\oo_H} \hat\mu_{\oo_H} 
\]
on a small enough neighborhood of $\theta \in G(F) \rtimes \theta$.
\end{hyp}

(3) "The third step" relates Waldspurger’s explicit map for standard endoscopy,
\[
\oo_H^{\mathrm{st}}=W(\oo_{H_1}^{\mathrm{st}},\oo_{H_2}^{\mathrm{st}}),
\]
to Spaltenstein duality using the proposition below.
\begin{prop}\label{WS}
For $i \in \{1,2\}$, let $\oo_{H_i}^\st \in \NN(\h_i(\overline{F}))$ be a special nilpotent orbit.
Then 
\[
W(\oo_{H_1}^\st, \oo_{H_2}^\st) 
\leq d_{H^\vee}(\Ad(H^\vee)\xi(d_{H_1}(\oo_{H_1}^\st), d_{H_2}(\oo_{H_2}^\st))). 
\]
\end{prop}

Finally, "combining the two transfer results," one relates the local character expansions of $S\Theta_\psi$ and $S\Theta_{\psi,s}$ to those of $\Theta_{\tilde\pi_\psi}$ and $S\Theta_{\psi_1}\otimes S\Theta_{\psi_2}$, and then "by induction on $m$" establishes the main theorem (later in the paper). The condition $(\star)$ in Cases (C) and (D) is needed to apply Waldspurger’s theorem, equivalently ensuring the relevant endoscopic groups $H_1\times H_2$ are unramified.
question: Let $H$ be a split classical group over a nonarchimedean local field $F$ with Lie algebra $\mathfrak h$, and let $\phi\in\Phi_{\mathrm{temp}}(H(F))$ be a tempered $L$-parameter. Assume one of the following: (i) $H=\mathrm{SO}_{2n+1}$, $p>6n+3$, and $\phi$ is arbitrary; (ii) $H=\mathrm{Sp}_{2n}$, $p>6n$, and $\phi$ satisfies $(\star)$; or (iii) $H=\mathrm{SO}_{2n}$, $p>6n$, and $\phi$ satisfies $(\star)$. Set $\psi=\widehat\phi$, and let $\Pi_\psi$ be the $L$-packet attached to $\psi$. If $d\phi|_{\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb C)}:\mathfrak{sl}_2(\mathbb C)\to \mathfrak h^\vee$ is the derivative of $\phi|_{\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb C)}$, let $N_\phi\in\mathfrak h^\vee$ be the image of $\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$, and let $d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$ be the stable nilpotent orbit in $\mathfrak h(\overline F)$ obtained from the adjoint orbit of $N_\phi$ in the dual group $H^\vee$ via the Spaltenstein duality map $d_{H^\vee}$. For $\pi\in\Pi_\psi$, let $\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ denote the set of stable nilpotent orbits in $\mathfrak h(\overline F)$ attached to $\pi$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the dimensions of the orbits in $\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ under these hypotheses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every $\pi\in\Pi_\psi$, there exists some $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}\in\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ such that
\[
\dim(\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}})=\dim\bigl(d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)\bigr).
\]
In other words, each member of the packet has a stable nilpotent orbit of the same dimension as $d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists $\pi\in\Pi_\psi$ such that for every $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}\in\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$, the equality
\[
\dim(\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}})=\dim\bigl(d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)\bigr)
\]
can occur only when $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}=d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There is a stable nilpotent orbit $\mathcal O_0^{\mathrm{st}}$, independent of $\pi$, such that for every $\pi\in\Pi_\psi$ and every $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}\in\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ one has
\[
\dim(\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}})\leq \dim(\mathcal O_0^{\mathrm{st}}),
\]
with equality only when $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}=\mathcal O_0^{\mathrm{st}}$; moreover one may take $\mathcal O_0^{\mathrm{st}}=d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every $\pi\in\Pi_\psi$, every orbit $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}\in\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)^{\max}$ satisfies
\[
\dim(\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}})=\dim\bigl(d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)\bigr),
\]
so the maximal dimension occurring in $\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ is always the dimension of $d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{thm:DKS2} \cite{DKS2} Let $M=S^2(q_1/p_1,q_2,p_2,q_3/p_3)$ be a Seifert manifold and assume $e(M)\neq 0.$ Then 
	$$|X(M)|=p_1^+ p_2^+ p_3^-  + p_1^- p_2^- p_3^- + \frac{1}{2}(|H_1(M,\Z)| +|H_1(M,\Z/2\Z)|)-x_M$$
	where $p_i^+=\lceil \frac{p_i}{2} \rceil-1$ and $p_i^-=\lfloor \frac{p_i}{2} \rfloor$ and $x_M$ is the number of exceptional abelian characters. Moreover, a point $[\rho]$ in $X(M)$ is reduced, except possibly if $[\rho]$ is an exceptional abelian character.,
question:
Let \(M=S^2(q_1/p_1,q_2/p_2,q_3/p_3)\) be a Seifert manifold with \(e(M)\neq 0\). Let
\[X(M)=\operatorname{Hom}(\pi_1(M),\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb C))//\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb C)\]
denote the \(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb C)\)-character variety of \(M\). For \(i=1,2,3\), define \(p_i^+=\lceil p_i/2\rceil-1\) and \(p_i^-=\lfloor p_i/2\rfloor\), and let \(x_M\) denote the number of exceptional abelian characters. Writing \(|A|\) for the cardinality of a finite set or group \(A\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(X(M)\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: \[|X(M)|=p_1^+p_2^-p_3^-+p_1^-p_2^+p_3^-+\frac{1}{2}\bigl(|H_1(M,\mathbb Z)|+|H_1(M,\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)|\bigr)-x_M.\]

choices.label: C
choices.text: \[|X(M)|\le p_1^+p_2^+p_3^-+p_1^-p_2^-p_3^-+\frac{1}{2}\bigl(|H_1(M,\mathbb Z)|+|H_1(M,\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)|\bigr).\]

choices.label: D
choices.text: \[|X(M)|=p_1^+p_2^+p_3^-+p_1^-p_2^-p_3^-+\frac{1}{2}\bigl(|H_1(M,\mathbb Z)|+|H_1(M,\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)|\bigr)-2x_M.\]

choices.label: E
choices.text: \[|X(M)|=p_1^+p_2^+p_3^-+p_1^-p_2^-p_3^-+\frac{1}{2}\bigl(|H_1(M,\mathbb Z)|+|H_1(M,\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)|\bigr)-x_M,\]\text{ and }[\rho]\in X(M)\text{ is reduced if and only if }[\rho]\text{ is not an exceptional abelian character.}

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question: Let $M$ be a closed hyperbolic $3$-manifold with a pseudo-Anosov flow $\varphi$, and let $S\subset M$ be a closed connected surface that is almost transverse to $\varphi$ but is not a fiber (that is, not a fiber of a fibration $M\to S^1$). Let $c^s$ and $c^u$ be the stable and unstable multicurves of $S$, namely the essential multicurves coming from the closed leaves of the singular foliations on $S$ induced by the stable/unstable foliations of $\varphi$. Write $M\!\backslash\!\backslash S$ for the manifold obtained by cutting $M$ open along $S$, and assume the numerical invariant $\mathfrak c(M\!\backslash\!\backslash S)$ is finite. Here $d_{\mathcal C(S)}$ denotes distance in the curve complex of $S$, $\mathrm{vol}(M)$ is the hyperbolic volume of $M$, and $\ell_M(\gamma)$ is the hyperbolic length of a closed geodesic $\gamma\subset M$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the uniform existence of bounds for $d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u)$ in terms of $\mathrm{vol}(M)$ and $\ell_M(\gamma)$ under these assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There is a constant \(k_S = k_S(\chi(S)) \ge 1\), depending only on \(\chi(S)\), and a constant \(k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S} \ge 1\), depending only on \(\chi(S)\) and \(\mathfrak c(M\backslash\!\!\backslash S)\), so that the following inequalities hold: \(d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u) \le k_S\,\mathrm{vol}(M)+k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S}\), and \(d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u) \le k_S\,\ell_M(\gamma)+k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S}\) for every closed geodesic \(\gamma\subset M\), whether or not \(\gamma\) intersects \(S\) essentially.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There is a constant \(K=K(\chi(S),\mathfrak c(M\backslash\!\!\backslash S))\ge 1\) such that \(d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u) \le K\,(\mathrm{vol}(M)+1)\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is a constant \(k_S = k_S(\chi(S)) \ge 1\), depending only on \(\chi(S)\), and a constant \(k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S} \ge 1\), depending only on \(\mathfrak c(M\backslash\!\!\backslash S)\), so that the following inequalities hold: \(d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u) \le k_S\,\mathrm{vol}(M)+k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S}\), and \(d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u) \le k_S\,\ell_M(\gamma)+k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S}\) whenever \(\gamma\) is a closed geodesic that intersects \(S\) essentially.

choices.label: E
choices.text: There is a constant \(k_S = k_S(\chi(S)) \ge 1\), depending only on \(\chi(S)\), and a constant \(k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S} \ge 1\), depending only on \(\chi(S)\) and \(\mathfrak c(M\backslash\!\!\backslash S)\), so that the following inequalities hold: \(d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u) \le k_S\,\mathrm{vol}(M)\), and \(d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u) \le k_S\,\ell_M(\gamma)+k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S}\) whenever \(\gamma\) is a closed geodesic that intersects \(S\) essentially.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question:
Assume that \(g\in C_b^5\) (that is, \(g:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is \(C^5\) with bounded derivatives) and that the initial condition \(\psi\in C^\theta(\mathbb T^2)\) for some \(\theta\in(0,1/4)\). Let \(\rho:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R\) be smooth, radial, nonnegative, supported on the unit ball, and such that \(\int_{\mathbb R^2}\rho=1\). Set \(\rho_\varepsilon(x)=\varepsilon^{-2}\rho(\varepsilon^{-1}x)\) and define \(\xi_\varepsilon=\rho_\varepsilon*\xi\). For each \(\varepsilon>0\), let \(u_\varepsilon\) be the solution on the two-dimensional torus \(\mathbb T^2\) with initial condition \(\psi\) of
\[
\partial_t u_\varepsilon = \Delta u_\varepsilon + g(u_\varepsilon)\,\varepsilon^{1/2}\,\partial_{x_1}\xi_\varepsilon - C_\varepsilon g'g(u_\varepsilon)-\bar C_\varepsilon (g')^3 g(u_\varepsilon)-\hat C_\varepsilon g''g'g^2(u_\varepsilon).
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the limit of \(u_\varepsilon\) as \(\varepsilon\to 0\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exist sequences of constants \(C_\varepsilon,\bar C_\varepsilon,\hat C_\varepsilon\) and constants \(c_\rho,\lambda_\rho>0\) such that \(u_\varepsilon\) converges in law as \(\varepsilon\to0\) in \(C([0,1]\times\mathbb T^2)\) to the renormalized solution \(u\) of
\[
\partial_t u=\Delta u+c_\rho g'g(u)\,\eta-\lambda_\rho g''g'g^2(u)
\]
on \(\mathbb T^2\) with initial condition \(\psi\), where \(\eta\) is a spatial white noise.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exist sequences of constants \(C_\varepsilon,\bar C_\varepsilon,\hat C_\varepsilon\) and a constant \(c_\rho>0\) such that for every sequence \(\varepsilon_n\to0\), one can extract a subsequence \(\varepsilon_{n_k}\to0\) for which \(u_{\varepsilon_{n_k}}\) converges in law in \(C([0,1]\times\mathbb T^2)\) to the renormalized solution \(u\) of
\[
\partial_t u=\Delta u+c_\rho g'g(u)\,\eta
\]
on \(\mathbb T^2\) with initial condition \(\psi\), where \(\eta\) is a spatial white noise.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every admissible mollifier \(\rho\), the same sequences of constants \(C_\varepsilon,\bar C_\varepsilon,\hat C_\varepsilon\), independent of \(\rho\), can be chosen together with a constant \(c_\rho>0\) so that \(u_\varepsilon\) converges in law as \(\varepsilon\to0\) in \(C([0,1]\times\mathbb T^2)\) to the renormalized solution \(u\) of
\[
\partial_t u=\Delta u+c_\rho g'g(u)\,\eta
\]
on \(\mathbb T^2\) with initial condition \(\psi\), where \(\eta\) is a spatial white noise.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exist sequences of constants \(C_\varepsilon,\bar C_\varepsilon,\hat C_\varepsilon\) and a constant \(c_\rho>0\) such that, with all \(u_\varepsilon\) constructed from the same underlying noise \(\xi\), one has \(u_\varepsilon\to u\) in probability in \(C([0,1]\times\mathbb T^2)\), where \(u\) is the renormalized solution of
\[
\partial_t u=\Delta u+c_\rho g'g(u)\,\eta
\]
on \(\mathbb T^2\) with initial condition \(\psi\), and \(\eta\) is a spatial white noise.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

sketch:
Their proof (of the main theorem) “can be viewed as showing that the above identity is a consequence of the \emph{confluence relation}.”

The text then indicates that the main theorem is recovered by specializing the following theorem to $w_1=z_2^{a-1}z_3z_2^{c-1}$ and $w_2=z_2^{b-1}z_3$.

\begin{theorem}[{Hirose--Sato~\cite[Proposition~14]{HiroseSato2022+}}]\label{thm:HiroseSato2}
For $w, w'\in\cH^{2,3}$, we have
\[
Z(w_1\tau(w_2))=Z(w_1\star w_2).
\]
\end{theorem}

It is also stated that Hirose--Sato “derived the theorem above from their \emph{block shuffle identity}.”
question: Let \(\zeta(k_1,\dots,k_r)=\sum_{n_1>\cdots>n_r\ge 1} n_1^{-k_1}\cdots n_r^{-k_r}\) denote the multiple zeta value, and let \(\{2\}^m\) mean a string of \(m\) copies of \(2\) (with \(\{2\}^0\) the empty string, which is omitted from the list of arguments). For every choice of positive integers \(a,b,c\), which identity is guaranteed to hold?

Let $d\in\{2,3\}$ and consider the compressible elastodynamics Cauchy problem on $\mathbb{R}^d\times[0,\infty)$
\[
\begin{cases}
\partial_t\rho+\operatorname{div}(\rho u)=0,\\
\partial_t(\rho u)+\operatorname{div}(\rho u\otimes u)+\nabla P(\rho)=\operatorname{div}(\rho F F^\top),\\
\partial_t F+u\cdot\nabla F=\nabla u\,F,\\
(\rho,u,F)|_{t=0}=(\rho_0,u_0,F_0),
\end{cases}
\]
where $P(\rho)=\rho^\gamma$ with $\gamma>1$, $I$ is the identity matrix, and $H_x^k=H^k(\mathbb{R}^d)$, $L_x^2=L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)$. Assume
\[
(\rho_0-1,\,u_0-x,\,F_0-I)\in H_x^3(\mathbb{R}^d),
\]
and that for some diffeomorphism $\xi_0:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^d$ and every $x\in\mathbb{R}^d$,
\[
\rho_0(x)\det F_0(x)=1,\qquad F_0(x)=\nabla_y\xi_0(y),\qquad y=\xi_0^{-1}(x),
\]
together with
\[
\|\rho_0-1\|_{H_x^3}+\|u_0-x\|_{H_x^3}+\|F_0-I\|_{H_x^3}+\|\xi_0^{-1}-x\|_{H_x^4}\le \varepsilon.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the uniform global existence, uniqueness, and decay of solutions $(\rho,u,F)$ under these assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
There exists a diffeomorphism $\xi_0:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^d$ and a small constant $\varepsilon>0$ such that, for every initial datum $(\rho_0,u_0,F_0)$ satisfying the stated compatibility and smallness conditions, the Cauchy problem admits a unique global strong solution $(\rho,u,F)$ with
\[
\Big(\rho(x,t)-\frac{1}{(1+t)^d},\;u(x,t)-\frac{x}{1+t},\;F(x,t)-(1+t)I\Big)\in C\big([0,\infty);H_x^3(\mathbb{R}^d)\big),
\]
and for all $t>0$ and $i=0,1,2,3$,
\[
\|\nabla_x^i(\rho(x,t)-\tfrac{1}{(1+t)^d})\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{-\frac d2-\frac12-i},
\]
\[
\|\nabla_x^i\big(u(x,t)-\tfrac{x}{1+t},\,F(x,t)-(1+t)I\big)\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{\frac d2+\frac12-i},
\]
where $C$ is a positive constant depending only on fixed known constants and is independent of $\varepsilon$ and $t$.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exists a diffeomorphism $\xi_0:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^d$ and a small constant $\varepsilon>0$ such that, for every initial datum $(\rho_0,u_0,F_0)$ satisfying the stated compatibility and smallness conditions, the Cauchy problem admits a unique global strong solution $(\rho,u,F)$ with
\[
\Big(\rho(x,t)-\frac{1}{(1+t)^d},\;u(x,t)-\frac{x}{1+t},\;F(x,t)-(1+t)I\Big)\in C\big([0,\infty);H_x^3(\mathbb{R}^d)\big),
\]
and for all $t>0$ and $i=0,1,2,3$,
\[
\|\nabla_x^i(\rho(x,t)-\tfrac{1}{(1+t)^d})\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{-\frac d2-i},
\]
\[
\|\nabla_x^i\big(u(x,t)-\tfrac{x}{1+t},\,F(x,t)-(1+t)I\big)\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{\frac d2+\frac12-i},
\]
where $C$ is a positive constant depending only on fixed known constants and is independent of $\varepsilon$ and $t$.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists a diffeomorphism $\xi_0:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^d$ and a small constant $\varepsilon>0$ such that, for every initial datum $(\rho_0,u_0,F_0)$ satisfying the stated compatibility and smallness conditions, the Cauchy problem admits a unique global strong solution $(\rho,u,F)$ with
\[
\Big(\rho(x,t)-\frac{1}{(1+t)^d},\;u(x,t)-\frac{x}{1+t},\;F(x,t)-(1+t)I\Big)\in C\big([0,\infty);H_x^3(\mathbb{R}^d)\big),
\]
and there is a positive constant $C$, independent of $\varepsilon$ and $t$, such that for all $t>0$,
\[
\|\rho(x,t)-\tfrac{1}{(1+t)^d}\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{-\frac d2-\frac12},
\]
\[
\|u(x,t)-\tfrac{x}{1+t}\|_{L_x^2}+\|F(x,t)-(1+t)I\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{\frac d2+\frac12}.
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exists a small constant $\varepsilon>0$ such that, for every initial datum $(\rho_0,u_0,F_0)$ satisfying
\[
(\rho_0-1,\,u_0-x,\,F_0-I)\in H_x^3(\mathbb{R}^d),\qquad \rho_0(x)\det F_0(x)=1\ \text{for all }x\in\mathbb{R}^d,
\]
\[
\operatorname{div}(\rho_0 F_0^{\top})=0,
\]
and
\[
\|\rho_0-1\|_{H_x^3}+\|u_0-x\|_{H_x^3}+\|F_0-I\|_{H_x^3}\le \varepsilon,
\]
the Cauchy problem admits a unique global strong solution $(\rho,u,F)$ with
\[
\Big(\rho(x,t)-\frac{1}{(1+t)^d},\;u(x,t)-\frac{x}{1+t},\;F(x,t)-(1+t)I\Big)\in C\big([0,\infty);H_x^3(\mathbb{R}^d)\big),
\]
and for all $t>0$ and $i=0,1,2,3$,
\[
\|\nabla_x^i(\rho(x,t)-\tfrac{1}{(1+t)^d})\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{-\frac d2-\frac12-i},
\]
\[
\|\nabla_x^i\big(u(x,t)-\tfrac{x}{1+t},\,F(x,t)-(1+t)I\big)\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{\frac d2+\frac12-i},
\]
where $C$ is independent of $\varepsilon$ and $t$.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists a diffeomorphism $\xi_0:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^d$ and a small constant $\varepsilon>0$ such that, for every initial datum $(\rho_0,u_0,F_0)$ satisfying the stated compatibility and smallness conditions, the Cauchy problem admits a unique global strong solution $(\rho,u,F)$ with
\[
\Big(\rho(x,t)-\frac{1}{(1+t)^d},\;u(x,t)-\frac{x}{1+t},\;F(x,t)-(1+t)I\Big)\in C\big([0,\infty);H_x^3(\mathbb{R}^d)\big),
\]
and for all $t>0$ and $i=0,1,2,3$,
\[
\|\nabla_x^i(\rho(x,t)-\tfrac{1}{(1+t)^d})\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{-\frac d2-\frac12-i},
\]
\[
\|\nabla_x^i\big(u(x,t)-\tfrac{x}{1+t},\,F(x,t)-(1+t)I\big)\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{\frac d2-\frac12-i},
\]
where $C$ is a positive constant depending only on fixed known constants and is independent of $\varepsilon$ and $t$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
There exists a diffeomorphism $\xi_0:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^d$ and a small constant $\varepsilon>0$ such that, for every initial datum $(\rho_0,u_0,F_0)$ satisfying the stated compatibility and smallness conditions, the Cauchy problem admits a unique global strong solution $(\rho,u,F)$ with
\[
\Big(\rho(x,t)-\frac{1}{(1+t)^d},\;u(x,t)-\frac{x}{1+t},\;F(x,t)-(1+t)I\Big)\in C\big([0,\infty);H_x^3(\mathbb{R}^d)\big),
\]
and for all $t>0$ and $i=0,1,2,3$,

\|\nabla_x^i(\rho(x,t)-\tfrac{1}{(1+t)^d})\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{-\frac d2-\frac12-i},
\]
\[
\|\nabla_x^i\big(u(x,t)-\tfrac{x}{1+t},\,F(x,t)-(1+t)I\big)\|_{L_x^2}\le C\varepsilon(1+t)^{\frac d2+\frac12-i},
