question: For each positive integer $n$, let $[n]=\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$. The empty word and every one-letter word are both Andr\'e I and Andr\'e II. For $n\ge 2$, if a permutation $\sigma$ of $[n]$ is written as $\sigma=\tau\,\min(\sigma)\,\tau'$, then $\sigma$ is an Andr\'e I permutation (respectively, an Andr\'e II permutation) if both $\tau$ and $\tau'$ are Andr\'e I (respectively, Andr\'e II) and the maximum (respectively, minimum) letter of the subword $\tau\tau'$ lies in $\tau'$. Let $\AndI_n$ and $\AndII_n$ denote the sets of Andr\'e I and Andr\'e II permutations of $[n]$, respectively. Let $\RS_n$ denote the set of simsun permutations of $[n]$, where a permutation $\sigma=\sigma_1\cdots\sigma_n$ is simsun if $\sigma_n=n$, it has no double descents, and this remains true after successively removing $n,n-1,\ldots,1$; here a double descent is an index $i$ with $\sigma_i>\sigma_{i+1}>\sigma_{i+2}$. For a permutation $\sigma$, write $\des(\sigma)$ for its number of descents, $\ides(\sigma)=\des(\sigma^{-1})$ for its number of inverse descents, and $\maj(\sigma)$ for its major index (the sum of its descent positions). Which statement holds for every $n$?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
The joint distribution of $(\ides,\des,\maj)$ is the same on $\AndI_n$, $\AndII_n$, and $\RS_n$; equivalently,
$$
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndI_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndII_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\RS_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}.
$$

choices.label: B
choices.text:
The joint distribution of $(\ides,\des,\maj)$ is the same on $\AndI_n$, $\AndII_n$, and $\RS_n$ for each fixed descent set; equivalently, for every subset $D\subseteq [n-1]$,
$$
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\AndI_n\\ \Des(\sigma)=D}} s^{\ides(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\AndII_n\\ \Des(\sigma)=D}} s^{\ides(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\RS_n\\ \Des(\sigma)=D}} s^{\ides(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}.
$$

choices.label: C
choices.text:
The bivariate statistic $(\ides,\des)$ is equidistributed on $\AndI_n$, $\AndII_n$, and $\RS_n$; equivalently,
$$
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndI_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndII_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\RS_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)}.
$$

choices.label: D
choices.text:
The joint distribution of $(\ides,\des,\maj)$ is the same on $\AndI_n$ and $\AndII_n$, and moreover the pair $(\ides,\des)$ is equidistributed with $\RS_n$; equivalently,
$$
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndI_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndII_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)},
\qquad
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndII_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\RS_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)}.
$$

choices.label: E
choices.text:
The joint distribution of $(\ides,\des,\maj)$ is the same on $\AndI_n$, $\AndII_n$, and $\RS_n$ after interchanging descents and inverse descents on the simsun side; equivalently,
$$
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndI_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndII_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\RS_n} s^{\des(\sigma)} t^{\ides(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}.
$$

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
The joint distribution of $(\ides,\des,\maj)$ is the same on $\AndI_n$, $\AndII_n$, and $\RS_n$; equivalently,
$$
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndI_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndII_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\RS_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}.
$$

theorem: Let $A_2,A_3,\ldots, A_m$ be a finite sequence of finitely generated abelian groups. Then there is a finite monoid $M$ with simply connected classifying space $BM$ such that $H_n(BM)\cong A_n$ for $2\leq n\leq m$, and $H_n(BM)=0$ for $n>m$.
sketch:
A wedge sum of Moore spaces $M(A_n,n)$ ($2\leq n\leq m$) is a finite simply connected CW complex with the homology prescribed in Theorem~A. Moreover, a simply connected CW complex is homotopy equivalent to such a wedge of Moore spaces iff the Hurewicz maps $h_n\colon \pi_n(X,x_0)\to H_n(X)$ are split epimorphisms for all $n\geq 2$; hence Theorem~A is reduced to proving Theorem~B.

For Theorem~B, the stated strategy is: (i) show closure (up to homotopy equivalence) of classifying spaces of finite monoids under suspension and wedge sum; (ii) realize $M(C,2)$ as the classifying space of a finite monoid for any cyclic group $C$. Suspensions were already realized in~\cite{homotopysmall} (including an $M(C_2,2)$ example), and since $S^2\simeq BM$ for a finite monoid~\cite{Fiedor}, this also yields $M(\mathbb Z,2)$.

The obstacle is that realizing wedge sums via the free product “immediately takes you out of the class of finite monoids.” The paper’s new input is that if $M,N$ are finite monoids with minimal ideals rectangular bands, then $BM\vee BN$ is homotopy equivalent to $B(\text{a finite monoid})$ whose minimal ideal is a rectangular band. Since Sweney’s suspension construction~\cite{homologicalepi} always produces monoids with minimal ideal a rectangular band, it then “remains to construct” such a finite monoid with rectangular-band minimal ideal whose classifying space is $M(C,2)$ for each cyclic group $C$, which the authors do.
question: Let $m\ge 2$ be an integer, and let $A_2,A_3,\ldots,A_m$ be finitely generated abelian groups. Here $BM$ denotes the classifying space of a monoid $M$, and a finite monoid means a monoid with finitely many elements. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of a finite monoid $M$ with prescribed homology groups of $BM$ under these assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There exists a finite monoid $M$ such that $BM$ is simply connected, $H_n(BM)\cong A_n$ for every $2\le n\le m$, and $H_n(BM)=0$ for every $n>m$.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists a finite monoid $M$ such that $BM$ is simply connected, $H_n(BM)\cong A_n$ for every $2\le n<m$, $H_m(BM)=0$, and $H_n(BM)=0$ for every $n>m$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a finite monoid $M$ such that $BM$ is simply connected and $H_n(BM)\cong A_n$ for every $2\le n\le m$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a finite monoid $M$ such that $BM$ is simply connected, $H_n(BM)\cong A_n$ for every $2\le n\le m$, and $H_n(BM)=0$ for every $n\ge m$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For each $n$ with $2\le n\le m$, there exists a finite monoid $M_n$ such that $BM_n$ is simply connected, $H_n(BM_n)\cong A_n$, and $H_k(BM_n)=0$ for every $k>n$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: There exists a finite monoid $M$ such that $BM$ is simply connected, $H_n(BM)\cong A_n$ for every $2\le n\le m$, and $H_n(BM)=0$ for every $n>m$.

question: For a positive integer \(N\), let \(X_0(N)\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) be the modular curve classifying isomorphism classes of pairs \((E,C)\), where \(E\) is an elliptic curve and \(C \subset E\) is a cyclic subgroup of order \(N\). A closed point \(x\) of \(X_0(N)\) is called a CM point if \(E\) has complex multiplication, and it is called sporadic if there are only finitely many closed points of \(X_0(N)\) of degree over \(\mathbb{Q}\) at most \(\deg(x)\). Which existence statement holds for sporadic CM points on \(X_0(N)\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
The modular curve $X_0(N)$ has a sporadic CM point if and only if $N\notin S_{CM}$, where
\[
S_{CM}:=\{1-13, 15-18, 20-26, 30-33, 35-37, 39-41, 46-50, 53, 59-61, 65, 70-72, 79, 80, 83, 87, 89, 94, 96, 101, 131, 144\},
\]
and $X_0(N)$ admits no degree-$2$ morphism to a curve of genus $0$ or $1$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{thm1.1} Suppose $\left( f_1^{\prime }\right) \left( f_2\right) \left(
f_3\right) $ hold, then the functional $J$ possesses an unbounded sequence $\left\{ u_k\right\} _{k=1}^\infty $ of critical points satisfying

$\left( i\right) $ $M\left( J,u_k\right) \rightarrow \infty $ as $
k\rightarrow \infty $.

$\left( ii\right) $ $J\left( u_k\right) \rightarrow \infty $ as $
k\rightarrow \infty $.
question: Assume that the hypotheses \((f_1'), (f_2), (f_3)\) hold for the energy functional \(J\) considered on \(X=H_0^1(\Omega)\). Here, an unbounded sequence means a sequence that is not bounded in \(X\), and for a critical point \(u\), \(M(J,u)\) denotes the generalized Morse index, i.e. the sum of the dimensions of the negative and null eigenspaces of \(J''(u)\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the critical points of \(J\) under these assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The functional \(J\) has an unbounded sequence of critical points \(\{u_k\}_{k=1}^\infty\) such that \(M(J,u_k) \to \infty\) and \(J(u_k) \to \infty\) as \(k \to \infty\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The functional \(J\) has an unbounded sequence of critical points \(\{u_k\}_{k=1}^\infty\) such that \(M(J,u_k)\ge k\) for every \(k\), and the sequence of critical values \(\{J(u_k)\}\) converges to a finite real number.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The functional \(J\) has a sequence of critical points \(\{u_k\}_{k=1}^\infty\) such that \(M(J,u_k) \to \infty\) as \(k\to\infty\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: For each integer \(k\ge 1\), the functional \(J\) has a critical point \(u_k\) with generalized Morse index exactly \(M(J,u_k)=k\), and \(J(u_k)\to\infty\) as \(k\to\infty\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: The functional \(J\) has an unbounded sequence of critical points \(\{u_k\}_{k=1}^\infty\) such that \(J(u_k)\to\infty\) and the dimension of the null eigenspace of \(J''(u_k)\) is at least \(k\) for every \(k\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: The functional \(J\) has an unbounded sequence of critical points \(\{u_k\}_{k=1}^\infty\) such that \(M(J,u_k) \to \infty\) and \(J(u_k) \to \infty\) as \(k \to \infty\).

theorem:
\label{thm:chordarc}
    Let $3\leq n\leq 5$, \(\Sigma^n \subset \mathbb{R}^{n+1}\) be a two-sided, embedded minimal disk with \(\sup_{\Sigma}|A| < \infty\).
    Then there exists a constant \(c = c\bigl(n,\sup_{\Sigma}|A|\bigr) > 0\) with the following property: for every \(x\in \Sigma\) and every \(R>1\) such that the intrinsic ball \(\mathcal{B}_{R}(x)\subset \Sigma\), the connected component \(\Sigma_{x, R}\) of the Euclidean ball \(B_{R}(x)\cap \Sigma\) containing \(x\) satisfies
    \[
        \Sigma_{x, R}\subset \mathcal{B}_{cR^n}(x).
    \]
    In particular, intrinsic distances are quantitatively controlled by extrinsic distances on \(\Sigma_{x, R}\), with constants depending only on the dimension and the curvature bound.
question: Let $3\le n\le 5$, and let $\Sigma^n\subset \mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ be a two-sided embedded minimal disk (i.e. an embedded minimal hypersurface diffeomorphic to the $n$-dimensional unit disk, with a globally defined unit normal) with $\sup_{\Sigma}|A|<\infty$, where $|A|$ is the norm of the second fundamental form. For $x\in\Sigma$, write $\mathcal{B}_r(x)$ for the intrinsic geodesic ball in $\Sigma$ of radius $r$ (with respect to the induced metric), and write $B_R(x)$ for the Euclidean ball in $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ of radius $R$. If $\Sigma_{x,R}$ denotes the connected component of $B_R(x)\cap \Sigma$ containing $x$, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the containment of $\Sigma_{x,R}$ in an intrinsic geodesic ball under these assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
There exists a constant $c=c\bigl(n,\sup_{\Sigma}|A|\bigr)>0$ such that for every $x\in\Sigma$ and every $R>1$ with $\mathcal{B}_R(x)\subset \Sigma$, one has
\[
\Sigma_{x,R}\subset \mathcal{B}_{cR^n}(x).
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exists a constant $c=c\bigl(n,\sup_{\Sigma}|A|\bigr)>0$ such that for every $x\in\Sigma$ and every $R>1$ with $\mathcal{B}_R(x)\subset \Sigma$, one has
\[
B_R(x)\cap \Sigma\subset \mathcal{B}_{cR^n}(x).
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists a function $\rho:[1,\infty)\to(0,\infty)$, depending only on $n$ and $\sup_{\Sigma}|A|$, such that for every $x\in\Sigma$ and every $R>1$ with $\mathcal{B}_R(x)\subset \Sigma$, one has
\[
\Sigma_{x,R}\subset \mathcal{B}_{\rho(R)}(x).
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exists a constant $c=c(n)>0$ such that for every $x\in\Sigma$ and every $R>1$ with $\mathcal{B}_R(x)\subset \Sigma$, one has
\[
\Sigma_{x,R}\subset \mathcal{B}_{cR^n}(x).
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists a constant $c=c\bigl(n,\sup_{\Sigma}|A|\bigr)>0$ such that for every $x\in\Sigma$ and every $R>1$, one has
\[
\Sigma_{x,R}\subset \mathcal{B}_{cR^n}(x).
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
There exists a constant $c=c\bigl(n,\sup_{\Sigma}|A|\bigr)>0$ such that for every $x\in\Sigma$ and every $R>1$ with $\mathcal{B}_R(x)\subset \Sigma$, one has
\[
\Sigma_{x,R}\subset \mathcal{B}_{cR^n}(x).
\]

question:
Let \(0<\mu<1\), let \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{R}^2\) have smooth boundary \(\partial\Omega\), and let \(n_x\) denote the outward unit normal at \(x\in\partial\Omega\). Write \(\Gamma:=\{(x,v)\in \partial\Omega\times\mathbb{R}^2: v\in T_x\partial\Omega\}\), let \(C^{1,\mu}_0(\bar\Omega\times\mathbb{R}^2)\) denote \(C^{1,\mu}\) functions with compact support, and let \({\rm d}l\) denote arc-length measure on \(\partial\Omega\). Suppose \(f_0\in C^{1,\mu}_0(\bar\Omega\times\mathbb{R}^2)\) is nonnegative and satisfies \(f_0(x,v)=\text{constant}\) whenever \(\operatorname{dist}((x,v),\Gamma)\le \delta_0\), and let \(h\in C^{2,\mu}(\partial\Omega)\) satisfy \(h>0\) and \(\int_{\Omega} f_0(x,v)\,{\rm d}x\,{\rm d}v=\int_{\partial\Omega} h(x)\,{\rm d}l\). For the relativistic Vlasov-Poisson system
\[
\partial_t f+\hat v\cdot\nabla_x f+\nabla_x\varphi\cdot\nabla_v f=0,\qquad \Delta\varphi=\rho,\qquad \hat v=\frac{v}{\sqrt{1+|v|^2}},\quad \rho(t,x)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^2} f(t,x,v)\,{\rm d}v,
\]
with initial condition \(f(0,x,v)=f_0(x,v)\), specular reflection boundary condition \(f(t,x,v)=f(t,x,v^*)\) on \(\partial\Omega\times\mathbb{R}^2\times(0,\infty)\), where \(v^*=v-2(n_x\cdot v)n_x\), and Neumann boundary condition \(\partial\varphi/\partial n_x=h(x)\) on \(\partial\Omega\times(0,\infty)\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about existence and uniqueness of solutions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There exists a unique pair \((f,\varphi)\) with \(f\in C^{1;1,\lambda}_{t;(x,v)}((0,\infty)\times \bar\Omega\times \mathbb{R}^2)\) and \(\varphi\in C^{1;3,\lambda}_{t;x}((0,\infty)\times \bar\Omega)\) for some \(0<\lambda<\mu\), solving the above relativistic Vlasov-Poisson system, satisfying the stated initial, specular-reflection, and Neumann boundary conditions, and having compact support in \(x\) and \(v\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists a unique pair \((f,\varphi)\) with \(f\in C^{1;1,\lambda}_{t;(x,v)}((0,\infty)\times \bar\Omega\times \mathbb{R}^2)\) and \(\varphi\in C^{1;3,\lambda}_{t;x}((0,\infty)\times \bar\Omega)\) for some \(0<\lambda<\mu\), solving the above relativistic Vlasov-Poisson system, satisfying the stated initial, specular-reflection, and Neumann boundary conditions, and having compact support in \(x\) and \(v\); moreover one may take \(\lambda=\mu\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists at least one pair \((f,\varphi)\) with \(f\in C^{1;1,\lambda}_{t;(x,v)}((0,\infty)\times \bar\Omega\times \mathbb{R}^2)\) and \(\varphi\in C^{1;3,\lambda}_{t;x}((0,\infty)\times \bar\Omega)\) for some \(0<\lambda<\mu\), solving the above relativistic Vlasov-Poisson system, satisfying the stated initial, specular-reflection, and Neumann boundary conditions, and having compact support in \(x\) and \(v\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a unique pair \((f,\varphi)\) with \(f\in C^{1;1,\lambda}_{t;(x,v)}((0,\infty)\times \bar\Omega\times \mathbb{R}^2)\) and \(\varphi\in C^{1;3,\lambda}_{t;x}((0,\infty)\times \bar\Omega)\) for some \(0<\lambda<\mu\), solving the above relativistic Vlasov-Poisson system, satisfying the stated initial, specular-reflection, and Neumann boundary conditions, and having compact support in \(x\) and \(v\), even without the flatness assumption \(f_0(x,v)=\mathrm{constant}\) whenever \(\operatorname{dist}((x,v),\Gamma)\le \delta_0\).

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every \(0<\lambda<\mu\), there exists a unique pair \((f,\varphi)\) with \(f\in C^{1;1,\lambda}_{t;(x,v)}((0,\infty)\times \bar\Omega\times \mathbb{R}^2)\) and \(\varphi\in C^{1;3,\lambda}_{t;x}((0,
fty)\times \bar\Omega)\), solving the above relativistic Vlasov-Poisson system, satisfying the stated initial, specular-reflection, and Neumann boundary conditions, and having compact support in \(x\) and \(v\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: There exists a unique pair \((f,\varphi)\) with \(f\in C^{1;1,\lambda}_{t;(x,v)}((0,\infty)\times \bar\Omega\times \mathbb{R}^2)\) and \(\varphi\in C^{1;3,\lambda}_{t;x}((0,\infty)\times \bar\Omega)\) for some \(0<\lambda<\mu\), solving the above relativistic Vlasov-Poisson system, satisfying the stated initial, specular-reflection, and Neumann boundary conditions, and having compact support in \(x\) and \(v\).

question: Let $\mu$ be a translation-invariant, ergodic, finite-energy edge percolation measure on $\mathbb{Z}^2$. Let $\mu^*$ denote the induced edge percolation measure on the planar dual lattice (identified with $\mathbb{Z}^2$), and let $\mu_{\hp}$ and $\mu^*_{\hp}$ denote the marginals of $\mu$ and $\mu^*$, respectively, on the upper half-plane $\mathbb{Z}\times \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$. Which statement gives the complete classification of the possible infinite-cluster behavior of the pair $(\mu_{\hp},\mu^*_{\hp})$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The possible behaviors are exactly the four cases obtained by allowing each of $\mu_{\hp}$ and $\mu^*_{\hp}$ to have either no infinite cluster or a unique infinite cluster; in particular, it is possible that both $\mu_{\hp}$ and $\mu^*_{\hp}$ simultaneously have unique infinite clusters.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Almost surely, at most one of $\mu_{\hp}$ and $\mu^*_{\hp}$ has an infinite cluster; equivalently, simultaneous primal and dual percolation in the upper half-plane cannot occur.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Almost surely, exactly one of the following two alternatives occurs: $\mu_{\hp}$ has a unique infinite cluster while $\mu^*_{\hp}$ has no infinite cluster; or $\mu^*_{\hp}$ has a unique infinite cluster while $\mu_{\hp}$ has no infinite cluster.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The three alternatives listed in option A are the only sample-wise possibilities, but under a fixed translation-invariant ergodic finite-energy measure $\mu$, more than one of them may still occur on events of positive probability; the theorem does not force a single alternative almost surely.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

sketch: To prove the main theorem, the approach “combines stable weighted slicing \cite{BHJ23} with a spectral Llarull-type argument compatible with the stability inequality.” One “construct[s] a stable weighted slicing of order $m$, i.e., a nested family of hypersurfaces $\Sigma_m \subset \cdots \subset \Sigma_1 \subset M$ with positive weights that record the torus directions.” On the bottom slice, one “use[s] the stability inequality together with the Weitzenb\"ock-Lichnerowicz identity for spinors.” “Equality forces $\Sigma_m$ to be a round $S^{n-m}$; we then propagate rigidity upward by foliating with weighted minimizers and applying a slice-by-slice splitting argument.”
question:
Let $3\le n\le 7$ and $1\le m\le n-2$. Suppose $M^n$ is a closed, orientable, connected spin manifold with Riemannian metric $g$, and let $\psi\in C^\infty(M)$ satisfy
\[
-\Delta_M\psi-\frac12|D_M\psi|^2+\frac12\bigl(R_M-(n-m)(n-m-1)\bigr)\ge 0.
\]
Assume there is a smooth map
\[
\Phi:(M,g)\to \bigl(S^{n-m}\times \mathbb T^m,\ g_{S^{n-m}}+g_{\mathbb T^m}\bigr)
\]
of nonzero degree, where $\mathbb T^m=\mathbb R^m/\mathbb Z^m$, $g_{S^{n-m}}$ is the standard round metric on $S^{n-m}$, and $g_{\mathbb T^m}$ is the flat metric on $\mathbb T^m$, such that the composition with the projection onto the sphere factor,
\[
\operatorname{pr}_{S^{n-m}}\circ \Phi:(M,g)\to (S^{n-m},g_{S^{n-m}}),
\]
is area non-increasing when $n-m\ge 3$ and is $1$-Lipschitz when $n-m=2$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about $(M^n,g)$ for every such choice of $(M,g)$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: $(M^n,g)$ is diffeomorphic to S^{n-m}\times \mathbb T^m$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The universal cover of $(M^n,g)$ is diffeomorphic to $S^{n-m}\times \mathbb R^m$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: $(M^n,g)$ is isometric to $\bigl(S^{n-m}\times \mathbb T^m,\ g_{S^{n-m}}+g_{\mathbb T^m}\bigr)$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: $(M^n,g)$ is isometrically covered by $\bigl(S^{n-m}\times \mathbb R^m,\ c\,g_{S^{n-m}}+g_{\mathbb R^m}\bigr)$ for some constant $c>0$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

sketch:
Part (i) is obtained by “computing the Jacobian of the identity map from $(M_1 \times M_2,g)$ to $(M_1 \times M_2,g_+^t)$,” giving \eqref{eq:anest1}.

For (ii), “at a regular point of $\Sigma$ the $g$-unit exterior normal vector $\nu$ can be decomposed as $\nu=a\nu_1+b\nu_2$ so that $a^2+b^2=1$ and $\nu_1$, $\nu_2$ unit vectors tangent to $M_1$ and $M_2$, respectively.” The Jacobian of the identity map restricted to $\Sigma$ “equals $t^{m_2-1}(t^2a^2+b^2)^{1/2}$,” which for $t\le 1$ yields
\begin{equation}\label{eq:anest2}
  \hh_{g_+^t}^{m_1+m_2-1}(\Sg) 
  \leq t^{m_2-1}\,\hh_g^{m_1+m_2-1}(\Sg).
\end{equation}

For (iii), “$\nu$ is tangent to $M_2$ precisely when $a=0$ almost everywhere,” which characterizes when equality holds in the equation above.
question:
Let \((M_i,g_i)\) be Riemannian manifolds of dimensions \(m_i\) for \(i=1,2\), let \(g=g_1\times g_2\), and write \(g_+^t:=g_1\times t^2g_2\) for the right anisotropic \(t\)-homothety on \(M_1\times M_2\). Let \(\Sigma\subset M_1\times M_2\) be \((m_1+m_2-1)\)-rectifiable, and let \(\mathcal H_h^{\alpha}\) denote the \(\alpha\)-dimensional Hausdorff measure with respect to the metric \(h\). Suppose that for \(t\le 1\) one has
\[
\mathcal H_{g_+^t}^{m_1+m_2-1}(\Sigma)\le t^{m_2-1}\,\mathcal H_g^{m_1+m_2-1}(\Sigma).
\]
Which of the following statements is equivalent to equality in this estimate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: At \(\mathcal H_g^{m_1+m_2-1}\)-a.e. regular point \((x,y)\in\Sigma\), the approximate tangent hyperplane \(T_{(x,y)}\Sigma\) contains the whole subspace \(T_yM_2\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: At \(\mathcal H_g^{m_1+m_2-1}\)-a.e. regular point of \(\Sigma\), the \(M_1\)-component of the \(g\)-unit normal vanishes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: At \(\mathcal H_g^{m_1+m_2-1}\)-a.e. regular point of \(\Sigma\), the \(g\)-unit normal makes a constant angle with the \(M_2\)-factor.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
If one writes the \(g\)-unit normal as \(
u=a
u_1+b
u_2\) with \(a^2+b^2=1\), then equality is equivalent to the identity \(t^2a^2+b^2=1\) holding at every regular point of a dense subset of \(\Sigma\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question:
Fix a depth parameter \(\delta>0\), and let \(T^\delta\) be the singular integral operator
\[
(T^\delta f)(x)=\frac{1}{2\delta}\,\mathrm{P.V.}\!\int_{\mathbb R}\coth\!\left(\frac{\pi (y-x)}{2\delta}\right)f(y)\,dy.
\]
Let \(n\in\mathbb N\) with \(n\ge 1\), let \(\mathbf c=(c_1,\dots,c_n)\) satisfy \(0<c_1<\cdots<c_n\), let \(\mathbf x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in\mathbb R^n\), and let \(U^{(n)}(\cdot,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\) be the corresponding \(n\)-soliton solution of
\[
u_t+\frac{1}{\delta}u_x+2uu_x+T^\delta u_{xx}=0
\quad\text{on }\mathbb R\times\mathbb R.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the uniform orbital stability in \(H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) of perturbations of \(U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text:
For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta_0>0\) such that for every initial datum \(u_0\in H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) with \(\|u_0-U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\delta_0\), if \(u(t)\) is the corresponding solution, then for every \(t\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\inf_{\tau\in\mathbb R,\ \mathbf y\in\mathbb R^n}\|u(t)-U^{(n)}(\tau,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf y)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\epsilon.
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta_0>0\) such that for every initial datum \(u_0\in H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) with \(\|u_0-U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\delta_0\), if \(u(t)\) is the corresponding solution, then there exists a single vector \(\mathbf y\in\mathbb R^n\), independent of \(t\), such that for every \(t\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\inf_{\tau\in\mathbb R}\|u(t)-U^{(n)}(\tau,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf y)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\epsilon.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta_0>0\) such that for every initial datum \(u_0\in H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) with \(\|u_0-U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\delta_0\), if \(u(t)\) is the corresponding solution, then for every \(t\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\inf_{\mathbf y\in\mathbb R^n}\|u(t)-U^{(n)}(t,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf y)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\epsilon.
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta_0>0\), depending only on \(\epsilon\), \(n\), and the fixed depth parameter \(\delta\), such that for every choice of wave speeds \(0<c_1<\cdots<c_n\), every \(\mathbf x\in\mathbb R^n\), and every initial datum \(u_0\in H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) with \(\|u_0-U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\delta_0\), if \(u(t)\) is the corresponding solution, then for every \(t\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\inf_{\tau\in\mathbb R,\ \mathbf y\in\mathbb R^n}\|u(t)-U^{(n)}(\tau,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf y)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\epsilon.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta_0>0\) such that for every initial datum \(u_0\in H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) with \(\|u_0-U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\delta_0\), if \(u(t)\) is the corresponding solution, then for every \(t\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\inf_{\tau\in\mathbb R,\ a\in\mathbb R}\|u(t)-U^{(n)}(\tau,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x+a(1,\dots,1))\|_{H^{n/2}}<\epsilon.
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta_0>0\) such that for every initial datum \(u_0\in H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) with \(\|u_0-U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\delta_0\), if \(u(t)\) is the corresponding solution, then for every \(t\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\inf_{\tau\in\mathbb R,\ \mathbf y\in\mathbb R^n}\|u(t)-U^{(n)}(\tau,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf y)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\epsilon.
\]

theorem:
\label{t1}
    For any $k\in \mathbb{N}$ and $2$-coloring of $\mathbb{N},$ there exist $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $\{x,y,xy, x+iy: i\leq k\}$ is monochromatic.,
sketch:
The authors state that “the basic strategy used in the proof of the main theorem … is essentially as follows:”
\begin{enumerate}
\item “First, show that the result holds when the color of the step size $y$ is ignored.” Then one finds monochromatic sets $\{x,xy,x+iy: i\leq k\}$, “which can be done either through Moreira's work … or the first author's …”.
\item “Define an appropriate structure vs randomness dichotomy, and argue that a $2$-coloring must either be structured or random.”
\item “Argue that in either case the extra information we obtain can be used to upgrade the proof from step (1) to control the color of the $y$ term as well.”
\end{enumerate}
They further note that they “give two proofs of the main theorem based on the two ways of handling step (1) and using two different structure vs randomness dichotomies.”
question: Let $\mathbb{N}$ denote the positive integers, let $k\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $c:\mathbb{N}\to\{\text{red},\text{blue}\}$ be any 2-coloring of $\mathbb{N}$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ whose values under $c$ make the relevant multiplicative and additive configurations monochromatic?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There exist $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x$, $y$, $xy$, and every number of the form $x+iy$ with $1\le i\le k$ all have the same color under $c$; equivalently, the set $\{x,y,xy,x+iy: i\le k\}$ is monochromatic.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The same conclusion in fact holds for every finite coloring of $\mathbb{N}$, not just for 2-colorings: for any finite coloring of $\mathbb{N}$ there exist $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x$, $y$, $xy$, and every number of the form $x+iy$ with $1\le i\le k$ all have the same color.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exist $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x$, $y$, and $xy$ all have the same color under $c$; equivalently, the set $\{x,y,xy\}$ is monochromatic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a single $y\in\mathbb{N}$, depending only on $k$, such that for every 2-coloring $c:\mathbb{N}\to\{\mathrm{red},\mathrm{blue}\}$ there exists $x\in\mathbb{N}$ with $x$, $y$, $xy$, and every number of the form $x+iy$ with $1\le i\le k$ all having the same color under $c$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exist $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x$, $y$, $xy$, and every number of the form $x+iy$ with $1\le i\le k+1$ all have the same color under $c$; equivalently, the set $\{x,y,xy,x+iy: i\le k+1\}$ is monochromatic.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: There exist $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x$, $y$, $xy$, and every number of the form $x+iy$ with $1\le i\le k$ all have the same color under $c$; equivalently, the set $\{x,y,xy,x+iy: i\le k\}$ is monochromatic.

theorem:
\label{Thm for E}
    If $E$ is uniformly weakly RC-positive over a compact K\"ahler manifold $X$, then $S^kE\otimes \det E$ is uniformly RC-positive for any $k\geq 0$, and $S^kE$ is uniformly RC-positive for $k$ large.
sketch:
To prove the main theorem, the text says that “instead of the fibration $p:P(E^*)\to X$, we will work on a more general fibration and prove a general theorem which contains the main theorem as a special case.” Concretely, one considers a proper holomorphic submersion $p:\mathcal{X}^{n+m}\to Y^m$ with $\mathcal{X}$ K\"ahler and $Y$ compact, a Hermitian line bundle $(L,h)$ on $\mathcal{X}$, and the direct-image bundle $V$ with fibers
\[
V_t=H^0(\mathcal{X}_t,\, L|_{\mathcal{X}_t}\otimes K_{\mathcal{X}_t}),
\]
endowed with the “naturally defined Hermitian metric”
\[
H(u,u):=\int_{\mathcal{X}_t} h(u,u).\tag{\ref{metric}}
\]
We first prove the following theorem.
\begin{theorem}\label{Thm 1}
    If the curvature $\Theta$ of $h$ is positive on every fiber, and for any point $t\in Y$, there exists a nonzero tangent vector $v\in T_tY$, such that $\Theta(\Tilde{v},\bar{\Tilde{v}})|_{(t,z)}>0$ for any lift $\Tilde{v}$ of $v$ to $T_{(t,z)}\mathcal{X}$, then the Hermitian bundle $(V,H)$ is uniformly RC-positive.
\end{theorem}
Under these hypotheses, one concludes that “the Hermitian bundle $(V,H)$ is uniformly RC-positive.”

Then the main theorem is obtained by applying the preceding theorem to $p:P(E^*)\to X$ (with $X$ K\"ahler so $P(E^*)$ is K\"ahler). In this specialization, one uses that
\[
K_{P(E^*)/X}\simeq O_{P(E^*)}(-r)\otimes p^*\det E.
\]
Choosing $L=O_{P(E^*)}(r+k)$ gives that the resulting direct image bundle $V$ is “$S^kE\otimes\det E$.” Alternatively, choosing $L=O_{P(E^*)}(k)\otimes K^{-1}_{P(E^*)/X}$ gives $V=S^kE$, and “the effect of [a metric on $K^{-1}_{P(E^*)/X}$] can be absorbed by taking $k$ large.”

Finally, the proof of the preceding theorem is described as “an adaptation of \cite[Section 3]{wu2025mean}” and attributed to Berndtsson (with references).
question: Let \(E\) be a holomorphic vector bundle of rank \(r\) over a compact K\"ahler manifold \(X\). Write \(S^kE\) for the \(k\)-th symmetric power of \(E\) and \(\det E\) for its determinant line bundle. Assume that \(E\) is uniformly weakly RC-positive, meaning the uniform version of weak RC-positivity: for some Hermitian metric on \(E\), the induced metric on the tautological line bundle \(O_{P(E^*)}(1)\) over the projectivized bundle \(P(E^*)\) has curvature positive on every fiber and at least \(r\) positive eigenvalues at every point. Here, a holomorphic vector bundle \(F\) is called uniformly RC-positive if for every point \(x\in X\) there exists a nonzero tangent vector \(u\in T_xX\) such that the Chern curvature satisfies \(R^F(u,\bar u,v,\bar v)>0\) for every nonzero fiber vector \(v\in F_x\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) and \(S^kE\) under these assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^kE\) itself is uniformly RC-positive for all sufficiently large integers \(k\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive for all sufficiently large integers \(k\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes (\det E)^{-1}\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^kE\) itself is uniformly RC-positive for all sufficiently large integers \(k\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^{k+r}E\) is uniformly RC-positive for every integer \(k\ge 0\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^kE\) itself is uniformly RC-positive for all sufficiently large integers \(k\).

theorem:
\label{thm:main1}
For each $n \ge 10$, the Torelli group of $X_n = 2\mathbb{CP}^2 \# n \overline{\mathbb{CP}^2}$ admits a surjective homomorphism $\Phi : T(X_n) \to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$.
sketch:
To prove the main theorem, the paper associates to each diffeomorphism $f\in M(X)$ its mapping cylinder $E(f)$ (a $1$-parameter family over $S^1$), and—when $f$ preserves a spin$^c$ structure $\mathfrak{s}$ with zero expected families Seiberg--Witten dimension—defines a numerical invariant by “counting the number of solutions of the Seiberg--Witten equations for the family $E(f)$.” Since $b_+=2$ this count depends on a chamber, but the authors identify circumstances giving a distinguished chamber:

(1) **Constant chamber:** if $f\in T(X)$, then $f$ acts trivially on $H^2(X;\mathbb R)$ so the local system over $S^1$ has trivial monodromy, yielding a (homotopically unique) trivialisation of $\mathcal H^+\to S^1$; the constant chamber corresponds to a constant section.

(2) **Zero chamber:** if $c(\mathfrak{s})^2\ge 0$ and $c(\mathfrak{s})$ is non-torsion, there is a chamber given by taking the self-dual $2$-form perturbation to be zero.

They show these chambers “coincide when they are both defined,” producing invariants $SW^c_{X,\mathfrak{s}}(f)$ and $SW^0_{X,\mathfrak{s}}(f)$ depending only on $(X,\mathfrak{s})$ and the isotopy class of $f$. In particular, the constant-chamber invariants give homomorphisms $SW^c_{X,\mathfrak{s}}:T(X)\to\mathbb Z$. By compactness, for fixed $f\in T(X)$ only finitely many $\mathfrak{s}$ contribute nontrivially, so one gets a combined homomorphism
\[
\Phi:T(X)\to\bigoplus_{\mathfrak{s}}\mathbb Z,
\]
(summing over zero-dimensional families moduli spaces). The proof of the main theorem then “follows by showing that the image of $\Phi$ has infinite rank,” by constructing an infinite sequence of spin$^c$-structures $\{\mathfrak{s}_n\}$ and diffeomorphisms $\{t_n\}$ with $SW^c_{X,\mathfrak{s}_n}(t_n)\ne 0$. The $t_n$ are built “in a similar fashion to \cite{rub1}, \cite{bk1},” using diffeomorphisms $E(1)_{p,q}\#(S^2\times S^2)\cong E(1)\#(S^2\times S^2)$.,
question: For each integer $n \ge 10$, let $X_n=2\mathbb{CP}^2\# n\overline{\mathbb{CP}^2}$, where $\#$ denotes connected sum and $\overline{\mathbb{CP}^2}$ is $\mathbb{CP}^2$ with the opposite orientation. Let $T(X_n)$ denote the Torelli group of $X_n$, i.e. the subgroup of the mapping class group (smooth isotopy classes of diffeomorphisms of $X_n$) consisting of classes represented by diffeomorphisms continuously isotopic to the identity. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about homomorphisms from $T(X_n)$ to $\mathbb{Z}^\infty$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: For every $n\ge 10$, there exists a surjective homomorphism $\Phi:T(X_n)\to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$, where $\mathbb{Z}^\infty$ is the direct sum of countably infinitely many copies of $\mathbb{Z}$.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every $n\ge 10$, there exists a homomorphism $\Phi:T(X_n)\to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$ whose image has infinite rank, but which need not be surjective onto $\mathbb{Z}^\infty$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every $n\ge 10$, there exists a homomorphism $\Phi:T(X_n)\to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$ with infinite image.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a single surjective homomorphism $\Phi:T(X_n)\to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$ for all sufficiently large $n$, but not necessarily for every $n\ge 10$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every $n\ge 10$, every diffeomorphism of $X_n$ that is continuously isotopic to the identity determines, via the families Seiberg--Witten construction, a surjective homomorphism $T(X_n)\to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For every $n\ge 10$, there exists a surjective homomorphism $\Phi:T(X_n)\to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$, where $\mathbb{Z}^\infty$ is the direct sum of countably infinitely many copies of $\mathbb{Z}$.

question: Let \(\kappa \ge 3\) be a cardinal. Here a real tree is a geodesic metric space in which any two points are connected by a unique arc, and the valence of a point is the number of connected components of the complement of that point. The universal real tree of valence \(\kappa\) is the unique complete real tree in which every point has valence \(\kappa\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved for every such \(\kappa\) about a transfinite family of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\), indexed by the countable ordinals \(1 \le \alpha < \omega_1\), together with isometric embeddings between earlier and later members of the family?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There exists a family \(\{T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}:1\le \alpha<\omega_1\}\) of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\) and isometric embeddings \(\psi_{\alpha,\beta}:T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\hookrightarrow T_{\kappa}^{[\beta]}\) for all \(\alpha<\beta<\omega_1\) such that distinct countable ordinals give non-isometric trees, the embeddings are coherent in the sense that \(\psi_{\alpha,\gamma}=\psi_{\beta,\gamma}\circ\psi_{\alpha,\beta}\) whenever \(\alpha<\beta<\gamma\), and the direct union of this system is isometric to the universal real tree of valence \(\kappa\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists a family \(\{T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}:1\le \alpha<\omega_1\}\) of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\) and isometric embeddings \(\psi_{\alpha,\beta}:T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\hookrightarrow T_{\kappa}^{[\beta]}\) for all \(\alpha<\beta<\omega_1\) such that distinct countable ordinals give non-isometric trees, the embeddings are coherent in the sense that \(\psi_{\alpha,\gamma}=\psi_{\beta,\gamma}\circ\psi_{\alpha,\beta}\) whenever \(\alpha<\beta<\gamma\), and for every countable limit ordinal \(\lambda<\omega_1\) the direct union \(\bigcup_{\alpha<\lambda}T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\) is isometric to \(T_{\kappa}^{[\lambda]}\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a family \(\{T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}:1\le \alpha<\omega_1\}\) of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\) and isometric embeddings \(\psi_{\alpha,\beta}:T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\hookrightarrow T_{\kappa}^{[\beta]}\) for all \(\alpha<\beta<\omega_1\) such that the embeddings are coherent in the sense that \(\psi_{\alpha,\gamma}=\psi_{\beta,\gamma}\circ\psi_{\alpha,\beta}\) whenever \(\alpha<\beta<\gamma\), and the direct union of this system is isometric to the universal real tree of valence \(\kappa\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a family \(\{T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}:1\le \alpha<\omega_1\}\) of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\) and isometric embeddings \(\psi_{\alpha,\beta}:T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\hookrightarrow T_{\kappa}^{[\beta]}\) for all \(\alpha<\beta<\omega_1\) such that distinct countable ordinals give non-isometric trees, the embeddings are coherent in the sense that \(\psi_{\alpha,\gamma}=\psi_{\beta,\gamma}\circ\psi_{\alpha,\beta}\) whenever \(\alpha<\beta<\gamma\), and the direct union of this system is incomplete while its metric completion is isometric to the universal real tree of valence \(\kappa\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists a family \(\{T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}:1\le \alpha<\omega_1\}\) of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\) and isometric embeddings \(\psi_{\alpha,\beta}:T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\hookrightarrow T_{\kappa}^{[\beta]}\) for all \(\alpha<\beta<\omega_1\) such that distinct countable ordinals give non-isometric trees, the embeddings are coherent in the sense that \(\psi_{\alpha,\gamma}=\psi_{\beta,gamma}\circ\psi_{\alpha,\beta}\) whenever \(\alpha<\beta<\gamma\), and the direct union of the subsystem indexed only by successor ordinals is already isometric to the universal real tree of valence \(\kappa\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: There exists a family \(\{T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}:1\le \alpha<\omega_1\}\) of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\) and isometric embeddings \(\psi_{\alpha,\beta}:T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\hookrightarrow T_{\kappa}^{[\beta]}\) for all \(\alpha<\beta<\omega_1\) such that distinct countable ordinals give non-isometric trees, the embeddings are coherent in the sense that \(\psi_{\alpha,\gamma}=\psi_{\beta,\gamma}\circ\psi_{\alpha,\beta}\) whenever \(\alpha<\beta<\gamma\), and the direct union of this system is isometric to the universal real tree of valence \(\kappa\).

question:
Let \((\mathcal M_1,g_1)\) and \((\mathcal M_2,g_2)\) be two smooth connected \(n\)-dimensional Riemannian manifolds with boundary. Let \(\omega_1\subset \mathcal M_1\) and \(\omega_2\subset \mathcal M_2\) be open connected subsets. For each \(j=1,2\), let \(L^2\Omega^1(\omega_j)\) denote the space of square-integrable 1-forms on \(\omega_j)\), and define the local source-to-final value map
\[
\mathcal N_j:\mathbb B_\delta\to L^2\Omega^1(\omega_j),\qquad \mathcal N_j(\mathbf f)=\mathbf u_{\mathbf f}(T,\cdot)|_{\omega_j},
\]
where \(\mathbb B_\delta\) is a small ball of admissible source 1-forms, and \(\mathbf u_{\mathbf f}\) is the corresponding solution of the geometric Navier--Stokes system on \([0,T]\times \mathcal M_j\) with zero initial and boundary data, evaluated at the final time \(T\). Assume there exists a diffeomorphism \(\Phi:\omega_1\to\omega_2\) such that, with \(\Phi^*\) denoting pullback on the relevant source and final-value 1-forms,
\[
\mathcal N_1\,\Phi^*=\Phi^*\,\mathcal N_2.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \((\mathcal M_1,g_1)\) and \((\mathcal M_2,g_2)\) under these assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The intertwining identity implies equality, via \(\Phi\), of the localized spectral projections for the stationary Stokes operator on \(\omega_1\) and \(\omega_2\); however, this determines at most the domains of influence of the observation sets up to time \(T\), not the full manifolds up to isometry.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The intertwining identity already forces the first-order linearizations of \(\mathcal N_1\) and \(\mathcal N_2\) at \(0\) to agree under \(\Phi\); equivalently, the local source-to-final maps for the corresponding linearized Stokes systems coincide on the observation regions, which is weaker than global isometry.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a sufficiently small \(\delta>0\), depending only on \(T\), such that intertwining on \(\mathbb B_\delta\) identifies the linearized Stokes dynamics on \(\omega_1\) and \(\omega_2\); to deduce that \((\mathcal M_1,g_1)\) and \((\mathcal M_2,g_2)\) are globally isometric one would need the additional hypothesis that \(T\) exceed the relevant geometric controllability time.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The diffeomorphism \(\Phi\) extends to a global isometry \(\widetilde\Phi:\mathcal M_1\to\mathcal M_2\), and after identifying the manifolds by \(\widetilde\Phi\) the full nonlinear source-to-final value maps agree for all admissible \(L^2\)-source 1-forms, not merely for sources in the small ball \(\mathbb B_\delta\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
